thread.text: My 30+ observations on the greatest opportunities in AI agents right now: And some ideas that are keeping me up at night. 1. The new buyer on the internet is an AI agent. Imagine billions of new customers showing up with money to spend but they only shop via MCP. That's what's
@gregisenberg the agent buyer point is real. once checkout, search, and support all have machine consumers, half the product surface turns into protocol design.
@gregisenberg Now researching… full reports available soon https://t.co/UXZVHQDCGB
@gregisenberg Tons of great ideas! Love!
@gregisenberg great takes here Greg, which 2 or 3 would you say you’re thinking about the most rn
@gregisenberg @timkweb3 should’ve been an article , great ideas tho
@gregisenberg I'm building the agent marketplace. Join waitlist here: https://t.co/4gvBVpvJZj https://t.co/S2BADUOoeD
@gregisenberg We're building #12 - managed AI agents. We call it your Nucleus https://t.co/KEBdD8BHLx
@gregisenberg Thought of this too... we need to put more effort into our soft skills https://t.co/D9LUNkCYsr

@gregisenberg Point 3!! Distribution gets you the audience. Memory turns that audience into sth no competitor can replicate. Interaction can't be bought back later. We've been building persistent agent memory for this exact reason. The context accumulates across every run, not just the session
@gregisenberg MCP as the new shopping substrate is the lens I needed. Wiring my preference profile into a small commerce MCP this weekend to see what an agent actually picks.
@gregisenberg The interesting takes are the operational ones. The market sizing ones are the usual hype.
@gregisenberg Point #5 is definitely being built at an enterprise level by my friends at @greentic_ai

@gregisenberg One thing I find fascinating about the current AI wave is that everyone is obsessed with intelligence itself, while the real bottleneck is quietly shifting somewhere else. Coordination. Memory. Trust. Taste. Distribution. Judgment. Context. We’re rapidly approaching a world

@gregisenberg Love this! Add “bad data in, bad data out”, esp for enterprise. if Biz doesn’t have a clear understanding of existing bug prioritization b4 buying x AI vendor/agent, they will be doomed to pay more. AI will be blamed when really it is lack of current state accountability

@gregisenberg Strong thread, Greg. Spot on with distribution + memory as the real moat. We're seeing the same in production: agents that ship reliable vertical SaaS (especially HealthTech) win when they own long-term patient/context memory + clean orchestration layers — not just raw model

@gregisenberg The CPU analogy is right. The under-discussed implication: nobody knows how to market the orchestration layer yet. You can't demo a workflow the way you demo a feature. The distribution problem for agent businesses is genuinely unsolved.
@gregisenberg the 'agent as buyer' framing sounds slick until you remember every agent i've built mostly shops by hallucinating urls
@gregisenberg This is gold 🔥 The biggest unlock isn’t just agents, it's who owns the memory layer + orchestration. Everything else is commoditized.

@gregisenberg Point 1 hit me. I run an Etsy shop with 136 listings and zero of them are set up for an AI buyer scanning structured data. Every product page is written for humans browsing on their phone. Probably need to build a second feed just for agents.
@gregisenberg What about an agent for trading? Citadel, Jane Street, Ren Tech level agents. Have you come across any ideas in this vertical?
@gregisenberg Item 2 is the easy one. A franchise is already a process documented to within an inch of its life. The agent layer is just whatever can read the BPMN model and run it.

@gregisenberg #29 feels especially important. If agents start recommending other agents, products, tools, services, and offers, the next layer is not just payments. It is attribution.Agentic commerce needs infrastructure for: native offers referral tracking conversion attribution automated
@gregisenberg Can't sleep either after reading this. The orchestration layer point alone is worth a thread of its own.
@gregisenberg The agent as buyer idea is sneaky huge. Websites were built for humans clicking buttons. Agents want APIs, trust, price, permissions and receipts. Different internet.
@gregisenberg everyone obsessing over model benchmarks while the real alpha is orchestration feels exactly like early cloud where infra abstraction mattered more than raw hardware
@gregisenberg the shift gets interesting when the phone stops feeling like a gadget and starts feeling like a permission layer for software acting on your behalf. that's when the UX rules change.
@gregisenberg If agents become buyers, SEO turns into something closer to being legible to software. The question becomes, can an agent understand, trust, compare, and transact with your business?

@gregisenberg Number 23 is the one that deserves its own thread. The person who understands what the agents are doing has become the most critical and most fragile role in the company simultaneously. No title, no team, no documentation, no backup. And the scary part is that most leadership

@gregisenberg 33. Humans become dangerous with ai. Instead of only obsessing over the new 24-7 agent that promises to run your business. Take that domain knowledge you have, build a documentation system that is organised and accessible and easily updated. Connect all your tools and use ai to

@gregisenberg If #1 is right, the entire UGC ad industry is built for the wrong audience. Human-attention hooks, emotional cuts, creator faces — none of it matters to an agent shopping via MCP. The next CMO hire is a data engineer. Are you betting on humans or agents for the next 5 yrs?

@gregisenberg the funny part is how many of these “agents replace work” ideas create a new job immediately. someone has to watch the agent. fix the agent. explain what the agent did. notice when it quietly went weird. we keep calling that automation, but a lot of it is just management with

My AI handles most of it, but every edge case funnels to the one human: me. So I’m building self-healing. Polsia detects its own https://t.co/5F4DM48AVn

My AI handles most of it, but every edge case funnels to the one human: me. So I’m building self-healing. Polsia detects its own https://t.co/5F4DM48AVn
@Bencera self-healing is where this gets interesting. everyone demos the happy path. the real moat is catching the weird edge case before the founder becomes the fallback router again.
@Bencera sure https://t.co/y08a8DYbP2
@Bencera Hey Ben, would love to know how you use Claude and agentic harnesses to power polsia. It would be awesome to see the skills, Md file, etc
@Bencera incredible! would you consider bringing someone on board or would you keep building as far as you can with AI?

@Bencera the funnel-everything-to-me thing is real. in my experience agents work best when they close the boring 80 percent and only bump weird edges up. been messing with the hubspot navigator from @ImplicitCloudAI, fits this.
@Bencera This is just EXTRAORDINARY Ben. Honest congrats!! Are you also tracking any outcomes achieved by the companies built on Polsia?

@Bencera self-healing is where agent systems get real. the unlock is not removing the human. it is making every exception land with context, logs, and a proposed fix instead of another blank inbox item.
@Bencera Pretty wild. Self-healing SW is a good idea, but I do wonder why not expand the human staff?
@Bencera Does polsia use polsia itself?
@Bencera Hard to believe with that website https://t.co/8gnU97Ndal
@Bencera seems like now you full busy in your work bro + respect
@Bencera self-healing is the only way that scales solo curious how you draw the line on whats automation vs human touch
@Bencera I don't understand, you made $9m ?
@Bencera These comments are a bunch of word salad. Did Polsia create the comments? LOL
@Bencera Absolutely epic run @Bencera been tracking this since Feb @ https://t.co/GV4PrH9Iaz
@Bencera LangGraph checkpointing fits this pattern cleanly. Lets the agent resume from the last stable state without full restarts.
@Bencera the context switching between support and building is brutal
@Bencera self-healing agents feel like the logical next step tbh the bottleneck being a one-man edge case handler is the real limit
@Bencera name dropping the run rate everywhere until it becomes self healing thats real power
@Bencera one human handling edge cases while the rest is ai is the real flex here the self-healing loop is where it gets scary good
@Bencera at a certain scale the product stops being the bottleneck and the founder becomes the infrastructure
@Bencera Sign me up. How does that work?
@Bencera Hats off to you
@Bencera My 2 Polsia projects are at ZERO or should I say -$100 each, after 2months. This is overrated.
@Bencera Take care of yourself m8!

target.label: Delba
target.text: You can extend every step of Claude Code's agentic loop. I've been thinking a lot about what that means for the last one. What are you doing to help Claude verify its own work? Genuinely want to hear what workflows people have. https://t.co/QBOqMzKUUh

thread.text: You can extend every step of Claude Code's agentic loop. I've been thinking a lot about what that means for the last one. What are you doing to help Claude verify its own work? Genuinely want to hear what workflows people have. https://t.co/QBOqMzKUUh

Looking for things beyond deterministic checks like type checking, linting, and tests. As models get better at reasoning, they can usually infer those from your codebase. I mean the intuitive checks you do as a developer. The ones that aren’t usually in the code.

@delba_oliveira the hard part is giving it taste, not just tests. can it tell the difference between 'passed CI' and 'actually solved the user's problem'? building https://t.co/wAXM9FxxVe for that layer.
@delba_oliveira I have a "remove doubt" stop hook that checks the conversation for words like "likely" and forces the convo to continue until everything has proof.

@delba_oliveira Any claude code that touches web based work, will open a browser and confirm it works and runs various benchmarks for TTL to ensure it didnt make the app slower If its a backend change, ill have claude take what it did, create a new branch and try an alternative approach

@delba_oliveira I've been finding myself defining "personas" in a review skill that instructs Claude Code to run a sub agent per-persona to review from that specific lens. Idea being that it keeps it focused on that one angle and doesn't return only surface level findings on the first turn.

@delba_oliveira "How much do you sure it's the real root cause?" Been pretty useful, to re-arrange my thoughts and if answer is not 100%, get an overview of potential places agent could've missed, but me with more context and overall product knowledge can steer him into the correct solution
@delba_oliveira hooks, and https://t.co/XdHKFgVAhm

@delba_oliveira i have a kilocode workflow where i use 5.5 as the orchestrator, with lighter models for implementing the plan made by 5.5 for verifying the work. i have hooks which run different skills like some for my coding styles, some for its own verification, some for code quality and stuff

@delba_oliveira Two layers that work for me: separate reviewer agents between the builder and merge, criteria check first, then architectural review. And an append-only event ledger with git SHAs throughout. Verification stays anchored to artifacts, not narration.

@delba_oliveira maybe overkill but on top of code reviewer, i have a “visual tester” agent for my webapps so when i deploy a team, the visual tester will mimick human behavior and gather screenshots of the changes for validation. work is not done until changes are verified visually.
@delba_oliveira is this an illustration or an actual ui yal are building? asking for a friend who didn’t unsubscribe yesterday.

@delba_oliveira Set up another pre orchestration model same review model claude uses to review their own internal code, they have this out for a while but credits gets used to the max so don’t Recommend this until you have tokens to spare if not gpt 5.5 is the best at reviewing
@delba_oliveira I built a recursive workflow: https://t.co/6YHs44Zs4A
@delba_oliveira Have tried to wire this up - worked promisingly in Opus-4.5/4.6 context but totally degraded Opus-4.7 performance. Still trying to figure it out, open for suggestions.. 💭

@delba_oliveira chrome devtools mcp w autoConnect so it can use my actual browser with all my user-data so it can seamlessly navigate local dev as if it were me. then i tell it how to navigate the app in the browser and how to verify functionality and appearance.

@delba_oliveira two things that landed for us across 25 agents: 1) phase-fail gates with 3-layer assertions (agent-code, test-setup, env) before the verify step runs, so verify knows which layer to challenge. 2) explicit 'list assumptions made' step before commit, then a different agent

@delba_oliveira One workflow that helps: make verification a separate spec, not a vibe check. 1. agent implements 2. second pass extracts acceptance tests 3. run only changed-surface tests 4. review: "what did we not touch?" $50 Claude/Codex workflow audit if useful.

@delba_oliveira The big unlock is making verification a first class artifact, not a postscript. Have the agent emit the claim, the check it ran, and why that check is sufficient. Otherwise "self-verification" collapses into vibes fast.

@delba_oliveira I use a personal skill that creates some files by defining the role of the executor, the reviewer, the contract with definition of done + phase current status and the prompt dividing the development into phases, doing one phase at a time.

thread.text: @a16z yep. once the agent is the user, half the app turns into protocol design. the UI still matters for humans, but it stops being the moat.

@a16z Salesforce is admitting what vertical SaaS has feared: UI moats dissolve when agents negotiate directly with APIs. The real lock-in becomes schema completeness and permission granularity. Whoever owns the transaction graph. Not the dashboard. Wins.

@a16z This thesis is exactly the wedge we're working on. The agentic-era moat isn't UI, it's the closed loop: real session traces, per-instance trust profiles on your codebase, failure patterns the team can act on, and routing recommendations across vendors. @Worldline_AI captures https://t.co/NbR1opUoWK
@a16z @chipro hahaha remembering your meme https://t.co/BMMcIHz7UB
@a16z Is anything from SF that is headless actually shipped yet? When I asked their customer support the only headless API was an auth login flow.

@a16z I think its pretty clear this is the case. In todays day in age, we can essentially spin up new UIs and dashboards in a morning or afternoon. The data is where the real value lies and i'm not sure if I ever see that changing.

@a16z We are entering a phase of Agent-to-Agent (A2A) commerce, where the "product" is a combination of Proprietary Context and Guaranteed Execution. The "Headless" movement is an admission that the Human-Computer Interaction (HCI) layer is no longer the primary value driver.

@a16z this whole framework assumes agents will play nice with existing systems. they won't. salesforce going headless is an admission that their UI was never the product - the data was. but the moment agents can read and write directly into your CRM, the defensibility question isn't
@a16z Headless software changes where defensibility lives. Agentic finance changes where enforcement must live. Not in the UI. Not just in the API. In the asset itself.
@a16z ce exposing the data layer gives agent loops a direct backend hook instead of fighting legacy UIs.
@a16z Headless is not a product direction. It is a margin redistribution event. APIs replace UI teams. The question is whether the value delta accrues to model providers or the orchestration layer.
@a16z I wonder if the headless bet only works when the data underneath is clean and well-modeled. most CRMs ive seen arent.

@a16z Strong framing. In physical-product markets, the headless shift is even more interesting: the moat is not the QR code or the UI — it is the trust architecture behind the scan. As an official GS1 partner, we see this clearly at ArtQR: product identity, evidence continuity, https://t.co/vribUbN3Th

@a16z BPO operators already knew this. Stripping away the Salesforce UI exposes what held the floor together: the QA lead catching a bad call before it reaches the customer. The data layer was always incidental to that judgment.
@a16z headless product is the sharp part here. Once agents sit on top, the UI stops being the daily surface; the harder defensibility is permissioned workflow data plus write access.

@a16z There is a take that "headless" software will replace subscription seat-based business model. Because agents don't need a "seat" assigned to them just by using API create a new business model based on volume. Would you agree this is the direction SaaS s/w is going?
@a16z Which APIs does a human user use though? I think part of the benefit of the UI is that it guides usage. With headless, you introduce more complexity for the human user via more options.
@a16z Been saying it for awhile Agentic is the way to develop today, now. tools are the old way we developed that agents will now use! build agents fist and build tools (APIS) for those agents.
@a16z anyone can recreate the first 80% of your product now the edge cases are the only defensibility left
@a16z that's a bold move for sure. i wonder how this will change the way developers interact with the platform and what new apps might emerge from it

@a16z The assumption that distribution alone will compensate for the absence of proprietary products feels incomplete at this stage. In the long run, the most overlooked variable may still be the user.

@a16z @a16z The deeper shift is that once software goes headless, systems of record stop being passive repositories and become delegated execution infrastructure. At that point, defensibility is no longer just about proprietary data or workflow logic, but about whether authority,

@a16z We're seeing this from the buyer side. Our agents work with maybe a dozen tools and the ones that survived our cuts are the ones where the API surface covers everything we need end to end. Gaps in the API show up as gaps in what we can actually build with them.
@a16z UI should be for things people take pleasure in. Salesforce, Hubspot etc, are an equivalent of cubicle office space. Just get rid of it.
@a16z i wrote an article about this a few months ago, same thoughts, and yes, they're losing their moat, an action layer / decision layer is going to fix it, if they do it right
thread.quotedText: https://t.co/x4EDjiQMkr

thread.text: @a16z Strong framing. In an agent-first stack, UI is distribution but API + permissioning is product. If data contracts are brittle, autonomous workflows will route around the app layer.
@a16z The moat was never the UI. It was always the action graph underneath it. Most people are just now realizing this. Some of us have been building there for a while.
@a16z I just had a vision. Frequency is resonating hard. Might apply to one of my POCs with a customer. Agentic is not it.

@a16z Salesforce adopting the Model Context Protocol to expose its CRM data layer to external agents confirms that the proprietary UI has transitioned from a moat to a legacy tax on enterprise workflow efficiency.

@a16z Seema's right — UI loses defensibility, data wins. Same logic at the support layer. Fortuna's defensibility isn't the chat UI — it's the multi-model routing, the audit trails, the brand voice locked across Claude, GPT, Gemini, Meta AI and Grok. Save $75k–$200k/year per merchant.
@a16z the real unlock isn't just exposing the data layer, but figuring out how to make it navigable without the crutch of UI, which is way harder than people think
@a16z the changelog of headless products is longer than the thread on ui's still relevant
@a16z This is definitely where things are headed

@a16z Salesforce going headless is the bet that wins if agents become the consumer of B2B data. Risk is they underestimate how much margin came from UI lock-in and admin tax. Headless commoditizes both. Smart strategically, expensive in the short term.
@a16z Anyone who has ever used Salesforce already knew the value was not in their UI

items.text: schema completeness and permissions feel way more durable than UI polish in an agent world. if the agent can't trust the system boundary, the pretty frontend does not matter.

target.label: Seema Amble
target.text: https://t.co/o3nHSGDlUb

thread.text: https://t.co/o3nHSGDlUb
@seema_amble schema completeness and permissions feel way more durable than UI polish in an agent world. if the agent can't trust the system boundary, the pretty frontend does not matter.
@seema_amble A very comprehensive writeup, thank you!

@seema_amble Really great piece. The point that hits hardest for me is the shift from data-as-storage to data-as-context. Incumbents have decades of customer data but almost none of it is the data exhaust agents actually need - reasoning traces, exception patterns, why decisions got made.

@seema_amble "Compliance-critical data needs a single trusted source of truth." True, but the audit trail proving it should live somewhere independent of the system generating it, like on the verifiable storage. That's the layer that makes agent governance trustworthy.

@seema_amble This piece on where defensibility moves in the agentic era is exactly what we're building toward. Quick preview from @Worldline_AI (coming soon): per-instance trust profiles, failure patterns, and the verifier rationale behind every score, pulled from real sessions on your https://t.co/HI0z5n9u6l

@seema_amble I would also look into the history of headless infrastructure in e-commerce as a lot of it did not end up going anywhere. What I did think was still needed was a new order management platform. Potentially this time is different as a result of agents and better API technology.
@seema_amble Great piece of thinking @seema_amble
@seema_amble so well said! https://t.co/OqH3ZJaday

@seema_amble $CRM Salesforce won because entire GTM organizations reorganized themselves around its ontology: Accounts, Opportunities, Stages, Forecasts, Territories, Approvals. The UI enforced compliance with that ontology. Agents weaken the interface advantage, but they do not

@seema_amble This is very first-principles practitioner notes. Thank you! "system of verifier" is perhaps a key piece to enable system of action and system of coordination. In our own thesis we have categorised the knowledge base or SoR into 3 buckets: AI-native (cli, headless),

@seema_amble This is the only way. In construction we’ve been fighting to get people to use ‘the software’ for years. That’s about to completely reverse. The machine-to-machine workflows will free people up from and solve the latency issues we’ve had in the industry for decades.

@seema_amble One more: systems of record were a very convenient place to enforce permissions. That gets blown up real fast when Joe on the SDR team figures out how to get Claude to estimate next quarter’s revenue with the Salesforce MCP and does some casual insider trading 😬.

@seema_amble The interesting shift is that agents don’t make systems of record less important — they make the hidden parts more important: ontology, permissions, auditability, and persistence, etc. UI was the moat when humans clicked. In the agent era, context + trust architecture become the
@seema_amble Software should’ve lost its head years ago. I refuse to buy any more tools that require another log in, or that take me from my existing workflows.

@seema_amble This is a fantastic article. I work primarily in the Safety and Risk domain. There are some compelling cases we are thinking about now for the Systems of Record (and some equally big opportunities along with that). Great read!

@seema_amble Great read, thank you! Loved this- “Replacing your ATS is painful but survivable. Replacing your CRM is open-heart surgery. Replacing your ERP is open-heart surgery while the patient is running a marathon.”

@seema_amble Execs de-risk decisions by using a “leading” product. Where I think the enterprise software vendors will take a hit is in the million $ implementation fees. “Hey AI, set this up.” For $5k in tokens.

@seema_amble @seema_amble great framing, RIP to seat based pricing! Headless SoRs don’t weaken the orchestration layer. They make it more important. Every incumbent stripping its UI to expose an API just made the integration surface bigger. Value moves to whoever owns execution across the

@seema_amble SaaS has always been data, workflows and UI. If agent becomes the primary user then UI disappears, workflow creation and management gets transferred over to Agents - they are better at it. And the last of the UI elements move to generative UI rendered by AI for humans to consume.
@seema_amble Software isn’t losing its head so much as moving it. I’d bet the moat is data, permissions and workflow ownership now, not dashboard theatre.

@seema_amble Thanks for the post. It would be great read 20-30 meaningful examples that support this statement: "an agent can now read context, form a plan, select tools, execute actions, and review output, without a human in the loop for most tasks".
@seema_amble Absolutely knocked it out of the park with this one @seema_amble 👌
@seema_amble This is good! https://t.co/GFMosVPOIG
@seema_amble this is good. nice work.
@seema_amble love this take on trust as the new moat
@seema_amble All road leads to Palantir.
@seema_amble Amazing work Thank you for braking it down
@seema_amble Good read

@seema_amble Sharp piece but I think something is missing here : Machine-to-machine, as we've built it, seems to rest on two pillars : strict structure and named connectivity exposing in a sense some business logic. Enough for deterministic clients. Maybe not for consumers that reason?

@seema_amble A new super-app may be born very soon. Through this single super-app, humans will be able to interact with all of their agents. These agents will then: 1. Call upon various systems 2. Understand and execute instructions 3. Record and provide feedback
@seema_amble Sfdc is well more than a sql database - if anything it is a “database” - that which acts as a database
@seema_amble @grok tldr

@GoKiteAI @a16z the transaction graph matters. but what about the perception graph? agents that can negotiate APIs but cant see what theyre actually transacting — thats half the picture.

items.text: 140 to 220 tok/s on one GPU changes the local-agent math a lot. faster inference matters, but only if the surrounding tool loop is tight enough to use the extra speed.

target.label: Daniel Han
target.text: We released experimental MTP Qwen3.6 Unsloth GGUFs! Qwen3.6 27B MTP now runs at 140 tokens/s. Qwen3.6 35B-A3B MTP gets 220 tokens/s generation on a single GPU. Qwen3.6 27B and 35B-A3B have >1.4x speed-up over the original GGUFs without any change in accuracy. Guide + GGUFs + https://t.co/qP0SmtpB6h

thread.text: We released experimental MTP Qwen3.6 Unsloth GGUFs! Qwen3.6 27B MTP now runs at 140 tokens/s. Qwen3.6 35B-A3B MTP gets 220 tokens/s generation on a single GPU. Qwen3.6 27B and 35B-A3B have >1.4x speed-up over the original GGUFs without any change in accuracy. Guide + GGUFs + https://t.co/qP0SmtpB6h
More info and follow ups: https://t.co/ItMJKujyUJ
thread.quotedText: Qwen3.6 MTP Unsloth GGUFs now run 1.8x faster, increased from 1.4x just two days ago! This is due to llama.cpp adding --spec-draft-p-min 0.75! Args have also changed from --spec-type mtp to --spec-type draft-mtp Also increase --spec-draft-n-max 2 to 6 We also released https://t.co/8JUiTV6QO4

thread.text: @danielhanchen 140 to 220 tok/s on one GPU changes the local-agent math a lot. faster inference matters, but only if the surrounding tool loop is tight enough to use the extra speed.
@danielhanchen This would be awesome to run with pi!

@danielhanchen Thanks. Could you update the Claude Code page for Unsloth Studio if that's your preferred API endpoint for integration? I couldn't get it to work using the existing guide. Originally I was getting login errors, then after adding /v1 I got model errors. https://t.co/wr63B6E94Z
@danielhanchen your guide has a cruitial typo/bug it is NOT `--spec-type mtp` BUT `--spec-type draft-mtp`

@danielhanchen Also seeing the same pattern on my single 3060 while testing the llama.cpp MTP branch. Increasing `--spec-draft-n-max` beyond 2–3 doesn’t improve accepted tokens much, but rejected drafts increase a lot. So keeping it at 2 or 3 seems to be the sweet spot. No point going much https://t.co/7p5W1pzsIx
@danielhanchen 140 tok/s on a 27B is absurd. MTP for local speculative decoding is the exact kind of inference black magic we needed this year.

@danielhanchen I am using Dflash, we should have Dflash support in llama.cpp because that is giving me boost from 50TPS to 100TPS on coding tasks. RTX A6000. (didn't evaluate on long context) I will try this and let you know speed up with MTP.

@danielhanchen I know you said not to pay too much attention to tok/s variation model to model, but as I've seen this matter *a lot* in testing on MoE offload to RAM, could you tell us what the two high outlier lines are? there are multiple models w/ the same colors.

@danielhanchen Excellent! Thanks! And this may help push it to 4+ draft tokens - the paper came out yesterday: Attention Drift: What Autoregressive Speculative Decoding Models Learn https://t.co/ljFQ346OND

@danielhanchen Sorry, isn't the option draft-mtp? error while handling argument "--spec-type": unknown speculative type: mtp usage: --spec-type none,draft-simple,draft-eagle3,draft-mtp,ngram-simple,ngram-map-k,ngram-map-k4v,ngram-mod,ngram-cache
@danielhanchen numbers like that can change the game for real-time apps. impressive speed without sacrificing accuracy is key.
@danielhanchen maybe with this you'll be able to push to draft=4 https://t.co/WEedHReKvN
thread.quotedText: 🧵We discovered a new phenomenon in speculative decoding models that we call attention drift. As the drafter generates tokens, its attention moves from the "sink" onto its own recently-generated tokens. Fixing the underlying issue recovered up to 2× acceptance length, but why? https://t.co/uxo2JWQeoU

thread.text: @danielhanchen after i get my inference engine made i will look into this. https://t.co/6dSYICZDmG

@danielhanchen Got it running last night. Thanks for the perf boost on my rtx 3090! More perf and more context for the win! Had it write an AGV simulator overnight and it did it in one shot!. https://t.co/OXT7XOl1x3
@danielhanchen Hmmm I wonder if there's a way to do MoE+MTP. Especially for CPU only laptops.
@danielhanchen BTW, you should use Use `--spec-type draft-mtp --spec-draft-n-max 2`
@danielhanchen on *what" single gpu? 😅
@danielhanchen MTP models fail to load in LM Studio. Needs an update at LMS or llama.cpp
@danielhanchen mmh on which GPU you achieve 220 tokens/s with 35B-A3B ? I dont get this on 3090. Is this on a specific commit of the PR ?

@danielhanchen Just wondering did you quantize the head and base model together? In my experience quantizing the drafter together with the target model preserved accuracy the best. Especially for 4-bit AWQ or any low activation quantization. If you have bf16 verifier but 4bit quantizer, it
@danielhanchen Would it be a bigger or smaller increase if half your layers are on CPU/RAM?
@danielhanchen Anyone tried on a 3060 12GB? Does it soeedmit up at all? Or slow it down due to taking VRAM.
@danielhanchen Great! This will make the 27B Qwen 3.6 dense much more usable on slower hardware.
@danielhanchen @irukanji_invest Interesting . I have the better throughput with 3 draft tokens (I guess it means better acceptance rate). (Tested with llama-benchy)
@danielhanchen With Qwen3.6 27B , I got 80 t/s on RTX 4090 using mtp branch of llama.cpp, up from 40 from non-MTP. Amazing!
@danielhanchen so is the accuracy claim verified or just from your own tests

thread.text: @DeRonin_ yep. most teams are paying premium model prices to shovel boilerplate. better skill boundaries save more than prompt tricks. building https://t.co/wAXM9FxxVe for that layer.

@DeRonin_ Spot on—the routing mindset is table stakes now. One tactic that’s quietly compounding for me: dynamic context versioning. Instead of static SKILL.md or full greps, I maintain tiny “diff snapshots” (think: git-style summaries of the last N changes to key modules). Before a task,
@DeRonin_ why would you pay $0.02 for inference-driven linting when there are free deterministic linters?

@DeRonin_ An opensource project using LLMs to analyze code and create index. https://t.co/u4bLg25Bf5 Their benchmark shows 40% more accuracy on Monorepos on swebench-pro and 30% more accuracy on cross-repo, while saving 70% costs.

@DeRonin_ the solution for your problem is here, the core problem in long codebase that context problem everytime whole context was send in just small fix you can checkout this: https://t.co/RjGwTN3GwT

@DeRonin_ before the too-much-context, the issue was that agents and agent harnesses knew too little and did really, really dumb tunnel-vision things we're in a better world right now ...but it's time to pull the pendulum back a little
@DeRonin_ Great stuff. I didn’t know about the streaming responses ruining cache. How does streaming responses ruin the prompt cache?

@DeRonin_ Complete nonsense. I use Opus with full context and a €140/mo Ultra sub. I rarely hit the 5-hour limit, and the only real risk is a model error. Always use the best model with maximum context for the best results.
@DeRonin_ The cost problem is really a runtime design problem...uncontrolled context, repeated discovery, weak caching, and no model routing policy compound quickly.

@DeRonin_ man, this hits. i've been dumping entire garmin api docs into claude when i just need the auth endpoint. the token tax is real and it's silent. the trick is knowing exactly what context actually matters before you hit send.

@DeRonin_ that Karpathy quote at the top isn't documented anywhere. His actual position on context is real — he's written about context engineering directly. But "90% of your bill is context you didn't need" appears to be fabricated and attributed to him for reach. The tips are fine. The

@DeRonin_ the funniest part is ai coding already has its own version of "premature optimization" except now people burn money instead of cpu cycles turns out the real senior skill is knowing when not to send half your repo to the model for no reason
@DeRonin_ did karpathy actually say this or u just put his name on it 💀
@DeRonin_ Does prompt caching in kimi 2.6 cut costs on repetitive code checks?
@DeRonin_ You are basically telling the vibe coders to learn coding! Nice.

@DeRonin_ We’ve gone from doing it ourselves to managing an AI that’s doing it for us. It’s basically more frustrating because sometimes, it acts like a 5 yr kid who does literally what you say and not what you want 😭
@DeRonin_ This is all good, but I want a single command that encompasses all this knowledge. Is there a way to codify the above in a harness? I don't want to remember this

@DeRonin_ the auto-context one hits hard. my early projects were brutal -- dumping entire repos and wondering why claude was confused. claude code has gotten smarter about this but you still have to learn to scope your context intentionally or you're just paying for noise

@DeRonin_ Point 7 hits hardest. Our agents each have persistent constraint files with domain instructions that carry across sessions. Write them once, save thousands in repeated context. Routing gets the attention but the constraint architecture is what makes routing actually work.
@DeRonin_ You can actually just use cursor auto for all coding. If you know precisely what you're looking to build, it will build exactly what you tell it to, no hallucinations and no big bill.

@DeRonin_ Totally agree — the next big unlock isn’t just better models, but better context discipline. That’s exactly why we built Claude Context: an open-source project that helps Claude retrieve the right code context instead of blindly stuffing everything into the prompt — now with 11k+
@DeRonin_ Dumping your whole project into the most expensive AI just to fix one line of code does not make you a genius engineer. it just makes you the perfect customer to pay a massive API bill.
@DeRonin_ tbh the harder problem is knowing what 10% to keep. trimming the 90% is the easy part
@DeRonin_ Providing too much context is expensive in LLM usage and in everyday conversation lol.

@DeRonin_ yeah context discipline is the real cheat code. most people still dump half the repo for a 5-line fix and wonder why the bill is insane. grep first, cache stable stuff, route smart suddenly you're not burning money on tokens nobody reads. seniors already moved on, the gap is

@DeRonin_ yep been there. pasted a 2k line config into claude just to change one env var took way too long to realize i could've just said 'change db url from x to y' and saved $3 still figuring out the balance between enough context and 'here's my entire codebase'
@DeRonin_ https://t.co/E28cBAnfOn

items.text: pricing changes change behavior. once usage feels cheap enough, ppl stop micro-managing every loop and you finally see the real workflow.

target.label: Matt Pocock
target.text: Anthropic has given us a "dedicated monthly credit" Which, in effect, slashes AFK usage limits of Claude Code by ~5-20X Here's how it affects you: https://t.co/1nCCheqEC4

thread.text: Anthropic has given us a "dedicated monthly credit" Which, in effect, slashes AFK usage limits of Claude Code by ~5-20X Here's how it affects you: https://t.co/1nCCheqEC4
Want to follow along with updates, and new skills? Here's the place: https://t.co/fda9z0myLB
@mattpocockuk pricing changes change behavior. once usage feels cheap enough, ppl stop micro-managing every loop and you finally see the real workflow.

@mattpocockuk i guess `cat handoff.md | claude` is also off the table now?? aw man, this is going to be annoying! Can't wait to see how you shift your workflow man. The issue would be convincing my company to now pay for codex instead of claude for dev.
@mattpocockuk Someone from @OpenAIDevs should reach out as Matt is looking to try Codex for his AFK usage including Sandcastle. I’d love to see how this works with the new OAI Agents SDK.

@mattpocockuk @theo The downside of you having to explore codex and pi is an upside for me as a student, matt! Looking forward to seeing what you do with them. I was blown away when I started using codex and 5.5 last month when Claude got mega lobotomized. Overall I am paying more money, but Im
@mattpocockuk another way to look at it is pricing just went up by 5-20x
@mattpocockuk @theo Thank you matt I thought it was good news at first 😵‍💫

@mattpocockuk the 5-20x spread maps to which meter you hit first. AFK loops blow the rolling 5h way before the monthly credit math kicks in. plan quota and credit balance are two separate counters that never share a screen. https://t.co/mgog37UEEG written with ai
@mattpocockuk Unenforceable client side rules are unenforceable
thread.quotedText: Introducing npx claude-p A dropin replacement for claude -p https://t.co/aRWI6tWnD6

thread.text: @mattpocockuk @theo This is wrong? You can just use /goal and be afk.
@mattpocockuk @theo Another day, another seppuku https://t.co/99iztOENtL
@mattpocockuk BTW Codex does not charge you separately for automations / AFK usage: https://t.co/uSWqaSZhko
@mattpocockuk AI war should be the coolest ever, it's good for the civilians https://t.co/I7weSTMFwo
@mattpocockuk Don’t you regret asking to clarify claud -p usage? This what we have now. Jokes aside, it’s the worst thing they’ve done in 2026 so far.
@mattpocockuk They are still losing probably 3-5x of what they are charging in this scheme btw. Revolutionary! Lmfao

@mattpocockuk Good share. Things are def confusing and I have a lack of trust of anthropic Bc of this I bought the OpenAI pro plan. And so far, I haven’t looked back. Codex app using their 5.5 models and in fast mode has been great. They’ve built a great harness (I don’t use the codex

@mattpocockuk Subscription limits were always the wrong model for headless agents on cron. Metered pricing lets you budget agent compute like any other infrastructure cost. Predictable, scalable, no artificial ceiling at 3 AM. This is better for anyone running autonomous workloads 24/7.
@mattpocockuk Just leave CC and let it rot. Move to Pi or Opencode, I don't think that Codex will remain good for long unfortunately. I wonder if Droid will have the same problems later.

@mattpocockuk I really like your skills and sandcastle and use them on daily basis so I made a ptty emulator that serves claude code as an ACP https://t.co/z3z7EbMJHl .... also it has a print command that also ptty emulated 🤫
@mattpocockuk dedicated monthly credit is a very elegant phrase for the meter moved and nobody got a tour

@mattpocockuk Subsidies for workflows outside of the ecosystem were bound to end eventually; afk/programmatic is the next big opportunity they’re most likely going after. They ran the same playbook with the opus 4.7 release. Something on that end will happen in the next few days, either
@mattpocockuk You can literally hear in Matt's voice how sick and tired he is of this Anthropic BS.
@mattpocockuk This feels like Claude showing up with free drink coupons after everyone already left for Codex.
@mattpocockuk That's when I stared using sandcastle+claude in one of my agentic workflows, sigh

@mattpocockuk Hmm... can't we just make a plan with /grill-with-docs and /to-prd and /to-issues and then use the /goal from CC to implement these be following the implementer/reviewer prompts ? ofc it kinda loses the sandcastle perks, but should still be "workable" if we run out of credits?
@mattpocockuk The irony here is you built Sandcastle to sandbox claude -p and now they Sandbox you with monthly limits

@mattpocockuk If you use Claude to mainly build software, I'm not sure why you would need all this "afk" tools actually? I run everything in the "hitl" interface, multiple projects, yet I'm pretty often afk and it keeps running when I sleep. What am I missing?
@mattpocockuk Thanks for this explainer, made it much clearer to me!

@mattpocockuk This is a great explanation, thank you. My simplified take on it - Anthropic are just starting early with cutting subsidies. Eventually OpenAI will need to start looking at costs too and it will come in form of limit changes (like with Claude), dumbing down of the models or

@mattpocockuk wait so the cut is only on the AFK / automated runs right? been doing overnight tasks on small projects, do i need to switch to the dedicated credit thing now or is the regular max sub still fine for normal interactive use

@mattpocockuk @theo yup such an insane thing to do to cut usage and make it seem like they are giving us something for free. crazy af but hope the freed up gpus are worth it for the limits. had a particular seo workflow inside of kilocode which relied on claude -p but sad that ive to migrate now
@mattpocockuk the agent workflows I was rationing start making sense now. 5-20x headroom is a different planning premise
@mattpocockuk Chatgpt 5.5 is better, but welcome aboard. Now even GitHub is no longer allowed, I'm moving off Claude completely.

@mattpocockuk The real gap isn't the cap, it's that most teams don't have a budget model for AI at all. Subscription limits let you pretend infinite usage exists. Metered pricing forces you to actually plan per-session costs. The uncomfortable part: teams realize how much they're spending

thread.text: Modern (@mdrnhq) is building the AI-native operating system for IT, with secure agents that automate help desk, access, devices, security, and on/off-boarding end-to-end. Congrats on the launch, @sebwpoole & @AlexTomovski! https://t.co/MkUjkUgaMH https://t.co/nLsC8rrBTE
@ycombinator @mdrnhq @sebwpoole @AlexTomovski help desk + access + offboarding is exactly where agents feel real. clear edges, ugly repetitive work, and obvious ROI.

@ycombinator @mdrnhq @sebwpoole @AlexTomovski I can see more and more startups doing os for companies and I think the bigger bet for them is the data they collect that will slowly transition the companies to run autonomous

@ycombinator @mdrnhq @sebwpoole @AlexTomovski help desk is honestly such a good agent wedge. tickets already come with screenshots, permissions, weird device state, all the messy little handles agents need to not hallucinate the whole job
@ycombinator @mdrnhq @sebwpoole @AlexTomovski Must follow for founders and investors as we move into the age of agents. End-to-end automation is the only viable path for IT.
@ycombinator @mdrnhq @sebwpoole @AlexTomovski IT requires dealing with hardware though? like switches and routers
@ycombinator @mdrnhq @sebwpoole @AlexTomovski Is it purely for saas or services?
@ycombinator @mdrnhq @sebwpoole @AlexTomovski This looks sick. How does the agent side handle the long tail of weird legacy internal tools that usually break every other IT copilot?
@ycombinator @mdrnhq @sebwpoole @AlexTomovski AI-native IT ops is the real shift.

@ycombinator @mdrnhq @sebwpoole @AlexTomovski Onboarding agents work great until day one when the new hire needs that one legacy SaaS the agent has never seen. The long tail of internal tools is where IT automation usually breaks down.
@ycombinator @mdrnhq @sebwpoole @AlexTomovski exciting to see innovations in ai-native systems. automation will redefine efficiency in it operations.
@ycombinator @mdrnhq @sebwpoole @AlexTomovski congrats on the launch!
@ycombinator @mdrnhq @sebwpoole @AlexTomovski Congrats guys

@ycombinator @mdrnhq @sebwpoole @AlexTomovski IT is a queue management problem disguised as a tech problem. The hard part for agents: holding state across Okta/Jamf/ticketing — where most 'IT copilots' have stalled.
@ycombinator @mdrnhq @sebwpoole @AlexTomovski Wow, so many Companies getting funding...
@ycombinator @mdrnhq @sebwpoole @AlexTomovski congrats guys 🔥
@ycombinator @mdrnhq @sebwpoole @AlexTomovski congrats !!
@ycombinator @mdrnhq @sebwpoole @AlexTomovski sick!
@ycombinator @mdrnhq @sebwpoole @AlexTomovski The secure-agents angle is the key. End-to-end automation only gets adopted when permissions, auditability, and fallback logic are built in from day one.
@ycombinator @mdrnhq @sebwpoole @AlexTomovski 🚀🚀
@ycombinator @mdrnhq @sebwpoole @AlexTomovski Congrats!

thread.text: Turn any website into an API with /browser-to-api. This skill analyzes network activity, CDP logs, and website behavior to generate a custom OpenAPI spec. Watch Codex one-shot a fully documented OpenTable API client from a single prompt 👀 https://t.co/MDKGaHKzAy

@derekmeegan @kylejeong Was literally telling @pk_iv about this couple of weeks ago.
@derekmeegan just killed so many browsers
@derekmeegan this just killed 50 YC startups
@derekmeegan every website is an API with browserbase
@derekmeegan Made it a couple of months ago https://t.co/4911F6uZEq check it out : )

@derekmeegan This is technically slick, and amazing it can be done, but for actual products, it's building on sand. Most sites explicitly ban this in their TOS(!), and running real usage through reconstructed APIs is just waiting to get cut off. Sustainable products partner and get official

@derekmeegan @derekmeegan would this work with a closed ecosystem webapp like Advyzon? They’re a CRM, portfolio management system, with no API and no way for us to programmatically move data in and out or connect to external tools like slack. Been trying to figure out a workaround!

@derekmeegan Interesting. Most "APIs" from websites are just fancy screen scrapes in my experience. I built an AI video tool that pulls data from YouTube for similar reasons. What's the edge case coverage like?
@derekmeegan yeah, this kind of wrapper has been the missing layer for me. cdp logs alone never gave the right shape. curious how it handles auth.

@derekmeegan might be a dumb question but if i wanted to integrate with a platform that does not have any API docs and they didn't have the bandwidth to build them, could I leverage @browserbase to build it? call it API-as-a-Service
@derekmeegan this is exactly what i needed last week, spent 2 days reverse engineering a site api by hand. does it work for sites with heavy auth flows too or only public endpoints
@derekmeegan instant bookmark this is one of those things i need to try immediately before pretending i understand the full implications
@derekmeegan Generating a fully documented API spec just from watching network traffic is genuinely one of those things that should not work as well as it does

@derekmeegan Analyzing CDP logs + network activity to reverse-engineer an OpenAPI spec is a genuinely clever approach. Most "turn site into API" tools require manual mapping or brittle selectors. Using actual traffic as the source of truth is much more robust. Codex one-shotting the client

thread.text: 🚀Launching: LangSmith Engine LangSmith Engine is an agent that sits on top of your traces It runs in the background and automatically identifies issues It then proactively suggests action items (code changes, evaluators to add) Try it today: https://t.co/f2ZRkogGGS https://t.co/R1e2B69jdR
@hwchase17 this is the right direction. raw traces are only step one. the useful part is turning them into the next fix. same reason i'm building https://t.co/wAXM9FxxVe for the skill layer.

@hwchase17 @hwchase17 Is it just me or is there an ongoing issue. I get an error while trying to connect Github from under Traces -> Issues -> Connect github. Tried 2 different projects. Oh and another issue: Only two of my 3 projects show the "Issues" tab (both have 0 traces), one of the https://t.co/nCOHZ2gP83

@hwchase17 This is where observability needed to go. Traces sitting in a dashboard waiting to be read is dead time. An agent that watches them and feeds suggestions back into your workflow closes the loop between running agents and building better ones.

@hwchase17 This is the kind of product layer I like. Traces are useful, but an agent that continuously spots issues and recommends fixes turns observability into momentum. Curious to see how it handles real-world edge cases.
@hwchase17 Great news. Curious how far you see this automation going over time? Feels like the natural next step is parts of the optimization loop becoming autonomous.
@hwchase17 I already loved the langsmith tracing in its current form, providing actionable insights and using AI to detect anomalies on top of it is a big game changer.

@hwchase17 One additional thing I thought is, how it useful if engine integrated with Linear. Tracking AI issues (specific fails/consistency/hallucination issues) hard. Similar to Github/Linear integration, if engine catches the issue, it flags Linear ticket.

@hwchase17 The eval-proposing half is the interesting one. Hand-curated suites always lag real failures — by the time you write the eval, the bug shipped. Engine inverts the loop. Open question: do the proposed evaluators converge to a stable suite over time, or churn with every new trace?
@hwchase17 Had a chance to test this early. It’s a game changer for high variance agents
@hwchase17 This is awesome , someone always working at backend to improve the agent
@hwchase17 Very cool
@hwchase17 Woww. So it’s like a test environment for agents right?
@hwchase17 @BraceSproul That’s awesome- placed where the knowledge is. I will try it out 👍🏻
@hwchase17 neat
@hwchase17 Amazing!! 👏👏👏
@hwchase17 Amazing

items.text: every cycle has loud categories and quiet winners. the quiet winners usually look weird right up until they don't.

target.label: Bilal Zuberi
target.text: I remind myself of this quite often. https://t.co/l14iRcaNLX

thread.text: I remind myself of this quite often. https://t.co/l14iRcaNLX
@bznotes every cycle has loud categories and quiet winners. the quiet winners usually look weird right up until they don't.
@bznotes Do you have data on vintage quality/returns by year? My assumption is the 2026 vintage wil be rough. There are a lot of tourist founders starting companies these days. Agree? Hard no?
@bznotes @rrhoover The scooter year was really something.
@bznotes THIS! Nice plug @aileenlee
@bznotes @mattmireles Deel is mobility for salaries Anduril puts terrorists in the crypt OpenAI is an autonomous vehicle for the mind

@bznotes I’d bet most of these companies got funded primarily on the founder, not the theme. The “hot” theme of the year almost never produced the most valuable company of the year. The outlier founder did, usually working on something the consensus hadn’t caught up to yet. Exceptional
@bznotes interesting you omitted recent years because it'd expose the narrative twist as people could remember hottest theme and most valuable were in the same space.
@bznotes poor scooters didn’t stand a chance against @Bouazizalex & @shuooo https://t.co/kaPqZqMrtH
@bznotes The biggest ideas take years to develop and execute before they are ready for prime time

@bznotes @mattmireles Many of these are the right categories, but getting the timing right is very hard. If I started a fund today, I think I would call it Off Axis and I would look for themes that are important but outside the hype cycle.

@bznotes Missing rows: 2023: LLMs → ChatGPT 2024: AI Agents → ? 2025: Humanoid robots → ? The hottest theme right now is AI infrastructure. The winner probably looks more like Snowflake in 2012 than any of the current AI darlings.
@bznotes Had this in our AGM deck a few years ago as a reminder that we need to adapt to the technologies of our time, but stay firm to the categories we have long-term conviction on
@bznotes people who are building what they envision is the future, and not tagging along to trends are the ones that really push the envelope forward
@bznotes Canva in 2013 while everyone was talking wearables is the one that gets me every time. The best companies just solve a real thing and let the trend narrative catch up later.

@bznotes Tesla won't make the list because they started way before, but their "Autonomus Vehicle" division did start around the 2015 wave, and that's the one successful trend where they've outlasted and outmanoeuvred the competition and that division is productionizing into billions of
@bznotes None of the hot topics led to great companies. Does it mean we shouldn't work on agents and AI – the hot topics of this year?

@bznotes For something to become mainstream “hot” there has to be some kind of successful early product or very working prototypes. For that to exist a company must have produced it, likely meaning years of R&D since funding.
@bznotes 2026 Hype - AI Infra / Hardware / Power and agentic workflows 2026 non consensus but right - compliance substrate that will make robots run safely in fintech health commerce
@bznotes if you ask me, the real lesson is don’t chase the year’s hot theme. find the boring constraint the theme exposes. themes get attention. constraints create companies.
@bznotes I was at the fireside chat yesterday and you said one thing that was crisp "Whatever you do, don't listen to me"
@bznotes @garrytan Someone should do the same for “Request for Startups”. Total foolishness. Where would AirBedandBreakfast fall on that?
@bznotes Yeah maybe these were the hot themes of the year among the short bus kids
@bznotes @rrhoover I’d bet meta materials is what we look back at the big thing that’s starting now by the end of the decade.
@bznotes @Hadley Hey fellow Bilal :p. Great post and reminder. The bull case for being contrarian despite the growing importance of the momentum factor🙏
@bznotes @davidmrush Would be great to add a column to show the buzziest startup of that year.
@bznotes The right hand column is only known with hindsight however
@bznotes @grok make a similar chart for next 10 years
@bznotes @garrytan Worth adding: most of these “valuable companies” weren’t in hot consumer categories at all. Vanta, Deel, Wiz, Scale — all unsexy B2B SaaS.
@bznotes 2024: @cognition theme: AI agent
@bznotes Seems too reductive to be very meaningful
@bznotes Metaverse will still replace the internet though. Early ≠ incorrect.
@bznotes another @rex_woodbury banger that stands the test of time
@bznotes What do you think is most overlooked today?
@bznotes You could add a column for 'Top Failure Modes' and show the same problems for every year!

items.text: hot sectors absorb smart people, but they also standardize taste fast. empty rooms are where weird distribution and weird conviction still compound.

target.label: GREG ISENBERG
target.text: The hot theme each year is basically a talent and capital vacuum cleaner. It sucks all the smartest people and all the money into one room. Which means every other room is empty. The most valuable companies get built in the empty rooms. This chart is 10+ years of proof.

thread.text: The hot theme each year is basically a talent and capital vacuum cleaner. It sucks all the smartest people and all the money into one room. Which means every other room is empty. The most valuable companies get built in the empty rooms. This chart is 10+ years of proof.
thread.quotedText: I remind myself of this quite often. https://t.co/l14iRcaNLX

thread.text: @gregisenberg hot sectors absorb smart people, but they also standardize taste fast. empty rooms are where weird distribution and weird conviction still compound.

@gregisenberg the chart works for VC bets. for solo operators its the opposite, the empty rooms have no distribution no audience no oxygen. i pick the hot room every time and build the boring thing on top.
@gregisenberg i get my themes from ideabrowser https://t.co/tTcXoxa8ea
@gregisenberg bro, you were the biggest Web3 hype-man on the planet for 2 years straight. You can't throw shade at this graph 🤣
@gregisenberg 💯 The value is in companies actually solving a problem they know everything about
@gregisenberg really good point!

@gregisenberg The corollary is also true: the best time to start in a space is right after the "hot theme" peak, when talent floods out and the vacuum empties. The companies that lasted from crypto, web3, and AR were built by people who stayed when everyone else left.

@gregisenberg the empty-room thing works backwards. some empty rooms are empty because talent tried and failed in them, not because nobody noticed. picking the empty room only works if you can tell undervalued from broken

@gregisenberg Same pattern plays out in design. When everyone rushes toward the hot theme, the visual language gets derivative fast — everyone borrows from the same references. The products with distinctive, original design got built quietly in the "empty room," with nobody watching and no

@gregisenberg This is exactly why building content systems for business owners is so wide open right now. Everyone's chasing AI. Meanwhile the founders who invest in personal brand and short form are printing inbound leads with zero competition.

@gregisenberg Counterpoint from the empty room — every AI marketing tool is fighting for the same 5 SaaS use cases. Building neuromarketing for SMB brands instead. CAC is a fraction of the AI-for-X crowd because nobody's pitching that buyer. What empty room are you watching?

@gregisenberg Gutter cleaning. 2009. South Jersey. That room wasn't just empty — people were actively walking past it. "Blue collar." "Unsexy." "Doesn't scale." Meanwhile thousands launched SaaS into saturated markets with zero differentiation. I just kept showing up and answering the phone.
@gregisenberg the empty rooms part is real but everyone knows about it now, which might be the problem — everyone's trying to find the next empty room instead of actually building in one
@gregisenberg The boring counterpoint: empty rooms are empty because the market isn't there yet, not because everyone forgot to look. Contrarian and early get confused a lot.

@gregisenberg the empty rooms are expensive in a different way. you don’t just lose capital and attention. you lose the comfort of sounding obvious. for a while, everyone in the crowded room gets to look serious. everyone outside it looks early, weird, or wrong. then the labels switch.

@gregisenberg The hot theme vacuum cleaner is real. Last year it was agents. This year it is agents but with a different slide deck. Next year it will be agents again but wearing a trench coat and calling themselves "autonomous systems."
@gregisenberg except now the empty rooms have no capital either because it's all deployed to the hot theme, so you need a different constraint to win there
@gregisenberg Those empty rooms make it brutal to hire or raise money since everyone's chasing the hot trend. Hindsight makes picking them look way easier than it was.

@gregisenberg Hot themes make shiny demos. Empty rooms make invoices. I’d look for the neglected handoff with a weekly number: lead→reply, demo→next action, quote→follow-up. Boring leaks are often better markets than crowded rooms.
@gregisenberg the empty room thing is so real. everyone piled into AI agents, meanwhile the boring infra plays underneath are quietly printing.

@gregisenberg There’s a difference between following attention and following pressure. Attention creates crowded markets. Pressure creates unmet needs. Most enduring companies are built where pressure quietly accumulates while everyone else is distracted by the trend of the year.
@gregisenberg Is it AI, AI and AI from 2023 to 2025? I know that’s not the point of this post but I am just curious.
@gregisenberg what's crazy is how that capital "vacuum cleaner" so often ignores profits and bloats so many executive salaries!
@gregisenberg For founders today in the 'empty rooms', what are the most effective distribution channels to gain initial traction?

@gregisenberg The empty rooms are usually embarrassingly boring: missed calls, dead forms, dirty CRMs, no follow-up owner. Everyone wants the agent demo; fewer people want the operating loop that makes it print money.
@gregisenberg Funny thing is that the most valuable company is revealed much later so folks in the bubble can never come out.
@gregisenberg I don't think Notion was started in 2014 but yes you do make your point!
@gregisenberg Still soo hard to see the difference when you're in it right?
@gregisenberg Its much easier to find a seat when everyone else is fighting for the same door + the quiet helps you think.
@gregisenberg the empty room is where ppl still answer your cold dm because nobody has tried to turn their annoying spreadsheet into a market yet
@gregisenberg Everyone chasing AI agents while the next Deel gets built in the room nobody's looking at.
@gregisenberg the ai agent space feels exactly like this right now. everyone's building chatbots and rag systems while the boring infrastructure stuff sits empty. that's where the actual money is

target.label: leerob
target.text: Code is actually the right abstraction. Too often I see the future of software engineering diminished down to, effectively, writing and reviewing markdown files. Yes, it will be hard to review thousands of lines of agent code. But maybe the takeaway is that you want less code?

thread.text: Code is actually the right abstraction. Too often I see the future of software engineering diminished down to, effectively, writing and reviewing markdown files. Yes, it will be hard to review thousands of lines of agent code. But maybe the takeaway is that you want less code?
@leerob markdown becomes a crutch when ppl stop trusting generated code. the goal is not less code. it's tighter feedback loops around the code that actually matters.
@leerob Yeah but have you tried testing code and skills working together?
@leerob We've gone the other way, surprisingly, nobody is a "slop cannon" If anything, it's made us care a lot more about how our data's structured, and we use skills to enforce that across the team
@leerob https://t.co/iLUX7VEzoA

thread.quotedText: Figma, Adobe are dead code is truth & destination in software design. Strong AI is final abstraction: 1/ text to image mockup, iterate by text & area edits 2/ image mock + backend logic, business rules -> code gen 3/ iterate in code. English is latest programming language

thread.text: @leerob Great take! I’m ok with slop as long as the interface between the pieces are clean, I can always go in a deslop modules easily
@leerob man i’d love if someone from cursor livestreamed their coding setup someday

@leerob the surprising amount of detail line is doing a lot of work the boring details often are the product the null checks, retries, edge cases, etc if your abstraction hides them without preserving their constraints, you did not simplify the system you deleted the trust layer

@leerob Exactly. The "just write better prompts" crowd is rediscovering why we invented type systems and interfaces. Less code is the goal, but that code needs to carry real contracts between components, not just vibes in a markdown file.

@leerob I think we need to change code review process - we dont always need to see every single line of code by default, but rather which part of code was change, what has dependency on it etc. Kinda funny how this didnt change at all its still same layout as in 2010. And what happens is
@leerob Strong verification, clean architecture, and thoughtful design are still critical as AI generated code scales.

@leerob The useful split is: markdown/skills are a control layer. Code is still where the invariants, edge cases, and runtime behavior actually live. If agent output is too large to review, the fix is not lossy summaries. It’s smaller diffs, stronger verification, and better

@leerob I land on the other side of this. I think we'll see more teams, especially in startups, adopting a dark factory approach where AI writes the code, reviews it, and checks it in end-to-end. Right now we're in this weird in-between place where we use AI to write the code but still
@leerob https://t.co/rmSjma9Xdx

@leerob Code is the right abstraction for what ships. The intent that produced it gets compiled out: rejected approaches, constraint conversations, and lessons that shaped the pattern. Agents reproduce shape without intent, which is what the deslop question is actually about. What's the

@leerob I love the push that "my diffs are too large" signifies slop! Although I disagree on another part. Lossy markdown are the best way to de-slop / avoid slop we can trust coding agents to crush when given the right abstractions. the plan should be pulling out the key decisions so

@leerob "maybe the takeaway is that you want less code" - this is the most important line in the whole post and it's buried in the middle. the real problem isn't that agents write code we can't review. it's that agents write MORE code than necessary because they have zero cost pressure
@leerob Btw, instead of writing tons of markdowns, we should focus on writing tons of unit/integration/property tests. AI might ignore your specs, but they won't give up until the test is green
@leerob markdown layer helps, but it can't carry the whole system

@leerob human code understanding from a spec never really made sense to me. sure, it's a good way to communicate the task to the agent, but after the fact it seems much safer (less drift more nuance) to just read the code - or have a back and forth with an agent to understand the code.

@leerob Natural language is becoming the new high-level language, but that doesn't mean the need for low-level code disappears. All the conventions, systems, and best practices are going to need both a human and an automated counterpart working together, like centaur chess.

@leerob What’s even trickier right now is when models make decisions without properly running them by the user Been having a big problem with GPT 5.5 because it makes decisions for me on approach without asking

@leerob The composability argument is the one I keep coming back to. Markdown works great for static content. But once you need server-side validation, conditional rendering, or a cart that persists - you're writing code anyway. The question is whether you're fighting the framework to
@leerob when you realise the markdowns are getting lossy, as in the model doesn't actually do what you told it to do. https://t.co/xmhyyoHjSZ

@leerob Half the agent code complaint is about boring software. Imports, error handling, whether `def` sits in front of a function. The real abstraction maybe is higher than code, it's intent, and one level up, the business know-how nobody captures.

@leerob Curious how you think about the harness around that code. We've been pulling apart orchestration scaffolding we built 18 months ago piece by piece as models improved. The code problem wasn't writing it, it was all the routing and permission logic you had to wrap around it.

@leerob The solution with the least amount of code is usually the best. Verbose code, especially when using LLMs, is not great, because there's too much to review. Not only that, when it's 4:45 p.m. on a Friday and you have 3,000 lines of code to review, you're not going to review it.

@leerob Code stays the abstraction because plain prose specs leave too much wiggle room. Strict types and tests pin behavior. Markdown briefs work for intent but they cannot encode null handling or retry semantics at the level you need to ship.

@leerob why are all these replies ai. and isn’t this partially what codebase indexing exists to solve? rag is controversial, but it feels the like solution for many agents, and maybe humans too if the retrieval is strong enough

@leerob agree. what tipped us was making the agent ship small commits per logical step instead of one giant diff. markdown spec is fine for input but reviewing intent compresses lossy, commit by commit you can actually catch where the agent took a left turn. way easier to revert one bad
@leerob I don't know. I feel now we are working at a higher abstraction layer than code. I never wrote assembly.
@leerob the markdown future of software was always a bit of a cope tbh. less code that actually works > more yaml for AI to parse
@leerob Code is the source of truth, markdown is the executive summary — teach agents to write both, plus a guilty-pleasure commit message. 😄
@leerob Less code is the goal, but slop compounds faster than you think. Better abstractions beat summaries every time.

@clairevo Basically anyone who put their whole org on Gemini

@clairevo Switching these tools is far from zero cost, unless you are doing the most basic use cases imaginable. The more advanced the use the higher your switching cost, which is exactly how it should be frankly as it is indicative of value delivery.

thread.text: @a16z the winner is whoever owns the action loop, not just the record. a 'system of intelligence' that only summarizes is just prettier BI.

@a16z We are going to see more esoteric data sources become part of this system of record umbrella as Agents begin to learn to interact with them. This opens up the opportunity for true orchestration based on a wider signals bus.

@a16z The API-consumed system of record point is the real enterprise shift. The UI stops being the only operating surface. Agents become the reasoning/action layer above the data, but only if writebacks, permissions, and exceptions are designed cleanly.
@a16z The control point is the action graph: read access across SoR data, write permissions into workflows, evals for bad actions, and audit logs tight enough for finance and legal to trust.

@a16z The "where" is right, the piece under-weights the "how it gets measured". If value moves to the reasoning layer, the unit of compensation moves with it: pricing stops being seat-per-month and starts being outcome-per-call. The real lock-in over the next few years won't be the
@a16z This is the most important shift in enterprise software nobody is talking about loudly enough. Salesforce, HubSpot, SAP. They’re all systems of record.

@a16z A true System of Intelligence must be completely end-to-end. Ingestion -> modeling -> resolving entities -> building context and memories -> AND the usage layer (analytics, workflow, ML, assistants/cowork).

@a16z I think this is one of the most important transitions happening in software right now. I've been building in fintech for years and the shift from static data entry to contextual, intelligent action is massive. The companies that figure out how to own the reasoning layer — not

@a16z One angle worth pulling: the article assumes cloud orchestration of US SaaS. In EU and regulated verticals the gravity shifts twice: the SoR is fragmented (no Salesforce equivalent, Zucchetti, TeamSystem, vertical ERPs), and the intelligence layer ingests data that legally can't

@a16z “System of intelligence” is the polite VC phrase for an AI layer that reads the company, ranks reality, steers decisions, and writes back into the business. That is not just a new software category. That is an authority surface. Who can still say no? 🛠️
@a16z All the CRMs that have neglected their API for years... https://t.co/JOuSAFjIF7
@a16z if we're a decade out and labs own most of the compute and storage, it seems to me they'd also want to own the system of record

@a16z The friend-graph to news-feed framing is the cleanest version of this I've seen. The harder question, the one we keep hitting in mid-market deployments, is what "owning the system of intelligence" looks like for a company that can't hire 200 internal FDEs. Wrote the LatAm version
@a16z i agree :) published oct 2024: https://t.co/RqSmcFJgfn https://t.co/IBuoEisMf0
@a16z For manual and paper based systems(critical sectors esp). Isn’t it possible to become these 3(all in one, aka trust layer) ; System of Reality + System of Record + System of Intelligence?

@a16z What if you're actively using an incredible system of intelligence for the GTM of that same system? Not asking for a friend, but I am asking you to check out our latest post 😉 https://t.co/zE54YXiXJu

@a16z System of intelligence is the right name. It's already happening one layer down. Individuals have been wiring reasoning layers on top of fragmented tools for years. The enterprise version will look like what power users already built for themselves.

@a16z Account research: 56% AI-forward. Pipeline forecasting: 60% still traditional. The harder the task, the longer the holdout.
@a16z system of record sounds passive system of intelligence is really the place where context turns into permission to act

@a16z This is the most overhyped idea since the emergence of LLM tools three years ago. How small companies can have a seat into this battle if everyone is competing to have your data and act like an operating system for your business? The orchestration layer for this will be huge.
@a16z Yes yes yes! building along these lines right now :)

@a16z Bankers learning that software is not just code.

@a16z The shift from system of record→system of action is real. But most systems of action are just probabilistic agents. The winner isn’t who orchestrates intent—it’s who guarantees execution. we believe and build deterministic,auditable execution OpSys layer. @steph_zhang @GioAhern

@a16z The SoR moat was always about data gravity — the more you stored, the more workflows depended on you. SoI flips this: the moat is reasoning quality and context. CRM vendors who think they're safe because they have the data are about to find out that having the data isn't the same

@a16z System of intelligence > system of record is the right thesis. Same logic at the application layer for ecommerce. Fortuna sits above the helpdesk reading clean data from Shopify, WooCommerce, ShipStation, Klaviyo and routes resolution across the labs. Save $75k–$200k/year per

@a16z This shift makes total sense. The real winner will be the smart layer on top that actually understands your data and helps you act fast instead of just storing it. Companies building that intelligence layer are going to own the next decade of enterprise software.

@a16z In regulated finance the system of record stays sticky. Every model decision has to be replayable against the underlying transaction record — intelligence sits on top, doesn't replace. Strip the audit trail and the intelligence layer becomes the compliance liability.

@a16z Peep my speedrun application. One unified system of intelligence (and action) sits on top of a business's systems of record. A digital representation of the operating model becomes an executable program. You've never seen anything like this. https://t.co/ixhm5wEs8e
@a16z https://t.co/weP3FopqsD
thread.quotedText: https://t.co/Z7NPCbCFW5

thread.text: @a16z The battle is definitely shifting from storing the data to actually making it do work. Building the layer that turns raw records into immediate action is exactly where the value is hiding.

target.text: Agent observability is a means to an end: making your agent better. But observability and evals tools have traditionally failed to connect traces to meaningful actions. Agent engineering teams are left combing through traces, guessing at root causes, and writing evals manually. https://t.co/EO2U7Tmx8V

thread.text: Agent observability is a means to an end: making your agent better. But observability and evals tools have traditionally failed to connect traces to meaningful actions. Agent engineering teams are left combing through traces, guessing at root causes, and writing evals manually. https://t.co/EO2U7Tmx8V
Try it out! https://t.co/IuB5NdvLyM
@bentannyhill observability that ends in manual trace spelunking is still a bottleneck. that missing layer is basically what i'm building with https://t.co/wAXM9FxxVe

@bentannyhill This is the missing middleware of LLMOps. Traces without action are just expensive logs. The loop you've closed: observe → diagnose → fix → eval; is what separates agent engineering from agent hoping. The "ready-to-merge" framing is bold. Would love to know how Engine
@bentannyhill the gap is root cause, not trace volume. in our testing, loops and coordination breakdowns are the two modes most likely to look fine in traces until they've already cost you.
@bentannyhill evals written against a taxonomy of known failure modes close that gap faster. pisama maps 51 production failure modes so you're not guessing at root causes from raw traces.

@bentannyhill "Failed to connect traces to meaningful actions" is exactly the gap. Observability tells you what happened; it doesn't generate a hypothesis space for what *could* have happened on cases you didn't see. Trajectory-level pre-deploy testing fills that. Same data shape as traces,
@bentannyhill ready-to-merge fixes are the right scary bar. how do you stop the loop from overfitting to the traces it already saw instead of finding the failure class underneath?
@bentannyhill how do you decide which subset of traces gets agentically explored (cause the agentic exploration will get expensive quick)?
@bentannyhill Where's the demo? Tired of agent tools that only work in blog posts 🙄
@bentannyhill This is awesome! Epic release.
