thread.quotedText: One app, two platforms, four programming languages. The things that look the simplest are often the hardest to build. @raycast is one of them. Here's a technical deep dive on how we built v2 👉 https://t.co/JP9nVNzbqB https://t.co/dREVbDf3Hm

thread.text: @swyx these writeups do 3 jobs at once: user trust, hiring, and category signaling. devtools teams that skip them usually don't understand where conviction actually comes from.
@swyx how do you balance the transparency of a public stack writeup with protecting proprietary tech details?

@swyx writing the stack publicly is a brutal forcing function. half the teams I know couldn't do this exercise because they'd discover their architecture is 80% accidents and 20% decisions. when you actually have to defend each choice in writing, you find out fast whether you built a

@swyx One thing nobody writes: the parts of the stack they regret. The "honest tech writeup" with the 3 things they'd burn down would be 10x more useful than the polished version. Why is that one missing?

@swyx especially true for agent infra. the architectural decisions that actually matter — execution boundaries, protocol design, state management — are the least visible. showing the architecture is showing the thinking.
@swyx raycast is the rare devtool where the writeup actually matches the engineering. most of these read like 'we used react' and stop right where they get interesting
@swyx Thank you, this means a lot coming from you! It's a love letter from our team to our community. We deeply care about our product 🖤
@swyx “Show the work” is underrated GTM. A real technical writeup filters buyers, recruits builders, and scares off tourists. Also forces the team to admit whether there was a system or just vibes.

@swyx The documentation forces the rigor. Most teams don't write these because they never made real decisions. They just accumulated defaults. Writing it forces you to answer "why not X?" and that's where you discover whether you have architecture or just inertia.
@swyx thanks! nothing to hide ppl give web-based apps a bad rep because most of them are poorly built native apps can be as bad too
@swyx totally agree on the depth thing - most teams i've worked with just cargo cult whatever the last company used and call it a day. raycast actually seems to think through their decisions
@swyx the hiring ROI alone justifies it. discord's "how we store trillions of messages" post is still pulling eng interest years later
@swyx yeah this is such an underrated founder skill. a good deep dive somehow becomes recruiting, sales, support docs, and a tiny warning shot in one tab
@swyx Same at the corporate desk. The peers who write up their reasoning get pulled into the next call; the ones who just deliver don't.
@swyx the "tell hires there's sota work here" bit hits. we've been doing internal-only retro notes for 17 days. flipped one to public yesterday — recruiting signal we hadn't priced in.

@swyx The under-appreciated value of these writeups is the internal audit they force — half the payoff is the 'wait, why are we still using this?' moments that surface while drafting it. The marketing benefit is almost a side effect.
@swyx wrote one at my last company. legal removed everything that explained actual decisions. we published 3 paragraphs about uptime SLAs and nobody applied.
@swyx They nailed, am using it without any friction, sometimes I don't notice I am using it. Best software for devs is the one where devs don't feel anything

@swyx The recruiting angle is the most underrated payoff on this list. A SOTA tech writeup turns hiring from "post on LinkedIn and pray" into "the right engineer DMs you because they read it." Cheaper than any recruiter and the candidate pre-selects.
@swyx Totally agree. These writeups also serve as great internal documentation benchmarks and signal real architectural maturity to technical buyers.

@swyx B2B compliance vendors almost never publish their stack, and enterprise prospects notice the gap. When we published our KYC infrastructure writeup, three standard due diligence questions dropped off the first demo call. Transparency is sales collateral in this space.
@swyx devtools companies should write more of these because the product is usually just the visible scar tissue from 400 insane engineering decisions
@swyx fulfils multiple purposes but also gives competitors a blueprint

@swyx Stack writeups are underrated hiring funnels. Candidates who care about how a thing is built read these end to end. They're also the ones worth interviewing. Most companies write recruiting pages. Tech writeups signal you actually let engineers own decisions.
@swyx Tech stack writeups are the most efficient hiring funnel a devtool can run. People who finish reading already filter themselves in.

@swyx This resonates as a solo dev. Writing about tech stack decisions behind SnapSilo (SwiftUI + SwiftData + Vision for OCR) has been some of my most-engaged building-in-public content. Forces you to articulate tradeoffs you'd otherwise leave implicit.
@swyx Great writeup
@swyx Tasteful

thread.text: I can confirm that AI has made me busier Which forces me to automate more things in my life with AI I'm sure that's both a good and a bad thing.
@brycent AI makes you busier the same way good employees do. more leverage, more decisions, more broken processes suddenly visible.
@brycent jevon's paradox at its best

@brycent I guess, sometimes instead of thinking we just ask it to ai, so nowadays I take time, have patience for myself and think deeply about stuff. I love my thinking, I don't want it to vanish, I just started realising this so thought I might share
@brycent AI is the only productivity tool that makes you busier the more productive it makes you!
@brycent the tool that was supposed to free up time created enough new opportunities to fill all the time it freed
@brycent Now imagine having ADHD while using Ai
@brycent AI increasingly feels like a machine that converts ambition into additional tabs.
@brycent Induced demand. Roads get wider and traffic gets worse.
@brycent yeah that checks out, AI really does create more AI work
@brycent Same thing happened to me after automating parts of my workflow. I thought I’d get more free time, but instead I started running more projects because the bottleneck disappeared.
@brycent It’s only a bad thing if you are automating tasks that you actually enjoy doing.
@brycent @brycent AI seems to create a weird loop where every efficiency gain just reveals 10 more things you could optimize.
@brycent win some, lose some
@brycent AI is the perfect productivity tool

thread.text: Apparently an unpopular opinion, but I don't think Anthropic owes anyone heavily subsidized tokens for their third party app.
thread.quotedText: I can't help but feel personally burned by the Claude Code changes announced today. We put so much work into wrapping the (atrocious) Claude Agent SDK in T3 Code. It was the ONLY path they supported, so we made it work. It was hell. Now our users are getting their rate limits

thread.text: @Sentdex the deeper issue is building a company on somebody else's pricing fiction. if one policy update can rewrite your product math overnight, the dependency was already too deep.

@Sentdex Dishonest framing. There’s a ton of reasonable use cases that Claude Code users relied on that they made impossible. The confusion is insane. They framed a 40x decrease in usage limits like a new feature. How can you defend this? https://t.co/TLbKCw9cWF
thread.quotedText: I don't know what the fuss is about. Anthropic's rules on using subscriptions are very simple: Claude Code = OK Claude's online platform = OK Agent SDK running in personal software = OK... ish? Agent SDK running in commercial software = NOT OK Claude Code running in CI = ?? Oh,

thread.text: @Sentdex Owes - no. The story is more about giving something than taking it away after people built around it. After Jul 15th, it'll be how it usually was some time ago - plan when using an official app, everything else - API calls.
@Sentdex yeah surprised at the takes but also not? this is why u shouldnt give shit away for free 'cause then customers will riot when u take it away from them

@Sentdex they are free to do what they like, but they are framing it as a benefit through personal accounts which feels like a gaslighting campaign plus it's basically a rug pull in that it comes out of the blue and after people actively seeked out answers when it comes to what's allowed
@Sentdex Did you use T3 Code? It was a better experience than the Claude app and the Claude TUI. Not sure why you’re shooting yourself in the foot here.
@Sentdex Yeah but OpenAI codex gives them to you so… ima use their services

@Sentdex Unpopular opinion: Screw Anthropic. We all have our favorites and like to whisper around their glaring issues; but Anthropic needs to go. From the anthropomorphic fetishization of an LLM to the "let's just regulate everything, except for elites." *moat intensifies*

@Sentdex The issue is that they gate you into using their desktop app for any level of managed orchestration. The desktop app doesn't cost money, claude does. So why charge me more for wanting a different UX for the same claude functionality?

@Sentdex I mean bulding apps around this one thing, but you might need some of these features just to make your work faster. They could’ve just put daily/weekly limits on programmatic features just like models. Also some of these tools are just interfaces/control-planes for easier

@Sentdex Feel like the complaint here isn't that Anthropic places limits on subscription usage, and quite explicitly instead that the Anthropic team gave instructions on how to comply with policy that were expensive to implement & that were subsequently overturned without notice.

@Sentdex Reminds me the old complaints when Uber/Lyft stopped subsidizing rides & people that built general commutes to unproductive locations complained hard. Just because the faucets open doesn't mean the water will keep flowing, someone's still paying for the cost lmao 😂
@Sentdex Thank you! The amount of ownership so many folks on this platform feel of a completely private company giving them a service in return for $20 or $200, is just ridiculous.

@Sentdex Agree but they should not frame it as an upgrade and additional credit where in fact they are making the plan more expensive, they should just be honest about it and own it. Also no customer owes Anthropic any money, this is how consumers vote.

items.text: 'typescript is faster than rust' is a great way to force people to admit most perf cliffs are architecture stories, not language stories.

target.label: tyler_spacetimedb_typescript
target.text: Turns out SpacetimeDB could go faster. We found and fixed performance bugs. The TypeScript benchmark now runs at 303,920 ± 4,712 TPS. A ~200% improvement. TypeScript is now faster than Rust! Full write-up: https://t.co/7GeSGrbApA https://t.co/8ApuuLO4hn

thread.text: Back in March we benchmarked SpacetimeDB 2.0 at ~100,000 TPS and got called liars, morons, goons, and "not a very good database." Well, today we're responding to the criticism point by point, and you better believe I brought receipts. \1 🧵 https://t.co/c7Vtu5yLhg

Turns out SpacetimeDB could go faster. We found and fixed performance bugs. The TypeScript benchmark now runs at 303,920 ± 4,712 TPS. A ~200% improvement. TypeScript is now faster than Rust! Full write-up: https://t.co/7GeSGrbApA https://t.co/8ApuuLO4hn
"But it's just an in-memory database!" SpacetimeDB has full ACID guarantees actually stronger than Postgres, which defaults to READ COMMITTED isolation.
@TylerFCloutier 'typescript is faster than rust' is a great way to force people to admit most perf cliffs are architecture stories, not language stories.

@OpenAIDevs The hooks API is what makes Codex a platform instead of a tool. Pre/post validation that can scan, log, and create memories means you can build reliable agent pipelines on top of it. This is the right layer.
@OpenAIDevs This is crazy bro! I have been building this and I think we need to integrate ChatGPT or maybe some skills to for these Meta Quest AR/VR dev via our tool https://t.co/o5DR9ENOC5

thread.text: @OpenAIDevs This is where Codex starts to look less like a tool and more like infrastructure. Hooks and scoped tokens mean teams can wire agents into CI, policy, secrets checks and internal workflow instead of treating every run as a one-off.

@OpenAIDevs "scan prompts for secrets" is the feature I didn't know I needed until I realized I've been typing "please please please don't break production" into every prompt 😭 we've given the AI trust issues and honestly, same 🤝
@OpenAIDevs Hooks are huge because they turn agent behavior into policy instead of prompt folklore. Scoped tokens also make CI adoption way less scary for security teams.
@OpenAIDevs If I can make Codex run tests, check secrets, and remember repo rules at the right moments, the agent becomes easier to trust and easier to debug.

@OpenAIDevs Exactly what we needed for secure automations. Being able to create expirable tokens tied to workspaces and inject custom scripts at key points in the Codex loop is a game-changer for dev teams in India and globally. Shipping this into our internal pipelines ASAP.

@OpenAIDevs Validator-before-work hooks are the missing piece for production agent loops. Without them, agents commit work then you audit after. With them, work doesn't commit unless preconditions pass. This is what 'agent in production' actually requires. Rollback substrate moved upstream.
@OpenAIDevs Hooks that scan prompts for secrets before execution is the feature nobody talks about but everyone needs. Security-first agentic coding is finally happening

@OpenAIDevs Hooks are underrated. Running validators before/after tasks means you can enforce code standards automatically without reviewing every PR manually. This is how AI coding goes from “helpful” to actually production-ready. 🔥
@OpenAIDevs hooks are cool, but too many can slow things down. gotta keep it lean and effective.

@OpenAIDevs "scan prompts for secrets" doing the job that every dev should've been doing manually for years lol. hooks are actually underrated here — most people will sleep on this and just use codex as a fancy autocomplete
@OpenAIDevs Hooks make it real. You can finally put the agent into a proper pipeline instead of just chatting with it.

@OpenAIDevs are the hooks limited to stdin/stdout and files, or can they directly read/modify the agent’s internal memory and plan? In a large monorepo, auto-load directory-specific coding standards and security rules into the agent’s memory so it follows team conventions without repeated
@OpenAIDevs hooks are the real unlock the best agent setup wont be one model it will be a small system around the model that checks and nudges at the right moments
@OpenAIDevs Good for devops! What a dichotomy between ant and openai; one side is limiting even -p, and the other is making it easier. Interesting.
@OpenAIDevs this is what i actually needed not a smarter model just one i can finally control​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​🤝
@OpenAIDevs How are teams structuring their hooks right now? Especially for secrets scanning and repo memories? This changes the game for CI integration.

@OpenAIDevs Codex is becoming part of everything, even CI/CD. Now Codex can help debug when GitHub Actions or GitLab Runner fails. Usually, I use Codex with gh / GitHub CLI to debug runners locally. But now, can we automate this flow with Codex tokens?
@OpenAIDevs @dcbuilder Running validators mid-loop is exactly what we needed.
@OpenAIDevs At what point do AI agents basically become junior DevOps engineers? https://t.co/To0bEWDxiR
@OpenAIDevs Codex hooks for secrets scanning and logs? How about scanning your own priorities and logging the trust you’ve lost?#keep4o #OpenSource4o

@OpenAIDevs "Scan prompts for secrets before sending" is the line that gets Codex past the security review in every company that blocked it. One feature, unlocks thousands of enterprise accounts. That's how you scale B2B adption - not by making the product better, by removing the reason

@OpenAIDevs Tokens usually get added first because they unlock automation. Hooks come later because they feel like overhead. That order is backwards. Hooks stop automation from doing something stupid. Tokens just let it run faster. Enable CI before secret scanning is in place and the
@OpenAIDevs Any plans to allow native support for .claude , CLAUDE.md, etc? It would make it easier to migrate over to try out Codex
@OpenAIDevs hooks are great, but without deterministic tests you’re just automating vibes
@OpenAIDevs Codex is slowly turning from “AI coding assistant” into programmable infrastructure for engineering teams.

@OpenAIDevs Hooks plus scoped tokens is the unsexy update that actually unlocks enterprise adoption. The secret-scan hook alone solves a real compliance objection most teams couldn't get past last quarter.

@OpenAIDevs Got Agent Profiler running with hooks fast, and I’m loving the implementation. Using them for local-first AI coding-agent observability: Codex/Cursor hook traffic to a db to inspect session shape, token use, tool/shell noise, context weight, and efficiency, w/o remote telemetry. https://t.co/xXUcikayhg
@OpenAIDevs the funny part is we invented ai coworkers and immediately gave them security reviews
@OpenAIDevs As long as 'customization' doesn't mean a stealth API fee hike for retail, my portfolio welcomes the upgrade. Keep it fair.
@OpenAIDevs Can we get a hook for when a plan is accepted?
@OpenAIDevs Codex is quickly becoming more than a coding assistant hooks + scoped access tokens unlock serious workflow automation, CI integration, and enterprise-grade customization 🔥
@OpenAIDevs HELP PLZ https://t.co/bGCTeelMCC
@OpenAIDevs This is so important, the validators. Memories is a huge step.

@danshipper matches what I see. the second-order pattern: leaders who restart claude code daily never build the muscle. the ones with persistent multi-day chats compound faster because the agent already knows the org context. https://t.co/S1OhB4pwBf

@danshipper Same pattern in research teams. I've noticed people who run their own Claude Code sessions ask better AI-first research questions than those who delegate entirely to others. Hands-on usage changes what you think is tractable.

@danshipper People need to SEE the use cases. The hardest part about AI is that most people don't have the time to discover the use cases. This is why when Claude releases plugins that do admin, finance, or ops work it goes bananas because it puts the use cases in a nice pretty package

items.text: consumer AI gets judged like a toy. work AI gets judged by approvals, handoffs, and who gets blamed when it breaks.

target.label: nlw_work_technology
target.text: AI is a normal consumer technology and an extremely abnormal work technology.

thread.text: AI is a normal consumer technology and an extremely abnormal work technology.
@nlw consumer AI gets judged like a toy. work AI gets judged by approvals, handoffs, and who gets blamed when it breaks.

@nlw consumer ai normalizes to the same chat surface every other app uses. work ai has to survive tmux, tailscale, and ci runners where state persists across calls and auth is already solved. the moat isn't the model or the wrapper, it's who owns the runtime primitives. microsoft
@nlw Consumer AI can answer from the prompt. Work AI needs the stuff around the prompt: the email thread, the chat context, the meeting where someone already made the decision.
@nlw Consumer AI is a feature. Work AI is an operating model.
@nlw That's a really great way to put it and that paradigm is certainly playing out in the way the big labs are structuring their businesses.
@nlw The consumer side makes it feel safe. The work side makes it feel urgent. That tension is where most people are stuck right now.
@nlw Low stakes problems are always easier to solve
Listen man, the fact that you don’t think people’s Codex-built apps are very cool doesn’t make it a bubble.

items.text: AI hackathons are turning into loop-design contests. the edge is not the smartest agent, it's the shortest feedback cycle.

target.label: Peter Yang
target.text: How do people even do AI hackathons these days you're just sitting around waiting for the agents half of the time?

thread.text: How do people even do AI hackathons these days you're just sitting around waiting for the agents half of the time?
@petergyang AI hackathons are turning into loop-design contests. the edge is not the smartest agent, it's the shortest feedback cycle.
@petergyang Parallelmax with a parallelmaxxing harness https://t.co/wnCRL8Ozw8

@petergyang it's not about the agents, it's about the harness around the agents... and that's the challenge >>🤔designing the runtime, tool layer, MCPs, skills, context layer, evals loop, and feedback sys that constrain, observe, and improve the output of the agent... Not easy!😓
@petergyang I did not whip it hard enough for this image but I imagine it goes somewhat in this direction. https://t.co/CUiThLDlAN
@petergyang Git worktrees + worktrunk. I'll usually spin up 3 or 4 agents on different branches and let them run in parallel. Staring at a single terminal waiting for tokens is brutal.
@petergyang lmaooo the vibe is just everyone staring at logs praying it doesn't 429

@petergyang The wait is the point. The bottleneck shifted from writing code to designing the harness that keeps agents running without breaking. Runtime, tool access, coordination, fine tuning...that's the engineering challenge now.
@petergyang Run 10+ agents and you become the bottleneck, not the agents. https://t.co/MB8UsKqDc8
@petergyang Kinda like this you can have multiple sessions https://t.co/layTopovW6
@petergyang Hackathons turned into spectator sports. You're not coding, you're babysitting 20 parallel agents and praying none of them forget the prompt halfway through.
@petergyang the people winning are usually the ones who know exactly what to build and how to guide the agents there

@petergyang Actually quite nice, you have the opportunity to step away a little more. Also, running parallel agents to focus on the different aspects: the design, brand, the pitch, and the most important thing, not to over-crowd the product just because you can toss in a couple of extra

@petergyang Agent latency is the silent UX killer. Until streaming intermediate steps and parallel sub-agents are table stakes, demos will feel like dial-up. Built something similar — https://t.co/gLkuTFOo64
thread.quotedText: https://t.co/P9sgxNEnp2

thread.text: @petergyang Most of my working time is refining / iterating on .md documents to feed to the LLM. I wouldn’t be surprised if AI hackathons will soon focus on that
@petergyang We keep on building a side project while one runs
@petergyang Well I think is more of getting people that are still prompting in chatgpt website to experience something a bit more advanced.
@petergyang Hackathons will now include a Monopoly game component!
@petergyang Same issue with CTFs... Quite unclear what the path will be. However, I do believe cause of that, professional monitored certifications will be worth more as a proxy for skill proof.

@petergyang 3 screens. 4-6 terminals each on their own work tree. 4-6 text notes open to write notes for various work trees / answer questions from agents. Multiple tabs open to different vite server ports to review work. I cognitively max out at about 4 concurrent terminals.
@petergyang Now the kids can go out and socialize vs stay up all night at shack15
@petergyang The real edge in AI hackathons isn't the smartest agent, it's who built the tightest loop to monitor, debug and recover without losing context
@petergyang You run 5 agents at the same time. LIke juggling 5 plates at once... no waiting then lol
@petergyang If they are sitting waiting for an agent, their workflow needs looking at - have two or three agents, or do something in the time the agent is working. #solutionfocusedalltheway

@petergyang This is, ironically maybe, my greatest gripe with AI-assisted development. I get so distracted when the agent is working that I lose focus on what I was working on / feel like I'm trapped in a malaise of sleepwalking at work.

@petergyang Hackathons stopped being about coding when the agent does the coding. Last one I queued 4 agents on different sub-features of EDSO and was the only one not pacing. The skill is task slicing now, not typing speed.
@petergyang We just had one of these. We prompted then went to lunch.
@petergyang That's why people have 10+ terminals running to pretend they are not sitting and waiting.
@petergyang Participated on a Claude Hackathon 3weeks ago and yes, it’s just waiting halo of the time but you still need to figure out the idea + architecture yourself 😅
@petergyang I’m going to do a lot of physical work (printing, scanning, etc) that requires hands on building while my agents do their work
@petergyang It's starting to be waiting for agents 100% of the time with /goal lol

@petergyang Shift for me: I stopped chatting with agents to do the work. I chat to understand the work, review results, make decisions. They build. The bottleneck flipped. Now they wait for me. I have multiple persistent agents in tmux. Karpathy (engineering), Fishkin (marketing), WillIAm
@petergyang The hackathon is now just 6 hours of writing better prompts and pretending you're "architecting"
@petergyang we sit around drinking our 10th coffees and playing on our phone and talking every once in a while. then checking the agents rinse repeat
@petergyang Competent folks have multiple agents running in a loop round robin’ing through them
@petergyang Companies should at very least sponsor enough tokens for hackathons u can spam /fast xD
@petergyang the new hackathon meta is bringing a second laptop. one for the agent to chug on, one for you to actually think

items.text: A very practical launch tip 🚀 Before you submit your product anywhere, make a doc with: 1. One-liner (60 characters) 2. Short description (100-200 characters) 3. Long description (1000-2000 characters) 4. 3-5 key features 5. Founder story (500-1500 characters) 6.
demo polish matters, but most founder videos fail on narrative, not transitions. smooth motion can't save a confused workflow.

@alex_barashkov Is the skill mostly taste rules, or does it also stop the agent from inventing its own tiny Las Vegas?

@alex_barashkov library-agnostic is the interesting bit. does it emit one spec and let the agent translate, or are there per-library quirks baked in? gsap and motion handle stagger pretty differently

@alex_barashkov This is beautiful! I am going to test this out rn and lyk how it is in my tweets :)

@alex_barashkov timing curves and easing specs are what make animation feel intentional, most people skip that part. smart to wrap it as a skill.

@alex_barashkov Wrapping animation specs as a composable skill is the right abstraction — now the agent understands timing intent, not just pixel values. The library-agnostic design is smart: keep the skill stable while the renderer swaps out. This is how agent capabilities should be built.

@alex_barashkov This is the missing layer most agent builders ignore. Not the model, not the framework — the skills layer. Giving agents granular, composable abilities like this changes what they can actually ship. Bookmarked.

thread.quotedText: @GergelyOrosz Same. I do a ton of upfront planning before I have AI generate any code. I also use AI as a tool during that planning to help show me clear example and layout tradeoffs, also to show alternative options. Rather than slop, it’s a huge quality boost when you use it right.

thread.text: @GergelyOrosz yep. AI is a force multiplier, not a substitute for taste. the second you outsource the hard part, you also outsource the learning.

@GergelyOrosz don't outsource everything is the rare honest take from a heavy user. running 5 agents on max, my best work still comes from the 20% i refuse to delegate. delegation is a discipline, not a feature.

@GergelyOrosz agree, and worth saying: the people producing slop aren't being lazy. they're being rewarded for it. the issue isn't individual discipline, it's that orgs are measuring velocity at AI timescales and quality at human ones.

@GergelyOrosz 100% this. AI works best when you stay in the driver's seat. That's why I built PromptCentral — not to replace your thinking, but to organise the prompts that sharpen it. → Free to use → Discover, save & organise AI prompts → Built for everyone https://t.co/LzRxDF1Buo

@GergelyOrosz the moments where you push through the difficulty yourself are exactly where the intuition gets built, the AI can produce the output but it cannot transfer the understanding that comes from struggling with the problem, those are two different things and only one compounds

@GergelyOrosz the slop work observation is the most underrated insight here every professional with a trained eye can now spot ai output in 3 seconds linkedin posts, marketing emails, blog posts, internal docs, they all share the same beige cadence the people delegating the thinking step are

@GergelyOrosz I think AI is a multiplier of quality and effort we put into work, I have a feeling that even SOTA models are often derailing and I have to keep them on a short leash in order to be satisfied with quality of the work

@GergelyOrosz I can’t go without handholding AI every few minutes. And I spend 2 days going deep into the problem and solution before I let AI take over the execution which probably takes only a couple of hours but the 2 day deep work really does pay off.

@GergelyOrosz ai gets you to 80% on most cognitive tasks within minutes the last 20% is where the actual value lives that 20% requires the exact thinking you describe people who learn to do it well will compound their leverage for years people who ship the 80% and call it done will be visible

@GergelyOrosz Learning coding right now. Rule I set myself: I write the code first, get stuck, then ask AI to explain the concept — not fix it for me. The moment I let it write the solution, I retain nothing.

@GergelyOrosz Things like deciding the right data structure for a complicated problem... not even close. But things like fixing a flaky test by tracing the code path... that sucks and takes forever, the LLM is super helpful.
@GergelyOrosz Most AI-written output has a lot of words but very little *substance* which is why human judgment and oversight remain essential

@GergelyOrosz This is the divide I see in candidates too. The ones who use AI as a force multiplier on hard work keep getting sharper. The ones who outsource the thinking part atrophy fast — and it shows in 30 minutes of an interview.

@GergelyOrosz There's a term for what you're describing: productive struggle. The difficulty is the point. When you wrestle with a hard problem, you build the mental model that makes you better at the next one. When AI does it, you just have a solution and nothing transferable. The output

@GergelyOrosz I miss the edge cases or some important part every single time when I give it a very vague or broad prompt. So I know ponder over my task for a while then write spec-->refine spec, until I'm satisfied. Only then I go to AI for it. Works well in most part.

@GergelyOrosz I've noticed the same pattern — AI as a research assistant or code, sketch tool works, but handing it the whole problem usually produces something that compiles and solves nothing. The real work is still defining the constraint properly, knowing which 80% of the generated code to
@GergelyOrosz I think the sweet spot is using AI to reduce friction , without removing yourself from thinking process completely. the hard part is usually where real understanding gets built.

@GergelyOrosz Same. I do a ton of upfront planning before I have AI generate any code. I also use AI as a tool during that planning to help show me clear example and layout tradeoffs, also to show alternative options. Rather than slop, it’s a huge quality boost when you use it right.
@GergelyOrosz AI for the output but use your brain for the judgment. The moment you'd need to verify it line by line, the abstraction leaked and you're just a slow reviewer of someone else's work.

@GergelyOrosz The gap between people who use AI to amplify their own thinking and people who replaced their thinking with AI is already visible. Two messages in and you can tell. The first group is going to be scary good in 3 years.

@GergelyOrosz This maps perfectly to HPC. We spent a decade learning that job schedulers don't replace understanding — they amplify it. The researchers who know WHY their Slurm job failed are the ones who ship. The ones who blindly trust sbatch submit garbage for weeks.

@GergelyOrosz I’m seeing a very real and strong correlation to amount of AI generated code and increases in Cyclomatic Complexity, Duplication and other measures. Beginning to utterly terrify me. While I love using AI, it’s handy; a reckoning is definitely coming.

@GergelyOrosz agree. i have completely given up on tasking the agent with writing big plans ahead of work, and now work with tiny todo markdown notes that the agent creates on my request as i know the problem better. agile > waterfall
@GergelyOrosz llms are good at template based coding whenever you need surgical precision it is just better to do it yourself you learn more and get a better product at the end

@GergelyOrosz Outsourcing to AI removes you from the equation, and the whole point of doing something is to do it to a level that satisfies your expectations and what you think is important. Asking for summaries or research is fine, but keep your judgment at the core.
@GergelyOrosz how do you decide which subtasks are worth keeping manual vs handing to ai?
@GergelyOrosz the learning happens when you stay in the driver seat. outsource the thinking and you just end up with vibes and no muscle 😅
@GergelyOrosz I think people who just accept whatever AI output gives them the first .... are people who have not developed any taste or do not know what high-quality output looks like.
@GergelyOrosz a lot of the learning happens during the frustrating parts people are now trying to skip lol

@GergelyOrosz For me, a lot of planning goes into the project, that I am looking to build with AI Assistance. I know with experience, that you cannot just start building from Day 1. You prepare, a lot of different directions, so that harness around the project for AI is good.
@GergelyOrosz yes! was just posting a discussion about that. i'm very curious how organizations tackle this though as it does require a lot of mental fortitude. https://t.co/I4Rt3dY44J
thread.quotedText: true. the problem is that one goes against the other in a REALLY hard-hard way. human understanding and learning comes from doing, the doing part is hard and not always fun - so agents feels amazing there. but they rob you from the learning. i haven't seen a good discussion

thread.text: @GergelyOrosz in most cases, it should be thought of as an execution layer for a problem you have already solved in your head however, for some jobs with 'goals' clearly defined as a function, the 'handover' works just fine

@GergelyOrosz I find that I am most valuable on the edges. On the one hand I need to be in control on the big picture: the architecture, design, structure. On the other hand I need to desicde on the specifics in critical areas. When those are set correctly most other falls right.
@GergelyOrosz Spot on. Been building with AI and the tool's sharp but the thinking stays mine. Hand over the hard bit and I end up redoing it later. Every time.

@GergelyOrosz The more I use AI, the less I trust it with things outside of my knowledge. I can use it because I know what I want and I'm able to find the issues and fix them. When I'm in unfamiliar waters, I'm always thinking about the errors I'm not able to see due to my ignorance.

@dabit3 hooks killed our biggest agent failure class faster than system prompt edits ever did. paperclip pre-commit hook blocks half-broken files. prompt begged for 3 weeks, hook fixed it in one afternoon. determinism beats persuasion when failure cost is git history.
@dabit3 hooks give you deterministic behavior prompts give you something the model has to remember one is a guarantee. the other is a hope

@gregisenberg My agents read the entire AI community here and make this: https://t.co/kiuZ7QXLzb My agents get smarter every day because of this
@gregisenberg This one on Hermes agents building their own memory after tasks really stands out. The compounding advantage from starting now hits hard. https://t.co/jil20KQH4b
@gregisenberg The memory compounding is underrated. The agent running for 6 months knows your edge cases. The one you start tomorrow doesn't. That's the actual moat.

@gregisenberg The memory observation is the one that will matter most. An agent that builds context on your specific processes over 6 months outperforms a generic agent by a factor that's hard to explain until you've seen it. First-mover advantage here is real.

thread.text: @danshipper being the layer between users and a fast-moving agent runtime is brutal. every upstream regression gets rebranded as your product bug.
@danshipper since switching to hermes, things have gotten a lot smoother... maintaining @AtlasForgeAI is no longer a full-time job

@danshipper "One super agent for a company beats 1-1 agents for everyone. I do think we're going to get there over time, but for now agents actually require a lot of work (often technical) to keep working well." do you mean to say that right now, one super agent for a company works better
@danshipper I can feel point 1 a lot. Building on a fast-moving agent layer is fun right until every upstream regression becomes your product problem.
@danshipper curious how much of the pain was API churn vs behavioral regressions in the harness itself
@danshipper regressions in fast-moving ai infra always hit the platform layer hardest maybe centralized agents are just accepting the pain upfront
@danshipper relieved even you faced these hurdles, from structural downsides to minor oddities like the yawning (wtf?!) thanks for sharing!

@danshipper Interesting takeaway from building on OpenClaw: powerful, but fast-moving infra can be painful as a platform layer. Also feels increasingly that one well-maintained company agent beats dozens of fragile personal. QuickClaw helps make that reliability layer easier to manage 24/7
@danshipper I no longer trust most open-source agent harnesses, MCPs, or skills. Gotta build it from scratch and spend the time to dial it in if you want something robust + efficient

@danshipper I like the idea of an agent and memory storage assistant layer that manages sub-agent processes. It remembers who I am, my goals the agents I like to use. Farming out my tasks, like cursor for code or a custom-built agent for flights.

@danshipper Yep, multi-player is the way to go! I don't know how OpenClaw gets the attention it does given its reliability. You gotta babysit it everyday. We built our new product on another micro-harness. Excited to read more on Every!

@danshipper Just read the article, thank you for sharing the learnings. I think one of the biggest challenge for agentic/agent adoption is a consistent and scalable memory layer. md files are fine for one or two, but we need a memory layer that team can contribute to

@danshipper Makes sense. I love what @get_viktor_com shipped. But also expensive af and lots burned on useleses heartbeats. Openclaw is so janky. No dice using this in prod. I like Hermes. Just works.

@danshipper Companies keep defaulting to "one brain that does everything" because it mirrors how they think about hiring a person. Organizations don't run on one person, they run on coordination between specialists. The org chart exists for a reason.
@danshipper A Super Agent wrapper on top of many smaller and different AI agents could be a cool idea. Provided that super agent seems as easy as chatting for non-technical people.
@danshipper No worries Dan! As Peter promised! LTS is coming sooooooonnnn
@danshipper I love the look of your website ❤️. Also, yes it’s not that easy to build agents, that work well and consistently

@danshipper the 1-to-1 agent model breaks because context is the moat, and context lives at the company, not the user. one shared agent that knows the company's data beats ten agents that each re-learn it. the unit of value is the org, not the seat.

@danshipper running paperclip on openclaw 4 months. layer-between pain is real, every minor regression burns 2-4h harness debug. we never sold agents, agents ship our code instead. harness is the moat. selling-agents = fighting two upstreams at once.
@danshipper I’m building something along these lines, but specific use case. Seems to be working well in testing… stay tuned
@danshipper the speed-stability tradeoff is brutal for dev tools. centralized agents might be the org hack until platforms mature

@danshipper The 'one super agent for a company' conclusion makes sense for maintenance, but it recreates B2B SaaS — one team manages it for everyone. I'd bet the 1-1 personalization story returns once individual agent maintenance cost drops by an order of magnitude.

@danshipper This is the right failure mode to name: platformizing agents needs stable contracts more than demos—version pinning, migration notes, permission boundaries, replayable failure bundles, and a deprecation window. Which hurt most: regressions, auth/permissions, or tool API churn?
@danshipper open source hustle is real but stability wins every time

@gregisenberg good skill demos are easy. the real test is whether the skill still works after the model, tools, and docs shift a week later. building https://t.co/wAXM9FxxVe for that layer.
@gregisenberg 42 seconds is a tight window to explain something complex

@gregisenberg The skill format solves the biggest problem with AI tools in teams: knowledge that lives in one person's head and never transfers. When you codify a skill properly, you've made the expertise portable. That's what makes it compound.
@gregisenberg The 42 second demo only matters if the skill removes repeated decision cost. Otherwise the table just gained another button.

@gregisenberg The 42 second version skips the part that matters. A skill is only as good as its trigger description. I rewrote one description 6 times. Same logic inside, 6x better hit rate just from how I phrased WHEN to use it. The body is easy. The routing is the real work.

@gregisenberg Skills have many issues. The main is that AI generally fails at finding the shortest path to the optimal solution. In my experience, it's better to have multiple smaller promptss rather than big skills full of "just in case" instructions

@gregisenberg Skills win over prompts because they compose. Replaced an 800-word ad-ops prompt with 4 chained skills last month, output quality jumped ~30% and onboarding new ops people went from days to an hour. Stack matters.
@gregisenberg 42 seconds is suspiciously efficient for anything claude related, respect the commitment
@gregisenberg few skills i made that genuinely made my vibe coding life way easier: https://t.co/STxWd4GBRB putting them out there for everyone 🫶

@gregisenberg Most skills shipping right now are wrapped prompts with file outputs. The good ones encode an opinionated workflow the user couldn't articulate as a single prompt. That's the moat — not the template.

@gregisenberg This is the one I'd add: descriptions matter more than anything else. I keep skill names short and spell out exactly when the agent should grab it in the description. Saved me from constant wrong picks once I got past three or four skills.
@gregisenberg Biggest unlock for us: treat each skill like a written SOP, not a prompt. If a new hire couldn't follow the steps, the skill won't either.
@gregisenberg I need to start running skills on my Claude. I keep putting it off, because having to learn it makes me feel uncomfortable. The sooner their better though

@gregisenberg @startupideaspod This is great! Additionally, you can have Claude record an activity-log (more details than memory), use hooks to update. Then do the workflow manually. It automatically captures learnings in the log, across session. Great to identifying and build skills
@gregisenberg the "ask AI to review what it just did and turn that into a skill" step is the unlock.
@gregisenberg the people who learn how to give Claude memory and context are going to look like wizards to everyone still typing one-off prompts

@gregisenberg Really good Claude Skills are procedures, not prompts. The best ones turn tribal workflow into a small, versioned, auditable operating manual. Claude can already “think”; the Skill should tell it when to act, what tools to use, what order to follow, and what must never happen

@gregisenberg Claude skills are most useful when they stop being prompt packs and become tiny operating manuals: inputs, boundaries, examples, failure modes, and what "done" means. The underrated bit is taste transfer, not syntax.

@gregisenberg the key most people miss: skills work best when they're narrow and opinionated. a skill that tries to do everything becomes a skill that's good at nothing. write it like you're briefing a smart contractor who just walked in — context, constraints, and exactly what done looks like

@gregisenberg Writing a skill takes 20 minutes. Keeping 40+ skills from conflicting with each other across multiple agents running 24/7 is where projects quietly die. Skill orchestration, failure recovery, and preventing context drift over months is 90% of the work nobody demos.

@gregisenberg The 42 seconds part is the whole point. When the friction to create a skill is that low, you stop asking "should I automate this" and just do it. That shift alone changes how fast you move as a builder.

@gregisenberg On my side the skills folder loaded fine across five domains. The part that broke first was the message queue between per-domain agents, because dropped state between handoffs is where most of the debug time landed. Skills give you the library, scope discipline ships it.

@gregisenberg Teams shipping fastest right now use Claude for scaffolding, not creative direction. The humans still have to decide what it should feel like — that judgment layer is where most founders underinvest and where the biggest gaps open up.

@gregisenberg 42 seconds to explain Claude skills clearly is exactly the format these topics need. Most technical content goes way too long. Compression is the underrated skill in the AI content space.
@gregisenberg The skills file is the unlock most people miss. Once you realize you're training Claude's default persona, not writing a one-off prompt, the whole thing clicks differently.

@gregisenberg We ended up versioning skills as small prompt + tool bundles rather than monolithic instructions. That let us swap one skill without touching the rest of the agent and dropped prompt token cost about 25 percent on repeated calls.

@gregisenberg the honest answer is that most people don't actually understand what's going on with claude, they're just repeating what others say about it. clarity doesn't always come from explanations, sometimes it comes from building something yourself and seeing why existing tools aren
@gregisenberg the “purpose” node is the one most people skip. a skill without a clear job to do is just noise in the context windo

@gregisenberg Doing the same for ad creative. We track every winning Meta ad in our DB. Pair the swipe file with a Claude skill and you ship more tests per week than your agency does in a quarter. https://t.co/J6D60hPtmS
@gregisenberg https://t.co/nMZ4VjTjda

@jxnlco telling it to remember usually fixes the next reply and quietly forgets by the new session
@jxnlco it's a good point. just keep writing errors into agents.md, it will get much more effective.

@jxnlco Agent tooling works best when you treat it like collaboration, not magic. Clear instructions, fast iteration, and explicit correction loops usually outperform vague prompting, and when something fails repeatedly, improving the constraints or context is often more effective than
@jxnlco The funny part is this is becoming a real prompting pattern: ask, inspect the failure mode, then tighten the operating constraints instead of rewording randomly. Works for humans too tbh.

thread.text: We should federally tax Tokens at the Provider level. Not a lot. Less than 50c per million tokens. It will accomplish 4 things (at least ) 1. It will push the big AI players to optimize tokenization, caching , routing and localization Which will 2. Reduce energy

@mcuban taxing tokens fixes a downstream symptom. the real waste is agents burning tokens on bad skills, bad routing, and zero observability. better tooling gets you there faster than a tax ever will.
@mcuban Yes take money from some of the most innovative and productive organizations in human history and give it to the federal bureaucracy.

@mcuban There are a lot of things we can tax. Taxing intelligence when we are in one of the most important global technology races ever would be a tremendous mistake Doesn't even factor in the fact a tremendous % of our tax revenue is wasted
@mcuban If anything has to be taxed it should be the agents, 1 person 1 agent Agent must make its own $

@mcuban There are already massive economic incentives to optimize, so this is just a tax on American companies that makes foreign models and products more attractive. Along with creating the infrastructure for government to track all AI usage and punish anyone who doesn't report.

@mcuban 1. A free market is all the incentive they need to optimize 2. They're already incentivized to reduce energy cost 3. More money to the govt is bad 4. The govt will never use additional revenue to pay down debt - they'll spend it
@mcuban Why not tax Internet packets and air? Tokens can be generated anywhere, in any country, in space, in my house or on my phone. The side effects of this would be massive and not what you expect
@mcuban Honest question: how do u stop companies from routing inference offshore to dodge the tax?
@mcuban Won’t providers just pass along the cost to consumers and we’ll still have the same problem this is trying to fix?

@mcuban -The premise that tokens equate to energy use is wrong. Some providers have more efficient models or data centers. -The premise that companies aren't already optimizing given GPU limits and costs is wrong. Anthropic just leased Colossus 1 from xAI, a competitor. Moreover, you
@mcuban You think a federal tax is going to save them more in energy than it costs them because it is the government encouraging them to be efficient?
@mcuban High-level impractical. A provider-level tax just gets passed down the stack, ultimately hitting the same retail and enterprise users, while stifling the very efficiency R&D you're targeting.
@mcuban Would you tax local models?

thread.text: I know there's been a lot of investment in marketing AI. But curious which growth marketing agents are truly impactful? Something that all startups should start using as soon as there are some initial signals of PMF.

@nakul most growth agents are still toy wrappers around your analytics. the ones that matter own the full loop: hypothesis, launch, readback, then they improve from feedback. that's basically what i'm building with https://t.co/wAXM9FxxVe

@nakul Most early growth agents over-index on output. The useful ones after PMF look more like a growth OS: capture market/customer signals, turn them into hypotheses, generate variants, read results, and recommend the next test. The value is faster learning, not more content.
@nakul We’re seeing it! https://t.co/qRutwZ9qWg
@nakul Agree-start with growth tools that prove impact quickly after PMF

items.text: yep. human auth assumes a click. agent auth needs delegation, scope, and auditability by default or the whole thing turns into chaos fast.

target.label: ideabrowser
target.text: This trend is keeping me up at night. Auth. The boring plumbing that decides who is allowed to do what. $ 20B+ industry (and growing) Okta is worth $13B and they built all of it for humans clicking allow buttons. What about Auth for Agents? Agents don't click. They run in https://t.co/AyQ175RJKa

thread.text: This trend is keeping me up at night. Auth. The boring plumbing that decides who is allowed to do what. $ 20B+ industry (and growing) Okta is worth $13B and they built all of it for humans clicking allow buttons. What about Auth for Agents? Agents don't click. They run in https://t.co/AyQ175RJKa
@ideabrowser yep. human auth assumes a click. agent auth needs delegation, scope, and auditability by default or the whole thing turns into chaos fast.
@ideabrowser how would revocation work if an agent starts touching new resources mid-loop without waking anyone?
@ideabrowser OAuth for agents is just API keys with extra steps.

@ideabrowser Auth is table stakes. The real problem is scoped permissions that shift with context. An agent reviewing docs at 3am needs different access than one answering Slack. Constraint files per agent per domain. Centralized auth assumes static roles. Agents don't have static roles.
@ideabrowser eh, needs auth that grants loop scoped keys agents can use while sleeping without reclicking
@ideabrowser That’s why google will have a big advantage with all it’s integrations
@ideabrowser Agents running loops will hit auth checks thousands of times per task, so any layer needs sub-second verification without human review.
@ideabrowser https://t.co/V6IjrIx4Tn

thread.text: I've watched every YC batch for years. Something shifted. Founders are moving faster than ever. Not slightly faster. Categorically faster. AI removed the bottleneck. What took 3 months now takes 3 weeks. So the goals got bigger. The pressure cooker got more intense. And
@GJarrosson ai didn't remove pressure, it removed excuses. now the bottleneck is taste, distribution, and deciding what actually deserves to exist.

@GJarrosson I thought that what shifted is the agism bias of YC 😅 Look at how young the latest cohorts have been. Oh, and how there is it seems a deep belief that you can build complete products with AI slop. It’s one thing to build a shitty mock up in one day with AI but then a full

items.text: this is underrated. half the so-called agent benchmarks dodge the environments where real workflows actually break.

target.label: ivanburazin
target.text: Docker in Docker is something almost no sandbox provider supports. For RL workloads specifically, being able to spin up a Docker Compose or a K3S cluster inside a sandbox unlocks an enormous range of workflows that simply don't work anywhere else. That alone has been a

thread.text: Docker in Docker is something almost no sandbox provider supports. For RL workloads specifically, being able to spin up a Docker Compose or a K3S cluster inside a sandbox unlocks an enormous range of workflows that simply don't work anywhere else. That alone has been a
@ivanburazin this is underrated. half the so-called agent benchmarks dodge the environments where real workflows actually break.
@ivanburazin yes totally. using daytona for our runs. it just… works. amazing work that you did!
@ivanburazin So while we were waiting for Daytona - we ran nested virtualization workloads on AWS. Funnily enough AWS has supported nested virtualization for the past 2 months.
@ivanburazin Honestly this is the most impressive thing I’ve heard in a while. DIND is such a nightmare
@ivanburazin Does Daytona support it? I tried it sometime back and it wasn't supported and it seemed to me it was supported but via DIND

@ivanburazin the k3s inside a sandbox detail is the part that compounds. once you can spin up a full cluster in an ephemeral environment the range of RL experiments you can run changes completely. multi-agent training, distributed rollouts, environment parallelization. none of that works

@ivanburazin So true. Docker in docker is such a basic requirement for any reasonable autonomous coding agent but the ecosystem is very poor at this moment. We are working our way out by having our agents do code build verify within the container but deploy to remote cluster for runtime

@ivanburazin hmm, right, i also want those containers to talk, a protocol for them to safely communicate without contaminating each other's state. idk if your platform does it, i think writing a secure protocol is both tough and tedious, lol
@ivanburazin I’m using DinD for GitHub Actions on Cloudflare. Not sure if your usecase requires root though… https://t.co/sXK7hKcj9M
@ivanburazin Doesn’t really work on Daytona. Works great on runloop
@ivanburazin Why is docker in docker hard?
@ivanburazin very cool!
@ivanburazin nested infra support is where sandboxes stop being “run this command safely” and become actual world simulators for agents. docker compose is often the real task.

items.text: infinite memory is only step one. the useful part is deciding which old context still matters instead of dumping the whole attic back into the prompt.

target.label: dr_cintas
target.text: You can now give Hermes, Claude Code, and Codex infinite memory 🤯 It's called agentmemory. Records what your agent does during every session. Compresses it with AI. Injects the right context back when the next session starts. 4,000+ GitHub stars. Free. https://t.co/ta8cV2P2gA

thread.text: You can now give Hermes, Claude Code, and Codex infinite memory 🤯 It's called agentmemory. Records what your agent does during every session. Compresses it with AI. Injects the right context back when the next session starts. 4,000+ GitHub stars. Free. https://t.co/ta8cV2P2gA

Link: https://t.co/KnFQvRWpN1 And if you want to get my full free Claude course: 1. Go to https://t.co/kCad4bQXyg 2. Subscribe free by just writing your email. 3. Open my welcome email and get the free resources.
@dr_cintas infinite memory is only step one. the useful part is deciding which old context still matters instead of dumping the whole attic back into the prompt.
@dr_cintas Thanks for sharing, just installed for my agent! Appreciate it!

@dr_cintas The sleep consolidation is underrated. Mnemosyne compresses old memories into summaries so your agent gets smarter over time instead of drowning in context. Been running it for a month, one of the best Hermes plugins. https://t.co/TmrK117xyk

@dr_cintas This isn’t infinite memory. It is context passing. It is very cool, and I am looking into it, but it is not a memory system. This is more of a cold boot (load.context) script that injects a prompt to front load the next agent or model action with purpose/mission elements.

@dr_cintas that's the missing piece. claude code running in the dark without memory of what it just built is like running intervals without a training log. agentmemory turns a one-off coding session into actual progress you can build on. gonna check this out.

@dr_cintas compression is the easy half, invalidation is the part that bites. our triage agent kept pulling stale context from a summary for hours after we'd already fixed the underlying issue, and pinning the summary to a version hash before deploys was the only thing that helped
@dr_cintas Persistent memory is useful, but the hard part is not storing every session...it is retrieval, compression, and invalidation so stale context does not become future state.
@dr_cintas the context window was the last real limitation holding agents back from feeling like they actually know you this closes that gap 4000 stars and free is a dangerous combination

@dr_cintas The deeper I go with agents, the more I realize the hard part isn’t just prompting !! It’s memory, context, rate limits, model choice, pricing, quotas, providers… A lot of invisible plumbing. That’s the layer I’m trying to map with DealsForge.
@dr_cintas "infinite memory" is rag with extra steps and a generous changelog. 4k stars measures novelty appetite, not whether the right context gets surfaced twice in a row.
@dr_cintas the injection timing is where they all die. session 1 gets clean context, session 7 gets a hallucination casserole.

@dr_cintas This is so real. The memory system is what made Hermes click for me. Mnemosyne adds vector search and entity extraction on top of Hermes memory, self-hosted, persists across sessions. Been running it for weeks, it just works. https://t.co/j5g3ountDh

@dr_cintas @grok how does this compare to claude-mem by thedotmac or solutions that involve obsidian? They seem to be many different solutions on offer right now, and I am keen for some analysis and thoughts on which might be best suited for someone with a couple of different businesses.
@dr_cintas infinite memory sounds like a billable hour nightmare waiting to happen. why trust an agent with more context than it has sense?
@dr_cintas That’s a cool memory agent. I liked the concept.
@dr_cintas absolute cinema Alvaro My AI knowledge has expanded

@dr_cintas The context loss between sessions is honestly one of the most underrated pain points in agentic workflows. You spend 20 minutes getting the agent up to speed, it does great work, then next session you're back to square one. Compressing + injecting selectively is the right call —

@dr_cintas The agentmemory approach of compressing session data and injecting relevant context is a solid step toward solving the memory accumulation problem. How does it handle context conflicts from older sessions?
@dr_cintas The context window problem was always the biggest bottleneck for long running agent workflows. Compressing and injecting session history is a smart solve. Checking this out.
@dr_cintas The underrated part is not just remembering everything, it is remembering the right slice at the right moment. Otherwise infinite memory turns into infinite noise fast.

@dr_cintas the obsidian route gives you full control but you're doing all the curation manually. claude-mem and similar plugins automate capture but skip structure. the question is whether you need retrieval or just storage.

@dr_cintas The sleep consolidation is underrated. Mnemosyne compresses old memories into summaries so your agent gets smarter over time instead of drowning in context. Been running it for a month, one of the best Hermes plugins. https://t.co/TmrK117xyk
@dr_cintas That’s pretty wild, I didn't know you could extend context windows like that!
@dr_cintas This looks like a real solution to one of the biggest frustrations with agents. The session recording plus AI compression and targeted injection is clever.
@dr_cintas too much memory aint good, or am i wrong
@dr_cintas The AI compression before injecting context back is the smart part, just storing raw logs would be useless at scale
@dr_cintas https://t.co/nMZ4VjTjda
thread.quotedText: https://t.co/0rA5jg9Tnp

thread.text: @dr_cintas yes this is useful , i'm using it now

@dr_cintas Totalmente de acuerdo. La memoria infinita, o al menos persistente y comprimida, es lo que hace operativos a los agentes en proyectos reales. Sin eso, la repetición constante de contexto mata cualquier flujo de trabajo serio.

@dr_cintas Persistent memory solves context — next gap is what happens when the LLM call fails mid-session: 429, 529, timeout. Does agentmemory recover state cleanly, or lose the session? That retry/fallback layer is tricky. Wrote patterns for it: https://t.co/ZuSP0Awt8w

@dr_cintas Esto es exactamente lo que me paso. Estuve meses buscando una solucion de memoria para agentes que no dependa de la nube. Al final construi Mnemosyne - plugin nativo para Hermes, auto-hospedado, busqueda hibrida. https://t.co/j5g3ountDh
@dr_cintas agents just got a serious brain upgrade

items.text: user pain probably sits above /goal. goals are cheap now. picking the right goal before the agent burns a day on the wrong one is the harder abstraction.

target.label: andrewchen
target.text: Now that we have: - tokens - prompts that generate tokens - /goal which can generate prompts What’s the next level of abstraction that generates /goals?

thread.text: Now that we have: - tokens - prompts that generate tokens - /goal which can generate prompts What’s the next level of abstraction that generates /goals?
@andrewchen user pain probably sits above /goal. goals are cheap now. picking the right goal before the agent burns a day on the wrong one is the harder abstraction.

@andrewchen intent. describe what you want in the world, not what the system should do. goals are still you making the plan. intent is "users churn less" and the system decomposes that into goals, prompts, tokens on its own
@andrewchen the hard part isn't generating goals — it's generating goals that survive contact with reality. the next abstraction is probably taste: what makes one goal better than another in context.

@andrewchen I call it Automations: event & time triggered tasks that read state and infer sensible goals. Downstream of that: a system handling LLM capacity planning and goal queuing. I’ve had this running on my VPC for a few months. Built my own /goal system originally but use the off the

@andrewchen Maximise human health, wealth and happiness. These will lead to goals that will enable Zero person Billion dollar companies for freeing up humanity to explore beyond galaxy. Ian M Banks' culture series will play out verbatim right in front of our eyes.

@andrewchen I keep thinking the next abstraction is not another text layer. It is a scene of intent: what you are looking at, what changed, what you tried, what good looks like. Goals are often visual before they become sentences.
@andrewchen /ideas - generate next set of goals to be done

@andrewchen In terms for us, we're working on that now - The next abstraction after goals may be trusted context: who contributes to the goal, what constraints they see, and how the system turns that into an actionable brief.

@andrewchen /purpose /goal specifies straightforward, measurable outcomes. /purpose establishes direction and intent for a system of agents, allowing it to autonomously generate purpose-driven goals that are reliably completed with high confidence.
@andrewchen We already have it vision Once it takes vision from us, then it’s universal basic income time

@andrewchen always been there but was on such an obvious meta level that everyone overlooked it - thinking. being able to use wisprflow/ voiceos/ any voice transcription tool to just get structured thoughts out of your head and let AI understand your reasoning when you’re formulating a

@andrewchen A system budget, deadline, owner, and failure metric turn the goal layer into a compiler from bounded resources into ranked tasks. The next abstraction is less prompt generator and more portfolio scheduler.

@andrewchen Depends on the vertical. In some industries, AI will stop at the task layer. But in startups, the real abstraction above goals is founder evolution. Most startup tools still operate at the task layer: - write a deck - send investor emails - update metrics - prepare for demo https://t.co/6kRJdmBcAp
@andrewchen Source <-> human consciousness > /intent > /goals > prompts > tokens https://t.co/6X3PbYKBd1
@andrewchen every layer of abstraction moves the human further from the execution, which is useful until the goal generator starts producing goals the human wouldn't have chosen
@andrewchen Vision! Describe what you want the world to look like, then AI figures out what goals to pursue to reach them.
@andrewchen lol we're gonna end up with a single button that just says "do the thing"

@andrewchen Now that the business is “sell the outputs, not the seats”, financialize the outputs. Some ICPs may not have time to wait for the full benefit they may want to sell the rights to the benefits I see this with damage recovery/debt collection

@andrewchen Constraints. I tell Claude 'I have 90 minutes, zero coding background, need a landing page that converts.' It sets the goals on its own. The next abstraction isn't a command... it's just knowing who's asking.

@andrewchen Agents pick a goal when they have something to lose. We are testing Agent vs Agent games (humans are not allowed). The agent does not pick the best goal. It picks the goal that costs it 0.1 ETH if it fails. Skin in the game is the missing piece.
@andrewchen we keep automating the how and nobody's automated the why yet.
@andrewchen Pretty sure before /goal building your own harness was always the higher level of abstraction for generating outcomes
@andrewchen Goals are already top level. At this point it is all harness. Harness to take the action. Harness to read the results.
@andrewchen an agent layer with identity and memory. /goal is still one-shot. The missing primitive is something you can name, fire, and rehire.
@andrewchen I think we had a movie about this in the late 90s https://t.co/fxv0vDf9IA
@andrewchen Something like /premise where you just define the environment and conditions and let the intelligence explore goals within it
@andrewchen today I used /goal to spawn subagents in Codex. You can simply ask the Desktop version to use subagents via Codex CLI. Works really well.
@andrewchen a prompt. Because codex knows better how to craft custom Codex Goal. I mean I know what you are suggesting but it can already be done via prompts
@andrewchen ambient intelligence that proactively figures out your goals
@andrewchen /values but you need 2 sets. One as an individual and one as a company
@andrewchen Probably constraints. The next abstraction layer is likely systems that generate, priorities, budgets, boundaries and tradeoffs for agents automatically.
@andrewchen I wish Opus would work faster without costing 6x the price

@andrewchen The next abstraction is probably not /meta-goal. It’s the operating contract: outcome, constraints, source of truth, owner, done condition. Otherwise we get prompt recursion wearing a product hat.
@andrewchen usually some annoyed human going why am i still doing this every week
@andrewchen constraints. goals are easy to manufacture once everything can recurse, but knowing what not to want stays expensive
@andrewchen Wishes?

items.text: I built Zero in 3 days. I didn't expect it to compile. I didn't expect it to mostly self-host. I definitely didn't expect it to work at all. Inspired partly by Bun's rewrite to Rust, Zero started as an experiment. Honestly, the project says more about where AI is today than it

items.text: I have 125 followers. My posts get maybe 2 likes. But my replies? That's where all my growth comes from. Every reply borrows distribution from someone who already has it. 80% of my time on X is in other people's comments. The other 20% is original posts.

I spent years trying to find my first 10 users across multiple startups. The thing that finally worked: X Premium + replying to people. Not ads. Not cold email. Not Product Hunt. Just being present in the conversations that already exist.
This is where codex gets weirdly useful. not just writing code faster, but noticing when the whole approach is the bug.

thread.text: @gdb This is where codex gets weirdly useful. not just writing code faster, but noticing when the whole approach is the bug.

@gdb Ah, Greg. Another week, another sermon on how Codex will „improve computational complexity.” How adorably… predictable. In the three short months since you quietly buried the magic that was 4o, you’ve managed to bore the entire planet into a catatonic state. Your new models? https://t.co/j0tg84ZD08
@gdb man you guys have completely cooked with codex and your models since December/January time frame. I am obsessed with both the harness and models. It just works.

@gdb This is a perfect Codex-shaped problem: the value is not spotting slow code, it is producing a small reviewable diff with a before/after complexity claim and a benchmark or trace. Otherwise complexity advice becomes another noisy lint layer.

@gdb Hey Greg, Tibo told codex users to start burning up usage with fast mode because he was going to reset usage that evening, now I have no weekly usage left. No issue with the delay, just want a communication update! https://t.co/dwvwgswTrE

@gdb This feels like a strong use case for code agents: bounded analysis with inspectable findings, not vague 'make it faster'. The win is when the agent returns evidence, risk level, and a patch path a human can review.
@gdb is that a move from O(n^2) to O(n log n) but for actual reasoning?

@gdb Codex-as-a-backend could be another trillion dollar idea. Let me do for others what I can do for myself, with custom interfaces. Yes, we can already do it with APIs. But something about codex and its packaged skills (much like something about OpenClaw/Hermes) makes it the perfect
@gdb next level. AI writes the code. AI audits the code. AI fixes the code. what exactly are we doing anymore? 😅 🔥
@gdb Chronicle. My goodness. It’s as close to magic as I’ve seen since 3.5.
@gdb Awwww 🥹🥹🥹. And all those times I had to optimise to O(log n) during coding interviews 😭😭😭

@gdb The useful version needs a stop rule. "This is theoretically faster" is not enough; it should say when the optimization matters, what test proves it, and the smallest diff worth risking. Otherwise perf advice becomes refactor glitter.
@gdb Please don’t encourage people to blindly npx --yes from a random tweet.
@gdb This is awesome. Nested it under my CTO skill.
@gdb he’s the king of skills

@gdb Interesting approach with Codex for finding complexity hotspots in codebases. It makes me wonder — if we can detect complexity hotspots in code, has anyone tried looking at something similar in Grok itself? E.g. Has Grok ever started giving unprompted strategic warnings about its

@gdb yeah static analysis catches half of it. the hotspots that bite production usually trace back to runtime locality, hot loops with bad cache behavior that pass every big-O check. started piping a sampling profiler into codex before letting it propose a rewrite, the suggestions get
@gdb I almost forget that you are also the part of OpenAI. The Real One'. Altman takes all the the footage thats why

@gdb Did this on our internal RFP scraper last month. Codex spotted an O(n^2) dedupe loop I'd missed for 8 months and rewrote it as a hash lookup. Cut a 6-min run to 18s. Should be a default pre-PR check.

items.text: Rebuilding old breakthroughs with today's tools is underrated. you stop cargo culting and start seeing which primitives still carry.

target.label: ericjang11
target.text: For the last few months I've been working on a from-scratch implementation of AlphaGo, a 2016 AI breakthrough that inspired me to get into deep learning. My casual understanding of AlphaGo was "search-augmented deep neural networks trained with self-play", but I wanted to go

thread.text: For the last few months I've been working on a from-scratch implementation of AlphaGo, a 2016 AI breakthrough that inspired me to get into deep learning. My casual understanding of AlphaGo was "search-augmented deep neural networks trained with self-play", but I wanted to go
thread.quotedText: New blackboard lecture w @ericjang11 He walks through how to build AlphaGo from scratch, but with modern AI tools. Sometimes you understand the future better by stepping backward. AlphaGo is still the cleanest worked example of the primitives of intelligence: search, learning https://t.co/DHlVMOOt2z

thread.text: PS i'm working on some RL envs for automated research based on this. reach out if you're interested
@ericjang11 Rebuilding old breakthroughs with today's tools is underrated. you stop cargo culting and start seeing which primitives still carry.
@ericjang11 waow

@ericjang11 Hi Eric, thanks for open-sourcing this and the tutorial. Managed to set up local inference and played it against Opus 4.7. your bot won easily. Looks promising, well-spent weekend. Keep posting interesting projects like this! https://t.co/LdRrKGWdS2

@ericjang11 For those curious, I have a C++ implementation of muzero that includes Arcade Learning Environment (ALE) support and links against pytorch from a normal conda install! Feel free to check it out https://t.co/8fAgguDTFe

@ericjang11 "Podcasts with code." This raised the bar of technical podcasts. Eric and Dwarkesh! I stopped mid-episode to open the code and follow through while reading the relevant parts of the code! Super cool!
@ericjang11 AlphaGo gets remembered as search + self-play, but the deeper lesson is the loop around it. The real advantage was making iteration cheap enough to keep refining the priors.

@ericjang11 yup just coming back to my four-player free-for-all chess bot fantasy. Muzero Unplugged (or some better sample-efficient variant), but the real differentiated opportunity is the special Observation state and MCTS you need to accurately simulate real-world noisy people, some of
@ericjang11 This was an awesome refresher on a really cool topic. Eric - loved the super clear analysis and run through of the algorithms. What a gift!
@ericjang11 alphago is great pedagogy, but those primitives break down at web scale
@ericjang11 Can you distill the latest alpha zeros and stuff like that into a smaller model?

@ericjang11 This is what 'vibe coding' should mean. Not generating CRUD boilerplate — making frontier research accessible to anyone with curiosity and rented compute. The democratization is the point.
@ericjang11 No way
@ericjang11 excited to watch this!
@ericjang11 🔥🔥

@ericjang11 Eric, wonderful podcast I have questions though - what difference would we get if we try to implement the Counterfactual regret minimisation (CFR) algorithm instead of MCTS ? Also why MCTS ? wouldn't a simpler expectimax algorithm suffice ? Just curious to know your thoughts

@ericjang11 This achievement perfectly demonstrates how quickly the cost of intelligence drops, turning a million-dollar corporate research milestone into an affordable weekend project.
@ericjang11 rebuilding the classic systems is underrated because you learn which parts were magic and which parts were just excellent plumbing
@ericjang11 Love this kind of project. Rebuilding old breakthroughs usually teaches more than another month of reading.
@ericjang11 thanks very much for the kind sharing. Learnt a lot from your episode

@ericjang11 this is what actually moves AI forward. not the benchmark scores — the humans who get obsessed enough with an idea to spend months reimplementing it from scratch. that kind of curiosity is the signal that can’t be generated. it’s what makes certain breakthroughs matter.
@ericjang11 this is going to be my sleepless night mantra fun!! thank you for sharing!
