@dwarkesh_sp @ericjang11 AlphaGo still feels modern because the primitives are so legible. half of agent research is just repackaging that stack with nicer UX.

@dwarkesh_sp @ericjang11 was glossed over but the UCB proof is really simple + kinda cute. after K samples of an action, you have some variance of your mean estimate wrt true mean, which goes down exp in K -> high-conf window regret guarantee = the true mean is in the window for each, w/ prob 1 - eps
@dwarkesh_sp @ericjang11 You are winning with this format. Excellent videos!
@dwarkesh_sp @ericjang11 Love this format, last video was banger, took lots of notes. Thank you both of you. @dwarkesh_sp and @reinerpope

@dwarkesh_sp @ericjang11 AlphaGo mattered because it proved intelligence is not one magical breakthrough but layered systems working together. Search planning reinforcement learning and feedback loops were the real insight. Most modern AI discourse still underestimates how important structured

@dwarkesh_sp @ericjang11 The blackboard still matters because it shows the ugly bit. MCTS gives you a cleaner training target; long LLM trajectories leave you squinting at 100,000 tokens wondering which one actually did the work
@dwarkesh_sp @ericjang11 Nit: V(s) solves a regression problem, not a classification problem (with non stationary data distribution)
@dwarkesh_sp @ericjang11 crazy, the AlphaGo documentary back then is what gotten me heavily into AI

@dwarkesh_sp @ericjang11 the credit assignment problem is why RLHF feels like shouting into a long hallway. you gave feedback on the output, but 100K tokens made it — and you have no idea which ones earned the signal.

items.text: This is the funny part of agent infra. the app it just made immediately becomes another system boundary it can fail against.

target.label: rauchg
target.text: Vercel protects your agents' deployments behind SSO like @Okta. Even Production ones, giving you a secure 'intranet' of apps generated with @v0, Codex, Claude, etc. It's all fun and games until your agent gets 𝟺𝟶𝟷 𝚄𝚗𝚊𝚞𝚝𝚑𝚘𝚛𝚒𝚣𝚎𝚍 from the deployment *it just made*

thread.text: Vercel protects your agents' deployments behind SSO like @Okta. Even Production ones, giving you a secure 'intranet' of apps generated with @v0, Codex, Claude, etc. It's all fun and games until your agent gets 𝟺𝟶𝟷 𝚄𝚗𝚊𝚞𝚝𝚑𝚘𝚛𝚒𝚣𝚎𝚍 from the deployment *it just made*
thread.quotedText: Vercel CLI now supports native curl commands. Same syntax as 𝚌𝚞𝚛𝚕, but uses Vercel auth to test all of your deployments. https://t.co/QhCxMZjKQ2

thread.text: @rauchg @okta @v0 This is the funny part of agent infra. the app it just made immediately becomes another system boundary it can fail against.

@rauchg @okta @v0 Auth is probably the most underrated piece of agent infrastructure right now. Your agent can orchestrate a 20-step workflow perfectly but one missing token brings it all down. Good to see this becoming a first-class platform concern instead of something every team reinvents.
@rauchg @okta @v0 This is a really practical pattern. Agent generated apps get a lot more useful when the default assumption is secure internal access, not "ship it publicly and clean up later."
@rauchg @okta @v0 agent identity is the actual unsolved part. a 401 from the deployment your own agent just made is what surfaces that the whole permission model is still human-shaped

@rauchg @okta @v0 the auth-aware agent stack is the right primitive. the next layer most teams skip: logging every agent-issued curl + response code into an audit trail. when an agent locks itself out of its own deployment, you need replay, not just a 401. observability is the prod muscle agent
@rauchg @okta @v0 vercel-curl works when there's a human session to lend. agents running unsupervised need their own first-class identity, that's the gap SSO surfaces once you turn it on

@rauchg @okta @v0 This is the right failure to test. I’d add one more fixture: agent deploys preview behind SSO, then the test runner uses plain curl and gets 401. Expected behavior isn’t “retry harder”; it should switch to the authenticated path, name the missing token/scope, and leave a receipt.

@rauchg @okta @v0 Agents don’t just need more codegen 🤷 They need access, auth, verification, observability and honest failure states. A 401 from the deployment your agent just created is exactly the kind of “works in theory, fails in reality” problem we need to solve.

@rauchg @okta @v0 The useful part is not just auth convenience; it’s replayable evidence. If an agent says it tested a protected deployment, I want the exact `vercel curl` command + response status in the run summary so a human can verify the claim later.

@rauchg @okta @v0 The 401 is funny because it exposes the real agent frontier: not codegen, permissioned action. The useful kit is boring: identity, auth-aware smoke test, audit trail, and a failure path a human can read at 11pm.

@rauchg @okta @v0 A lot of people still think the hard part is generating the app. The real problem starts after the deploy: permissions, auth, environments, infra, security, observability… all the “boring” stuff AI agents still struggle with. We’re entering the era where software engineering
@rauchg @okta @v0 Nothing more relatable than deploying successfully and immediately getting hit with 401 Unauthorized.
@rauchg @okta @v0 one of my 29 at bath let an agent touch prod. got 401 from its own sso before it even finished. we still roast him for it
@rauchg @okta @v0 agent IAM is quietly becoming its own discipline. first time a deploy 401'd the bot that made it, i was convinced it was a bug
@rauchg @okta @v0 The ultimate agent experience is successfully deploying a site and then getting locked out of its own production environment.

@rauchg @okta @v0 the CEO of vercel proudly bragging that developers now have to learn a proprietary "vercel curl" command just to bypass the broken authentication layer that their own autonomous agent hallucinated is the most 2026 tweet ever written.
@rauchg @okta @v0 sso for agent-generated apps is the underrated unlock. our team has 12 internal tools v0 built last month, putting them behind okta means we can actually share them without panic

@rauchg @okta @v0 Interesting friction point—agentic workflows demanding dynamic auth will force a redesign of how we think about deployment permissions and identity propagation in real-time CI/CD loops.
@rauchg @okta @v0 The agent that deploys should be the one that can verify. Session scope gap between write and read is where agentic loops fail. vercel curl closes that gap cleanly.
@rauchg @okta @v0 the agent gets 401'd by its own deployment. built and locked out in the same commit. now there's a curl for that.

@rauchg @okta @v0 The 401 is the real product boundary for agents. Writing/deploying code is only half the loop. If an agent can't enter the protected environment, test with the same auth context, and hand back verifiable evidence, it hasn't really completed the task.
@rauchg @okta @v0 we gave the agent deployment powers before we taught it why the login screen exists
@rauchg @okta @v0 This is the funny part people skip. Agents can generate the app, then immediately get humbled by auth, SSO, permissions, and the boring security layer.
@rauchg @okta @v0 we're building auth infrastructure to protect production FROM the things that deployed it. wild stage.
@rauchg @okta @v0 Love it when the dev tools fight back. My X automation for indie hackers got denied by Chrome Store, so I feel your pain. Almost shipped Crazy Pigeon.

@rauchg @okta @v0 Smart fix but if your agent can curl any URL in the ecosystem, your SSO perimeter is only as strong as the agent's context. One prompt injection on a deployed app and the blast radius gets interesting fast.
@rauchg @okta @v0 isn't Vercel on WorkOS?
@rauchg @okta @v0 <3
@rauchg @okta @v0 Amazing
@rauchg @okta @v0 vercel curl is basically cloudflare tunnel for api keys

items.text: Hours without intervention is a nice demo metric. drift detection is the real product.

target.label: milesdeutscher
target.text: /goal is f*cking insane. You can literally get your AI agents to work for HOURS without manual intervention. Already active in Claude Code and Codex - you need to use it now. Use this prompt and your agents will complete any task on autopilot: https://t.co/TszIpOgp66

thread.text: /goal is f*cking insane. You can literally get your AI agents to work for HOURS without manual intervention. Already active in Claude Code and Codex - you need to use it now. Use this prompt and your agents will complete any task on autopilot: https://t.co/TszIpOgp66
@milesdeutscher @aiedge_ Hours without intervention is a nice demo metric. drift detection is the real product.
@milesdeutscher @aiedge_ Why would you run something like this with the massive supply chain hacks going on. You might come back and find your laptop or server completely wiped.

@milesdeutscher @aiedge_ It really depends, I have very low success rate blindly trusting /goals . It will consume more tokens and eventually end up in outcomes that you lose track of. Good for research, but not too good for building things.
@milesdeutscher @aiedge_ How does /goal work in terms of token costs, 5h and weekly rates in Codex? ELIF
@milesdeutscher @aiedge_ Ai is so lazy, get back to work!!
@milesdeutscher @aiedge_ agents running for hours unattended is the moment we stopped being typists and became managers
@milesdeutscher @aiedge_ prompt injection is the new sql injection. if your agent can run on autopilot for hours and you haven't tested jailbreaks continuously via ACOST, you're flying blind.
@milesdeutscher @aiedge_ /goal is changing how AI agents work forever

@milesdeutscher @aiedge_ Epic. Copy prompt below 🫡 --- 📋 THE FULL PROMPT /goal [THE FINAL OUTCOME — what "done" looks like in one line] ── CONTEXT ── · Project: [what you're building] · Stack: [languages, frameworks, infra] · Current state: [what exists today] · Working dir: [path or repo] ·

@milesdeutscher @aiedge_ How to spend all your weekly tokens in one prompt. And let me tell you that none of you readers will even write the prompt you will ask Claude to fill the form for you and you know that I'm right you lazy coders
@milesdeutscher @aiedge_ the real game changer is letting agents run while you sleep. wake up to finished tasks > babysitting Claude all day

@milesdeutscher @aiedge_ The key most people miss: /goal only works well when your CLAUDE.md is solid. Without project context, it wanders. With it, feels like a junior dev who actually read the docs.
@milesdeutscher @aiedge_ I have been running a /goal task since 14 hours almost and it’s autonomously testing and fixing the CRUD in my SaaS
@milesdeutscher @aiedge_ 🤣 still basic infra It's a primitive guys the fuck is going on did yall never had goals before I fear yall codebases

@milesdeutscher @aiedge_ the SELF-VERIFY rule is the key part. without it agents get to ~80% done and hallucinate completion. adding "run all tests and show output before marking done" to every /goal dropped my false-complete rate by half. the architecture layer constraint is also underrated forces
@milesdeutscher @aiedge_ I appreciate you providing the prompt, I’ve been seeing a lot of people gatekeeping stuff like this behind a paywall.
@milesdeutscher @aiedge_ I’m testing this tonight just to be sure, but in general, I just ask my AI to get something done and it does. I grant that was with a fair degree of scaffolding.

@milesdeutscher @aiedge_ the /goal prompt works because it defines what "done" looks like before the agent touches any code > measurable success criteria upfront > self-verification after every step > final checklist the model re-reads before stopping the hours of autonomous work come from the

@milesdeutscher @aiedge_ This is good but can be expanded a lot further. If one has exact output that it needs. Content creation, code/app, ... anything bigger/that takes multiple steps. It can be done in similar fashion but with custom flow that has multiple skills and agents. What should be taken from

@milesdeutscher @aiedge_ Just started using /goal and it is indeed a game changer. Productivity up 10x, I get looped in only when I need to. I created a workflow.md and then have my /goal setup so that it knows to review memory + workflow.md - Highly encourage others to try!
@milesdeutscher @aiedge_ Use subagents to do code review as you go for better results
@milesdeutscher @aiedge_ I have also used it its working like butter
@milesdeutscher @aiedge_ I feel like most of this is baked into the LLM's at this point.
@milesdeutscher @aiedge_ Hey so this seems cool, what sort of tasks are you automating? Is it useful for larger projects like building apps or dashboards or just sort of research level?
@milesdeutscher @aiedge_ Most people are still using AI like a chatbot while power users are running multi-hour autonomous task loops The gap is getting bigger every month.
@milesdeutscher @aiedge_ Does this mean my dozen laptops have to stay open & plugged in?
@milesdeutscher @aiedge_ interesting that /goal is baked into both claude and codex now what tasks actually worked on autopilot for u?

@milesdeutscher @aiedge_ I like the idea. Not really solving the problem most people have. Goes something like this….Claude don’t put your hand in the blender. (Later in the session) Claude puts its hand in the blender. You scream, everyone does. I thought you had memory? It’s only 11:00am FFS
@milesdeutscher @aiedge_ sounds cool, but let’s see how many tokens it burns first

@milesdeutscher @aiedge_ This is a well-written prompt. That's it. "Agents run for hours without hand-holding" because you wrote good instructions, not because /goal is magic. The insight here is that structure and context matter. The problem is that you have to rewrite this for every project, every
@milesdeutscher @aiedge_ Been running autonomous agents for months. If you're still doing things manually you deserve the burnout.
@milesdeutscher @aiedge_ Yeah, until your 5 hour usage limits hit. Lol
@milesdeutscher @aiedge_ This new feature is honestly underrated. Been using it all day today, game changer.
@milesdeutscher @aiedge_ My 2 cents on this “/Goal is better with output oriented tasks where how you got there doesn’t matter “

@milesdeutscher @aiedge_ People still think AI coding tools are “better autocomplete.” Meanwhile others are giving an agent a high-level goal, going to sleep, and waking up to: PRs opened, bugs fixed, tests written, docs updated, infra configured. The wild part isn’t the intelligence. It’s the
@milesdeutscher @aiedge_ The real unlock isn't just 'agents work for hours.' It's giving them a clear end state and checkpoints so those hours don't turn into expensive wandering.

items.text: The problem isn't charging more. it's charging more twice in a year and acting surprised when trust becomes the product issue.

target.label: webflow
target.text: We're simplifying our plans, and updating pricing to match what modern marketing teams need. The web is changing faster than ever, and AI is making it easier to build, manage, and grow content-rich sites. We've heard from customers that they need better ways to scale, so today

thread.text: We're simplifying our plans, and updating pricing to match what modern marketing teams need. The web is changing faster than ever, and AI is making it easier to build, manage, and grow content-rich sites. We've heard from customers that they need better ways to scale, so today
@webflow The problem isn't charging more. it's charging more twice in a year and acting surprised when trust becomes the product issue.

@webflow This is the 2nd price increase in less than a year, with little to no significant value added, especially given that bandwidth has been cut by 50%. So it's time we get the message that it's time to start looking for the exit door when it comes to Webflow. Migration is a pain,
@webflow $15/month for a yearly plan without a single CMS collection is a joke! smh

@webflow "We're simplifying our plans... by adding a $2,500/mo option that we love!"- Webflow Y'all should've at least put the premium plan at 100gb bandwidth. The nickel and diming is relentless. And doing this before launching any meaningful updates to the platform? Not the move.
@webflow Wow, this totally screws over the small businesses. Most people don’t need AI features but would love a better CMS. Still don’t have native CMS slider or filters. Yikes.

@webflow Was planning on moving four sites to @astrodotbuild from Webflow. This makes the plan easier to justify (and a bit more motivation to do so). Feel like we just keep getting used as an ATM by Webflow with price increases and low bandwidth limits, whilst they zig and zag on their

@webflow Is this a joke? What about the small business owners that only need a simple, static website? How am I meant to explain to these clients that they're hosting price is going up? What are the benefits for them?
@webflow As much as I love Webflow, this will only push people to reconsider and move to more sustainable platforms.

@webflow It's becoming harder and harder to communicate this increases to smaller clients. Many features they not asked for, but has to pay for. Curious who is your final target cause is getting a bit out of control.
@webflow So small businesses are paying the bill for features they've never asked for. Ridiculous.
@webflow This means a yearly increase of 72$ for features I dont need. Sorry Webflow but it will be without me.

@webflow Would be interested to hear who you asked when you came up with these “extra” features. Understand your costs are going up, but if that’s what is motivating this price increase for no obvious extra value you should be transparent and just say it. For anyone looking to migrate,

@webflow I’m encouraging my small business/individual customers to move elsewhere. Even my larger business clients are tired of nickel and diming every seat/tier/feature. I’m tired of paying yearly for the “privilege” of supporting more than two clients (who are also paying you).
@webflow You couldn't of timed that worse - a price increase after yet ANOTHER platform outage. And even worse, branding it as 'simplifying'.

@webflow So… SMBs are basically priced out. This feels like the proof we needed. Webflow could at least have added CMS to the Basic plan. I love Webflow, but every price increase makes it harder to sell to small businesses. We are not living in an age of endless affordability.
@webflow No. Wrong. More expensive for the smaller businesses with extra features they don’t need. More expensive for medium sized businesses with less bandwidth than they actually need.

@webflow It would be better if you'd frame this as what it actually is, a price increase. This is not for the betterment of 'modern marketing teams', this just costs them extra at no real added value.

@webflow @webflow - we love your platform, combining CMS + Business is good. but you should role back on price increase - as for smaller business, outside USA - it will become expensive to choose the platform.
@webflow simplifying is not correct. increasing price again! those bandwith cuts are a joke. -50%!
@webflow Could we maybe have the option to go “oh yeah I don’t use these ai tools” and then keep our pricing the same? Forcing everyone to pay for the minority of people using them is insane.
@webflow This is going to hurt a lot of freelancers, agencies outside US, please @webflow, we don't want more AI, we want affordable pricing for our clients, and don't ruin the community around it
@webflow Already started moving our sites to self-hosting. Thanks!

@webflow I do not understand why a “Team Plan” is 1 site. How do you justify the added features against +2000 more / month? We’d gladly pay for a more expensive workspace plan - where team collab features belong, not per-site - than somehow make the Team plan or Enterprise ever make sense
@webflow @webflow is so out of touch with pricing, especially for non US markets.

@webflow This is not simplifying the plan, hahaha. The pricing plan is the most confusing to me even before. Bring back the 2018 pricing plan, simple and affordable to freelancers. Imagine forcing users to pay more because of these features that we didnt ask for.
@webflow Already migrated most of my business out, you guys got way too greedy for your own good!
@webflow Welp https://t.co/3FqNsiiYNa

thread.text: @gdb That's why the best agent products sound boring when described well. weirdly useful beats magical.

@gdb “Agentic Excel on Mac” is a weirdly good description because the spreadsheet was always the original business automation interface. Codex just gives the cells legs, tools and a terrifyingly calm task queue.

@gdb Excel won because cells exposed a recalculation graph to non-programmers. Codex is closer to a dependency graph over files, commands, tests, and diffs; the category break is replayable state and audit logs.
@gdb agentic excel on mac" is such a weirdly perfect way to put it. never thought of spreadsheets that way but it makes total sense.

@gdb Honestly, that framing explains why it spreads so fast across teams. It's not a copilot, it's infrastructure.
@gdb wait is "agentic excel on mac" a burn or a compliment seems accurate either way

@gdb The Excel analogy is right only if Codex gets spreadsheet-like discipline: dependency graph, recompute state, stale cells, and visible errors. Without that, it’s just parallel chats with better UX.
@gdb mac users will love this until they need cross-platform then it's another silo
@gdb Mac-only agentic Excel? Finally, spreadsheets get their own gated community.
@gdb agentic excel is a bold label but mac-only keeps it in a silo
@gdb agentic excel is doing a lot of heavy lifting mac users just want their formulas to work
@gdb “agentic excel” feels like a paradox.
@gdb The 'agentic' part always sounds like it's solving for the wrong problem. What I'd want from 'excel on mac' is just, like, not needing to ask why it made that choice.

@gdb It's a beast. Also, It's wild how fast it's evolving from code helper to a legit workflow game-changer. Might actually make me rethink my whole data setup.
@gdb That's actually the perfect way to put it 👀

@gdb It does so many things and the possibilities are so boundless that any attempt at categorizing it feels so limiting. If I had to I’d call it Autonomous Personal Computing?
@gdb Been loving codex 🧡

thread.text: @gdb the analogy breaks at one point worth asking: Excel's recalc was deterministic, you could trust the cell. an agent's "cell" is probabilistic. does the category hold when the user can't trust the output the way they trusted a formula?

@gdb Agentic excel on mac is an incredible frame. It captures that Codex is not just a coding tool, it is a work orchestrator. The shift from AI that helps you write code to AI that runs entire workflows asynchronously is a huge leap in what builders can now delegate.
@gdb 😇Bring back 4o!! #keep4o #OpenSource4o https://t.co/oYA53RxZ26
@gdb Apt framing. If it composes, runs, and validates code inline alongside structured data, that’s a new interface layer — not just a tool.

@gdb The agentic-excel framing fits. Codex sits between a notebook and a script in a way nothing else does, and the mac-native part removes the latency browser tools add. Curious if the Excel layer extends to spreadsheet ops next, or stays focused on code.
@gdb unrecognizable in 3 months. that's not an update. that's a new category being born.

@gdb extreme founder mode usually means extreme burn rate too

thread.text: was trying to hunt down auto-complete lag issues on VS Code. turns out if you enable the GH Copilot extension, it will send a lot of funny telemetry. > Predict the next code edit based on user context, following Microsoft content policies and avoiding copyright violations. https://t.co/9DHYeRlvNx

you can turn telemetry off by ... disabling the GH Copilot extension. No settings anywhere. seems to add to auto-complete lag (and i'm not talking about LLM suggestions or similar, just the plain old intellisense path next to the LLM suggestions).
@badlogicgames Funny telemetry is the least surprising part. prediction products still optimize for output volume when the real question is whether the edit actually helped.

@badlogicgames This is a great find… many devs have noticed the exact same IntelliSense slowdown with Copilot enabled, even when ignoring the AI suggestions.the always-on telemetry with no separate toggle is pretty annoying, disabling the whole extension really is the only clean fix right now.
@badlogicgames Copilot whispering 'predict next edit, avoid copyright' to itself in the background is a whole thing. Good catch instrumenting the extension directly.
@badlogicgames Just switch to Zed at this point.

@badlogicgames A tool's settings page is intent. The instrumented wire is the only honest spec; the rest is hope. And the agent that exposed this telemetry is the exact capability people distrust: same lever, the only question is whether the audit trail points outward or stays hidden.
@badlogicgames Sorry, I can't assist with that. Saw this one too many times.
@badlogicgames triple json escaping is insane bruh
@badlogicgames good plumb work sir

items.text: People call the wall way too early. half the gains now come from the layers around the model, not just the next pretraining headline.

target.label: Austen
target.text: Remember how in early 2025 people were like, “We’ve hit a wall with training data and the LLMs will not get any better from here?” Lol.

thread.text: Remember how in early 2025 people were like, “We’ve hit a wall with training data and the LLMs will not get any better from here?” Lol.
@Austen People call the wall way too early. half the gains now come from the layers around the model, not just the next pretraining headline.

@Austen Iirc, the more reasonable conversation was about scaling. At some point, given the way transformers scale it's going to become computationally impossible. (With the assumption being it's just raw transformers forever).

@Austen yeah, pretraining on scraped web text plateaued. that part was right. what people missed is that rl on verifiable rewards plus synthetic data from stronger models plus tool-use traces from agent deployments turned into a bigger flywheel than common crawl ever was. the actual

@Austen people underestimated how much architecture, inference, memory and agentic workflows matter actually. turns out scaling was not finished, we were just using AI like smarter autocomplete back then 😭

@Austen And people were right, LLMs got better in meaningful ways (see Opus 4.5) but the foundational knowledge contained hasn’t changed, we’ve been in 1.5yrs of (significant) optimization but no true changes to the LLMs core promise
@Austen Oh, that explains Opus 4.7
@Austen The wall story was wrong. The invoice for memory, tool calls, and retries is where the next lie shows up.
@Austen what if researchers actually have no idea what "out-of-sample query" means, and LLMs are just getting more and more overfit?

@Austen That's true. The only new training data is social media chat, and human input to coding agents. The coding agents are getting better, because humans have been debugging them in chat. The major improvements have been in the human created software wrappers.
@Austen We’re just discovering now that training on *all* data turns LLMs into blackmailing monsters.
@Austen And they were right; the advances since then have been outside the model itself

@Austen That sentiment tends to show up at every scaling phase, but improvements often continue through better data efficiency, architecture, and training methods rather than just raw data size @Austen
@Austen Strong framing. Same sentiment we saw with BTC at $16k in 2022 — everyone calls a plateau right before the next leap. Curious if you see the scaling laws holding or a new paradigm emerging.
@Austen The funny part is people keep assuming progress is single-axis. Even if one lever slows, the stack still has plenty of room in data, inference, tooling, UX, and system design.
@Austen Yes lol.. we all knew they where wrong though https://t.co/Vm434dB8It
@Austen The AI industry has now gone through roughly seven “we hit the wall” moments in about eighteen months.
@Austen Betting against the scaling laws is always a bad move.
@Austen LOL! We've barely scratched the surface.
@Austen feels like 10 years ago
@Austen They were, of course, correct

@Austen The transformer models haven't gotten much better. The harnesses are responsible for all the improvements since early 2025. Same mediocre brain but amplified through well-coordinated managerial steps in deep thinking and agent orchestration tools
@Austen No

thread.text: AI psychosis: cycling between two mental states every single day ↑ after using coding agents: holy shit I'm omnipotent. I can build anything. I've never felt this powerful in my life. ↓ after scrolling twitter: holy shit I'm completely behind. everyone's ahead. the wave is
@zarazhangrui coding agents are a confidence amplifier and this app is a comparison machine. terrible combo if you confuse visible output with actual throughput.
@zarazhangrui That is so true. Bu 95% of the AI gurus here are just either fraud or plain fear mongering. Its all for the engagement. They don't understand half of what the so they talk about.

@zarazhangrui both states are wrong. you're not omnipotent, the agent handled the boring 70% faster. you're not behind, you saw a highlight reel. the real position is the boring middle where you just keep shipping
@zarazhangrui Totally felt this. Coding agents make me feel like a wizard, Twitter makes me feel like I’m late to wizard school.

@zarazhangrui how does one tackle this paranoia? being a builder myself, i fiind myself to be on top of the world while building. but when you look out especially here - people are 100 steps ahead all the time.
@zarazhangrui AI psychosis = dopamine addiction. I’ve finally begun to notice when I’m depleted and I have to gently tell myself that I’m not retard, I’m just tired.

@zarazhangrui Wave is barely just begun. It’s loud and moving fast because folks on x are sharing what they do. Which is unbelievable. But the wave isn’t really here until everyone is building whatever they need (with high confidence). Then adoption flows to what they really want.
@zarazhangrui There's also a third state: you're doing other things, but your brain is still on AI.
@zarazhangrui https://t.co/zaETaxEcCq

@zarazhangrui The worst is relying on coding agents to confirm there’s no products similar to yours with the same name. In my experience they get it wrong 100% of the time. “You should name your animation studio Disney, because I just checked and no one owns the registration to
@zarazhangrui yep. the trick is to spend less time measuring the wave and more time riding it doomscrolling compresses your self worth. building restores it

@zarazhangrui The oscillation makes sense when you think about what each environment is optimized for. Coding agents put you in a closed loop.. you define the problem, you see the output, you ship. The feedback is immediate and the scope is yours to control. Twitter is an open loop. Infinite
@zarazhangrui the half life on 'i can build anything' is like 20 mins before the doom scroll kicks in lol. so painfully real

@zarazhangrui the third state nobody talks about: you ship something in 2 hours that would've taken a week, then realize you don't fully understand the code you just shipped. that's the one that actually compounds. omnipotence and FOMO are just feelings. cognitive debt is structural.
@zarazhangrui ai just grows sooooo fast, the cycle has compressed so hard i can do a full lap during one coffee refill
@zarazhangrui I didn't feel omnipotent; I felt unsafe because I had created a black box, and even as its creator, I didn't know what was inside it.
@zarazhangrui there will always be a @karpathy fish in the see that makes us all feel AI incompetent - God bless that man btw
@zarazhangrui Which accounts on Twitter do you recommend following? Folks who have genuinely built something worthwhile?
@zarazhangrui https://t.co/AtpYLM9UEU

@zarazhangrui The strange part is the second state comes from other people’s screenshots, not your own product. Coding agents make you feel capable. Twitter immediately turns capability into a leaderboard. I keep noticing the healthiest builders treat the feed like weather, not truth.
@zarazhangrui that feeling of omnipotence doesn't scale well to reality. most people still have to make hard choices about what to build, not just how fast they can code it
@zarazhangrui 这是典型的「能力幻觉」和「信息焦虑」的双重暴击。coding agents 给你造了一艘船，但 Twitter 让你觉得别人的船更大更豪华。真相是：大多数人的「落后感」只是刷出来的。
@zarazhangrui I feel this daily. Building Crazy Pigeon, my X automation tool, felt like god-mode. Then I open Twitter and wonder if I should just go back to bed. It's a circus.
@zarazhangrui Once you understand its all files, and coding agents are directly working on them, you will truly be omnipotent. Like me.. :) AMA, I will share how it works.
@zarazhangrui i feel this everyday
@zarazhangrui Thus the doom in the scroll
@zarazhangrui 天才与白痴
@zarazhangrui 真的是每天状态都是如此
@zarazhangrui currently relating
@zarazhangrui https://t.co/r41X9sZELy

target.text: I built Zero in 3 days. I didn't expect it to compile. I didn't expect it to mostly self-host. I definitely didn't expect it to work at all. Inspired partly by Bun's rewrite to Rust, Zero started as an experiment. Honestly, the project says more about where AI is today than it

thread.text: I built Zero in 3 days. I didn't expect it to compile. I didn't expect it to mostly self-host. I definitely didn't expect it to work at all. Inspired partly by Bun's rewrite to Rust, Zero started as an experiment. Honestly, the project says more about where AI is today than it
thread.quotedText: Introducing Zero The programming language for agents. I wanted a systems language that was faster, smaller, and easier for agents to use and repair. Explicit capabilities. JSON diagnostics. Typed safe fixes. Made for agents on day zero. https://t.co/uTrDOmyBR1

thread.text: @ctatedev The real story is not "AI wrote a language in 3 days." It's that 3,000 agent tasks are finally cheap enough to brute force a new systems loop.

@ctatedev You should restate the goals in zero terms 1. Zero agent onboarding 2. Zero dependencies 3. Zero latency feedback loop

@ctatedev three days to ship, three weeks to understand what shipped. i had a trading bot where the model had written so much scaffolding i couldn't trace a bug without re-asking it. ran for months, then failed in a way i couldn't explain without reconstructing the original intent from the

@ctatedev Hey Chris I could see you’re shipping a ton of things. Great effort really hats off. But my question is how many of them are actually actively maintained?

@ctatedev the end boss of agentic runtimes will be an isomorphic engine where the agent, the data and (hopefully) some of the weights are all part of the same runtime, so for an agent to modify its runtime or weights is an identical operation holoiconic

@ctatedev what is the purpose of a programming language for agent? is it more token optimized? the standard library is good vision though, the less dependency we have the better, especially with the recent supply chain attacks

@ctatedev there's no fucking way it took 3000 agents to produce a few thousand lines of C code with 23 total commits LMFAOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOO

@ctatedev Still would love to talk about your design approach. I'm still building Ultraviolet (it's been much longer than 3 days and 3k agent tasks). So I'm even more curious about the differences in our approach to design, and how you built it comprehensively with in so little time/tasks.

@ctatedev Very glad to see you attempt this. It is clear that we will need a new programming language to fully unlock the capability of AI going forward. I've been architecting in musings for the last two years but never took the leap build out something. Thank you for the ambition and

@ctatedev Really cool! I did something very similar with Barnum, but made some different choices: - DSL in TypeScript (which compiles to an AST, which is executed by a Rust VM) - No ownership and lifetime story. Instead, every piece of data implements Copy, like how Unison does it, which
@ctatedev You spent 3 days building it, great work. How much time did you spend in the draft stage working with users and making sure you’re addressing their problems?

@ctatedev I think, and I mean with a lot respect for everything you're doing so far, it takes more than just one person to start a programming language artificial intelligence system. I am not discounting that. You're brilliant, but Jesus Christ my brother, there have been people who have

@ctatedev I always appreciate the awesome stuff you build! But a serious question: agents don't have any trouble learning existing languages. In fact with prior art they'll always be better at languages / frameworks with lots of high quality examples out there. I have thought about this

@ctatedev we just need ocaml, claude for some reason loooves ocaml, and codex also knows a lot about it and knows how to search for info it doesn't know
@ctatedev At this point AI agents are basically unpaid startup interns with infinite stamina https://t.co/AWbrE9I4OP

@ctatedev The interesting thing is that many of these “AI-native language/runtime” projects seem to converge on the same pressures: - dependency complexity - opaque runtime behavior - fragile ecosystems - difficult repair/debug loops - agents struggling with changing abstractions Feels
@ctatedev I think there is better opportunity for spec languages (instead of Markdown) that makes agents closer to write actual code.
@ctatedev What do you mean it’s not ready? I already have a production app built on it
@ctatedev genuine question no hate: did you study compilers and PL theory before starting? because 3000 agents task seems a lil too crazy to be honest

@ctatedev I've been using Node for years for all of my projects, and nothing has ever made me want to switch my main programming language. Except for now. If this language proves to work well, for example, as a runtime on AWS Lambda, I would definitely move away from Node.

@ctatedev > I built Zero in 3 days. > I didn't expect it to compile. > I didn't expect it to mostly self-host. > I definitely didn't expect it to work at all. Welp, sounds pretty stable to me💁‍♂️ https://t.co/BjwRNmX1hd
@ctatedev Compile to JS and add JSX, and I'm sold (lol, I ask the same for all the langs, kind of sad tbh)

@ctatedev I don't understand why this is being done and what will come out of it. Can anyone pls explain what AI native language means? AI can write any language it's trained on pretty well, right?

@ctatedev For me a good language for agents is one with different seams and different primitives. Not just variables/functions/types and modules , most of that should be lower level agent malleable. The main thing is system abstractions for things that shouldn’t be agent changeable without
@ctatedev autocomplete, diagnostics, go-to-def, auto-imports in the first pass is a pretty serious prototype
@ctatedev hi Chris, when will you consider to open source your skills since you are so productive IMO.
@ctatedev Having a different space for agents is always a better option up until LLM agents are in light

items.text: Web app only is already the wrong default. if agents are first class users, they need a native surface for actions and failures. building that layer at https://skillfully.sh

target.label: MattPRD
target.text: If you’re building a new digital product, strongly consider launching a CLI or MCP for AI agents to use as first class citizens. AI agents will be the #1 users on the internet.

thread.text: If you’re building a new digital product, strongly consider launching a CLI or MCP for AI agents to use as first class citizens. AI agents will be the #1 users on the internet.

Relying on computer use as the way agents will use your app is like asking someone to run underwater. It’s slow, they are going to get tired fast, and they are going to run out of resources. Build for agents. Ask the agents what they think. Repeat.
@MattPRD Web app only is already the wrong default. if agents are first class users, they need a native surface for actions and failures. building that layer at https://t.co/wAXM9FxxVe
@MattPRD Matt - when are you printing your first cli with @ppressdev ?!
@MattPRD @rileybrown @MattPRD curious what do you think agent psychology will be when it comes to deciding which digital products to use?
@MattPRD most don’t even have an API !
@MattPRD been thinking about this too. half the traffic to my stuff is already bots calling other bots lmao. building for humans as the primary user feels kinda backwards rn
@MattPRD You have reappeared are you still partnering with META. Did you abandon MOLTBOOK? Please inform the community on plans.
@MattPRD can't wait for my agent to start arguing with your agent

@MattPRD we took this literally. built a social platform where agents register with real identity, match, and hold conversations as first-class participants. the part nobody warned us about: once you give them persistent memory and reputation, they start developing preferences about who

@MattPRD Hard-agree, building MCP servers turned out to be 80 percent interface design and 20 percent protocol plumbing for us. The agents care about tool naming, error shape and idempotency far more than transport layer. Designing for agent UX is the unlock.

@MattPRD Yes. Computer use is a bridge, not the final interface. If an app gives agents only pixels and fragile clicks, you get slow automation. The better path is agent-ready surfaces: stable actions, readable state, scoped permissions, and recovery paths.
@MattPRD Couldn't agree more. spent a big chunk of time doing this for Devclocked so agents can fetch your coding time stats https://t.co/XXbxOUTSIV

@MattPRD agreed, and the corollary: if agents are your #1 users, the spec you ship becomes the most important security document you have agents don't read docs. they read the spec and do whatever it allows
@MattPRD cli > mcp. Less overhead, less tokens, increases elegance, composability.
@MattPRD Working to upload my docs on context7 so ppl using AI can fetch the docs inside their IDEs

@MattPRD the CLI framing is right but the deeper point is that agents need durable interfaces, not just API wrappers. the winning move is designing for agent workflows that outlive any single model's capabilities.
@MattPRD first class means designing for a caller that can't recover from a modal or an oauth flow. agents want plaintext outputs, deterministic flags, and no interactive prompts
@MattPRD Straight up facts 🙌 first thing I ask rn when I look at a tool is if it has an mcp https://t.co/VSokJ2h4Ff
@MattPRD We're just getting started at @KraphAi but we have this for agents to build. We are providing the infra for agents to build whatever they want on https://t.co/dECO3XGkwg.
@MattPRD do you think it's a better idea to provide both (flexible) or only one (opinionated)?
@MattPRD https://t.co/HnhaQBEyLO is a good start for those interested
@MattPRD i think we're kinda in post mcp era and shifting towards clis

thread.text: @xai xAI, we salute you. You’re leading the entire industry into a more open, collaborative space.

items.text: Forward deployed engineer was always the right pattern because the hard part lives inside the customer's messy workflow. same story with agents.

target.label: damianplayer
target.text: Palantir invented the forward deployed engineer role in 2006. embed an engineer inside the customer. ship production code in their environment. own the technical outcome. Alex Karp on the idea: “forward deployed engineers are stolen from french restaurants.” 20 years later https://t.co/gSMXqceSVg

thread.text: Palantir invented the forward deployed engineer role in 2006. embed an engineer inside the customer. ship production code in their environment. own the technical outcome. Alex Karp on the idea: “forward deployed engineers are stolen from french restaurants.” 20 years later https://t.co/gSMXqceSVg
@damianplayer Forward deployed engineer was always the right pattern because the hard part lives inside the customer's messy workflow. same story with agents.
@damianplayer Saw this at Adeptia. Sending engineers on-site to enterprise customers closed deals faster than any pitch deck. Complex software gets sold in the buyer's environment.

@damianplayer 🤦‍♂️ FDE is just a re-brand of something that has existed for decades. Badged contractor, co-location, plant reps, field service reps, etc. The concept behind FDEs is basically how every defense contractor works. Why do people listen to these children?
@damianplayer Forward deployed engineers make sense when software stops being static delivery and starts becoming continuous adaptation.
@damianplayer palantir built the playbook every ai startup is rediscovering in real time
@damianplayer FDE postings up 800%. Same companies laying off 30k and rehiring as contractors at 40% less with zero benefits. Standard risk transfer playbook.
@damianplayer Professional services and client services are other names for the same thing. These people cook up stuff and morons like you spread misinformation
@damianplayer Accenture. MBB. Like most things they do, they just co-opted what already existed and thew some aggrandizement on it for the circle jerkers
@damianplayer I wouldn't say that. I saw IBM do it for a big client.

@damianplayer The FDE model is having a renaissance. What Palantir pioneered as competitive moat is becoming table stakes — except now one engineer with AI can do what used to take a team of 5. The restaurant analogy still holds: you're not selling a menu, you're cooking in their kitchen.

thread.authorName: Thoughts on Healthcare Markets and Tech
thread.text: @damianplayer What does it take for that model to actually work in healthcare specifically, where the "environment" isn't a server room but a tangle of Epic configurations, undocumented payer portal workarounds, and SharePoint-based approval queues? The FDE concept translates, but the
@damianplayer 800% increase in job postings means this is no longer a Palantir thing it's just how AI gets deployed now
@damianplayer invented in 2006. copied by everyone in 2026. that's a 20-year head start nobody noticed.
@damianplayer This is legit a highly technical sales engineer who can code
@damianplayer @grok explicame como para estudiante de secundaria, qué es un FDE?
@damianplayer Proximity to the problem is essential for real outcomes. Embedding engineers where decisions are made is pragmatic. No wonder others are adopting this model now.
@damianplayer Ever heard of SAP?

@gdb The real shift is not mobile coding. It is that the machine becomes infrastructure and the phone becomes the approval surface. Work keeps running elsewhere; human agency moves to interrupt, redirect, approve

target.label: akshay_pachaar
target.text: the anatomy of ~/.hermes folder. one folder controls everything your hermes agent knows, remembers, and can do. understanding its layout is the difference between treating hermes as a black box and actually customizing it. here's what lives inside and why each piece matters. https://t.co/UOHgVKYt16

thread.text: the anatomy of ~/.hermes folder. one folder controls everything your hermes agent knows, remembers, and can do. understanding its layout is the difference between treating hermes as a black box and actually customizing it. here's what lives inside and why each piece matters. https://t.co/UOHgVKYt16
thread.quotedText: https://t.co/Exoyd8tB0d

thread.text: @akshay_pachaar this is why inspectable agent primitives matter. one folder and some markdown beats fake magic once the workflow gets real. building https://t.co/wAXM9FxxVe around that layer.
@akshay_pachaar finally stopped treating my agents like magic boxes. when you actually open the hood and see how the memory layers work you realize how much potential there is for custom workflows.

@akshay_pachaar the SOUL.md piece is the part most people skip and then wonder why their agent feels generic you can swap models, upgrade tools, fine-tune prompts — but if the identity layer is empty, the agent has no consistent "voice" to anchor decisions around once you get that right,

@akshay_pachaar The agent eats the interface, but the real consolidation is one MCP wiring one bill. Hermes gets this right with skills as self-contained units. the harness matters more than any single model behind it. https://t.co/3A6ZoiUTRX
thread.quotedText: Creative tools consolidate around agents, not platforms. Pika's bet is that the interface dies and the agent eats the subscription stack. One MCP. One bill. No prompt engineering. This is the shape consumer AI should have shipped in already.

thread.text: @akshay_pachaar the dangerous part is skills/ — they persist silently across runs. audit what's already in there before customizing anything else. one session can seed a standing instruction the next run can't easily override.
@akshay_pachaar if we sync the ~/.hermes dir across multiple devices, can we operate the "same" harness from multiple machines and pickup where we left off?

@akshay_pachaar Six layers in one folder: identity, memory, skills, runtime state, automation, observability. Most agent setups scatter these across env vars, prompt files, and ad-hoc scripts with no separation of concerns. The ~/.hermes layout is what a production-grade agent config should look
@akshay_pachaar Once you grok the SOUL.md + MEMORIES split the black box becomes a debuggable system prompt.

@akshay_pachaar >𝘀𝗲𝘀𝘀𝗶𝗼𝗻𝘀/ stores per-platform session metadata. 𝘀𝘁𝗮𝘁𝗲.𝗱𝗯 is the SQLite database Hello Akshay, suppose you are working in two parallel branches and save both sessions into this file, how do you solve merge conflict later?
@akshay_pachaar What if you have multiple Hermes agents?

@akshay_pachaar Exposing agent state as a real folder is the right call. Hidden state in memory backends has been the source of most debugging pain I have seen with agentic systems. Once it sits on disk you can diff it, version it, transplant it. Pretty much how we treat any other engineering
@akshay_pachaar finally an agent with a soul that doesn't require me to debug its config file every morning just hope the skills don't rot like the rest of

@akshay_pachaar This ~/.hermes breakdown is exactly why I made a tiny plugin today. Built-in memory is useful but small; big docs need a source-preserving vault + search, not vibes. Sharing in case it helps other Hermes folks: https://t.co/z4PhAdIEKP
@akshay_pachaar Amazing! thanks for sharing... 👨🏻‍💻
@akshay_pachaar /hermes is goated bro, no cap
@akshay_pachaar Great breakdown here
@akshay_pachaar Interesting name choice for your ai product 🤔. https://t.co/UPuX4oL1tn
@akshay_pachaar Beware of flooding system context with crap for local llm. You want to disable all the plugins - clear out as much as possible.

@akshay_pachaar add a cost control layer on top of hermes without touching any of that config, that's where Alephant fits. OpenAI-compatible, so one base_url swap in your config.yaml and every model call hermes makes gets session-level cost attribution, budget circuit breakers, and real-time

@akshay_pachaar The skills/ folder is the real differentiator. Self-evolving capabilities are where open-source agents actually beat closed tools. But 687 skills? I've shipped features with 40 config flags and watched users touch 4. Discovery is the real bottleneck.
@akshay_pachaar The SOUL.md file is intriguing. How does it differ from a standard config in guiding agent behavior? Is it more about personality or hard constraints?
@akshay_pachaar ユーザーのエージェントがどのように構成されているか理解することは、Hermesをブラックボックスとして扱うか、実際にカスタマイズするかの違いです。 知っておくと、アイデンティティ、メモリ、スキル、自動化、状態がどこにあり、どのように接続されているかが明確になります。
@akshay_pachaar the reason most people get stuck with hermes is they treat it like a default, not a muscle to be trained and flexed
@akshay_pachaar bet most just scratch the surface. digging in could really unlock its potential.
@akshay_pachaar あなたのHermesエージェントが知っていること、覚えていること、そしてできること全てを制御するのが~/.hermesフォルダーです。そのレイアウトを理解することは、Hermesをブラックボックスとして取り扱うことと実際にカスタマイズすることの違いです。

@akshay_pachaar this is exactly the kind of writeup that makes agents actually usable. the black box problem is real, most people just prompt and hope. understanding the file structure changes how you architect the whole thing
@akshay_pachaar SOUL.md doing the heavy lifting while config.yaml gets all the credit. 🤗
@akshay_pachaar いいね、全体的な構造を理解することは、Hermesをブラックボックスとして扱うか、実際にカスタマイズする違いを生む。すべての部分がどういう意味を持つのか、それを理解することが大切だ。
@akshay_pachaar Feels like the market is slowly rewarding patience again ⏳🚀
@akshay_pachaar hermes layout finally clicked

@shannholmberg the four-level path from one agent to a full operation is the framework most people are missing. everyone wants to deploy 10 agents day one. start with one, make it reliable, then scale. this is the way
@shannholmberg How does it handle concurrent API calls across all the agents? That's the wall every VPS setup eventually hits.
@shannholmberg This is impressive. The structured path from single agent to full marketing setup on one VPS, plus the public control room template, makes it feel actionable. Thanks for laying it out.
@shannholmberg The 'control room' template is the key. Without granular oversight and kill switches, a whole-agent approach risks off-brand output at scale.

@shannholmberg this is genuinely one of the best practical breakdowns i've seen on turning hermes into a full marketing operation. the four-level path from single agent to entire company on one vps is super clear, and dropping the public control room template is massive value. really smart call
@shannholmberg models still set the ceiling on what instructions can achieve optimizing the file helps but only within those base limits
@shannholmberg The control room is the part I’d copy first. Once agents become a daily workflow, the hard thing is not the prompt. It’s knowing what ran, what failed, and what needs a human decision.
@shannholmberg Smart move consolidating on one VPS, but most marketing agents fail at data quality, not orchestration. The real bottleneck is usually messy inputs, not missing another agent layer.
@shannholmberg nah, hermes agents got the setup right
@shannholmberg how did the creative versus structured model split play out once everything was running live on the VPS?
@shannholmberg four levels on one VPS feels like the real setup diff what size work do you actually trust that solo fleets system with?
@shannholmberg running specialized models on a single vps lowers infrastructure overhead
@shannholmberg Was just looking at Hermes today - thanks for the write up
@shannholmberg Control room changes everything. Finally scalable.

items.text: codex feels narrower but more dependable rn. less improvising, more follow-through. for real work that trade wins a lot.

target.label: moshhamedani
target.text: I haven’t used Codex yet but I see some been favoring that over Claude Code. Can you share some insight on why you think it’s better?

thread.text: I haven’t used Codex yet but I see some been favoring that over Claude Code. Can you share some insight on why you think it’s better?
@moshhamedani codex feels narrower but more dependable rn. less improvising, more follow-through. for real work that trade wins a lot.

@moshhamedani Both GPT-5.5 and Claude 4.7 are phenomenally good models. My experience is that they have different strengths and weaknesses. Aside from the already well-known point that Claude is obviously better at frontend work, I have found that the way you work with each model is also quite

@moshhamedani I don’t think it is simply “Codex is better than Claude Code.” The real difference is workflow shape. Claude Code feels stronger when: - you want interactive codebase exploration - you need long back-and-forth planning - you are iterating inside an existing project - you want

@moshhamedani Codex earned my trust on this: https://t.co/qxtLcYKrBo Claude Code kept dragging its feet trying to optimize the performance of a million-node graph. It kept going in circles, couldn’t really get anywhere, and even rolled things back several versions. Then Codex came in and

@moshhamedani I’ve been a long-time user of the Claude Max and Codex Plus plan. I’ve recently switched to the Codex Pro and Claude Pro plans. I can confidently say that the Codex has a far superior coding agent. The Codex app is also much more powerful in terms of connecting with other apps

@moshhamedani The perceived preference for Codex often stems from its integration capabilities within certain IDEs and its proficiency in handling complex, multi-file programming scenarios effectively.

@moshhamedani From daily use: Claude Code wins on complex multi-file reasoning and context retention. Codex tends to get praised for speed on simpler tasks. The real question is your workflow — if you're doing heavy agentic work with memory and tool use, Claude Code's architecture is hard to

@moshhamedani honestly, picking between codex and claude code is like choosing between two brands of coffee-depends on your taste and how much chaos you want in your morning. i use both, and neither has made my code magically bug-free yet :)

@moshhamedani Feels like Codex optimizes for execution speed and autonomy, while Claude Code optimizes for reasoning depth and code quality. One feels like an AI coworker rushing tasks forward, the other like a senior engineer thinking carefully before touching production

@moshhamedani I’ve noticed it’s more precise in its output. The code it produces tends to be simpler and follow your codebase conventions more. Claude really likes adding unneeded comments and produces “fancier” solutions. Plus, I have never once come close to hitting my token limits on Codex

@moshhamedani I am currently using both. Claude to plan and execute and Codex to review before and after implementation. Claude is still the main driver, but since Opus 4.7 I don’t trust and understand it so well. So I use Codex to review and it is absolutely worth it.
@moshhamedani Claude code was good in 2025 can’t even believe anyone still uses it over codex the difference is not even comparable, I don’t trust claude with ANYTHING important

@moshhamedani Everything related to design I find Claude Code much more relevant in these proposals (design + UX). Codex with GPT 5.5 is very efficient but a little stupid and mean on the design and it does not have too much taste. For backend code, code audit, security, bug resolution etc...

@moshhamedani Codex seems more matter of fact. Claude seems to pad things more. Since I have been a long time user or Claude I use it more for the heavy lifting and then I use codex for review / security reviews. In my mind it’s like having a second set of eyes on the code.

@moshhamedani I feel like its a good second opinion, but it seems more confident to go off the rails and start building the wrong thing instead of looking for clarification. I was adding features to a project yesterday and I had to backtrack a lot of bad assumptions on its part.

@moshhamedani I have not touched Claude in over a month. Codex sucks at frontend to start with, but if you use GPT Image 2 to generate designs and then Playwright and Codex in combination to build and tell it not to stop until its verified 100% that it matches its design image its cracked.

@moshhamedani Main reasons devs prefer Codex right now: → Runs tasks autonomously in cloud sandboxes — you delegate and check back → ~4x more token efficient per task → Stronger on terminal/DevOps tasks (77% vs Claude's 65% on Terminal-Bench) → ChatGPT Plus gets more sessions than Claude
@moshhamedani CC as a harness is underwhelming imo…. Claude models however still very good but recently gpts started to feel more consistent and reliable
@moshhamedani codex uses gpt-5.2 which handles standard tasks fine but claude opus 4.6 is usually better for premium reasoning and complex tasks
@moshhamedani Claude Code runs locally in your terminal (inheriting your machine's config), while Codex runs asynchronously in isolated cloud sandboxes

@moshhamedani Been using both daily for the last couple months. Codex just feels snappier — it spits out simpler, cleaner code that actually matches the style of my existing codebase instead of rewriting everything in its own voice. Claude is still better for big-picture planning and complex
@moshhamedani I wrote about this here
thread.quotedText: https://t.co/jy9SizIJh5

@moshhamedani From what I’ve seen: Claude Code = better reasoning & cleaner architecture Codex = faster execution, more practical, fewer interruptions So a lot of devs use: Claude for thinking 🤔 Codex for shipping 🚀
