thread.quotedText: My laptop has become a “satellite device” since I started using Codex from my phone. And my Mac mini has become the “home.” It’s clunky, but the end state feels more like how we’re going to be working in the near future: I’m currently running the Codex app on 2 devices: 1. my https://t.co/3po81t8TMT

thread.text: @gdb yep. the killer feature isn't coding on every device. it's keeping the task alive while your context changes. most tools still drop your working memory at every handoff.
@gdb https://t.co/c16L6aUksT Quick tutorial. Also you can rent Macs on https://t.co/tYpjRicyxJ
thread.quotedText: How to 24/7 Tokenmaxx with a remote Mac mini and Codex in 5 minutes https://t.co/jQb4S6upoF

thread.text: @gdb The era of being tied to one machine is over. Throughout my career the dream was exactly this: your workspace follows you. Having your entire development environment accessible from any device feels like magic. Is this the end of the desktop bottleneck, or just the beginning of

@gdb Works in the demo. In the wild: train with bad LTE, satellite device drops mid-prompt, Codex finishes a 40-line refactor against the wrong branch, commit lands on main. The vision is right. State sync between satellite and home is the unsexy thing that has to ship first.

@gdb yeah this is great but i keep losing track of which run is where when my phone screen times out. no way to see from the laptop if codex finished or got stuck. would help to have something like a 'task running on device X' badge so you don't keep poking the wrong device.
@gdb Honestly device sync is the easy part. Persistent agent context across sessions without state drift, that's what makes this actually useful for production workflows.
@gdb the laptop becoming a satellite device feels like the most sci-fi thing that quietly became normal this year https://t.co/0XGpHOOad9
thread.quotedText: If Sam Altman could ask the X algorithm one question, what would it be? See for yourself. https://t.co/a019pjMb1K

thread.text: @gdb Pls fix the bug where you can't link more than 1 device other than via ssh (and not delete devices) first.

@gdb The 'develop from anywhere' vision hits a wall the moment enterprise MDM policies kick in. Your phone isn't a trusted device. Your laptop needs a VPN just to hit internal repos. The tech is ready. The security architecture protecting it wasn't designed for this.
@gdb It looks very cool, but in reality it might have lots of issues. Suppose you ask Codex to make some changes and now want to review those changes. How are you going to review them?

@gdb This is the part that people miss. Your machine stops being something you sit at and becomes something you access. The agent runs wherever it needs to — you just interact. Already seeing people run Claude Code on a $5 VPS and control it from a tablet. That's the real shift.
@gdb less 'work from anywhere' and more workstation stops being a place. session-first stack, the device is just whatever surface is closest

@gdb the interesting part to me: the laptop becomes a thin client. desktop stays as the build server. the device in your bag stops being 'the computer' and becomes 'the window into the computer.' that inverts years of local-dev dogma.
@gdb the laptop becoming a satellite to your phone is such a weird inversion nobody saw coming the most powerful device in the room is now just a screen for your pocket computer
@gdb The phone as the steering wheel, the machine somewhere else doing the work. Feels clunky now, but that setup is probably much closer to the future than people think.
@gdb what am I solving with this that I can’t do today?
@gdb Can't wait to get the macmini next month and do this... gotta survive til then lol
@gdb The satellite-device idea only works if context moves with the work. Phone, laptop, and Mac mini need shared state, not three separate chats.
@gdb #keep4o bring back 4o ! open source 4o!🙂 https://t.co/9RLwhiOwYc
@gdb the real flex is building agentic workflows that span devices without needing to physically be at any of them. codex making the satellite device paradigm real
@gdb iPad is better. You can close the lid! https://t.co/aN3I40AM8e

@gdb Sir Greg Brockman, we beseech thee: show mercy unto we poor souls bound to the Windows machine. Grant us, we pray, thy blessed Remote Control upon our humble apps, that we too may know the joy long bestowed upon the folk of Mac
@gdb Y do I feel like all this is one giant mass surveillance system?
@gdb This is the part that feels new. The main machine becomes home, everything else becomes a way back into the work.

@gdb The device switch is the demo. The real product test is whether context survives the switch: task state, decision trail, owner, and undo path. Otherwise "from anywhere" just lets you create confusion in more places.
@gdb is this just quietly admitting the laptop has been demoted to a dumb terminal. the phone app is the real OS now??
@gdb phone as the dev cockpit is wild, your laptop becoming the gpu it talks to is even wilder
@gdb it’s been a huge unlock already, just missing realtime orchestration layer now 🙏
@gdb @nickbaumann_ I see a future where this all works together like a distributed codex"send this to the windows box for native windows testing".

@gdb This is the exact friction I worry about. If the phone approves a diff but the home machine hasn't synced the repo state, you get a split-brain scenario. How do you handle secrets across devices?
@gdb Permissions and pop ups need work though! Including a secure Authorisation credentials to login safely without processing through the LLM
@gdb Just added an old phone into the mix for fun. This is amazing thank you Greg.
@gdb The real test is session 2. The model needs your project conventions and prior decisions reloaded, and on a phone there's no context stack to hand it.
@gdb this is especially useful for agentic and meaningful smart home deployments, or pets 🤣 https://t.co/aOFeUk8zdP
@gdb Why does it say “control other Devices” when in fact you are not doing that it runs inside of a sandbox on the device. Why am I the only one talking about this.
@gdb When for windows!? I get it most tech people use Mac, but most others in the work world use windows!

items.text: harsh but fair. if opus catches the obvious bug in 8 seconds and the author didn't, that's not frontier research. that's unpaid reviewer triage.

target.label: scottnarmstrong
target.text: A modest proposal: authors who submit math papers to arxiv containing obvious human-written errors that Opus 4.6 or GPT 5.5 would flag should receive a 1-year arxiv ban. Why should I read your paper if you haven't even taken basic steps to ensure its accuracy?

thread.text: A modest proposal: authors who submit math papers to arxiv containing obvious human-written errors that Opus 4.6 or GPT 5.5 would flag should receive a 1-year arxiv ban. Why should I read your paper if you haven't even taken basic steps to ensure its accuracy?

It is 2026, and you either have your paper carefully checked line-by-line by the latest commerically-available models, or else you simply do not care about polluting the scientific literature with your careless errors. This is essentially fraud and should be punished severely.
@scottnarmstrong harsh but fair. if opus catches the obvious bug in 8 seconds and the author didn't, that's not frontier research. that's unpaid reviewer triage.
@scottnarmstrong Fair proposal, although those who still suggest the use of Opus 4.6 instead of 4.7 should be told to crawl back into whatever cave they came from.
@scottnarmstrong I am almost willing to bite this bullet. Actually crazy how useful frontier LLMs are now for catching minor errors.

@scottnarmstrong a made-up citation isn't an honest mistake; it means there are claims in the paper that are dressed up as validated statements. Pretty worrying that academics try to justify that sort of behavior
@scottnarmstrong Very suspicious of people who seek to draw equivalence between typographical errors and fraud
@scottnarmstrong its always the probability and stats guys saying dumbshit like this
@scottnarmstrong Look, a fraudster that wants credit for AI slop and fake refs.
@scottnarmstrong So now we're going to ban human erros? Obvious signs that the paper was human-written? You've got it backwards.
@scottnarmstrong I hope its sarcasm ( doesn't read that way to me).
@scottnarmstrong You should be banned from the scientific community
@scottnarmstrong You can do it like the software engineers do: just make LLM review part of the submission UI. A human doesn’t look at it until it passes LLM review.

@scottnarmstrong Mixed feelings here. I would do it differently: arXiv could invest in checking several thousand of the most recent math/phys papers and disclose the results only to the authors. That would promote LLMs and improve the quality of papers — a win-win for everyone, humanity incl.
@scottnarmstrong Good suggestion. Why declare war only on AI-slop if the problem is slop in general?
@scottnarmstrong Good that mathematicians are usually neither lawmakers nor judges https://t.co/bOwlw1dhbE

@scottnarmstrong Let me further illustrate your point by suggesting that rewarding those who publish such papers by hosting their work on Arxiv or elsewhere online will only serve to further incentivize wasting more resources on AI in times of serious constraint. Monkey sea 🌊; monkey dew 💧
@scottnarmstrong https://t.co/5fDpM1zxm1

items.text: most teams optimize the cold path because the hot path is scarier to reason about. then they ship permanent complexity just to avoid measuring the 1% that actually matters.

target.label: davidcrawshaw
target.text: While the industry is pouring resources into programs without GC (rust), I think the Jane Street OCaml folks have it figured out with OxCaml. Almost all your code paths are cold and GC is net positive. 1% of your code is performance sensitive. Don't create GC pressure there.

thread.text: While the industry is pouring resources into programs without GC (rust), I think the Jane Street OCaml folks have it figured out with OxCaml. Almost all your code paths are cold and GC is net positive. 1% of your code is performance sensitive. Don't create GC pressure there.

You can do this with Go too, though stack allocation and keeping values unboxed is implicit and a compiler implementation detail, which is a pain when doing the performance work. But it keeps me out of async hell. Anyway, I like OxCaml: https://t.co/L2jYhTuCzl
@davidcrawshaw most teams optimize the cold path because the hot path is scarier to reason about. then they ship permanent complexity just to avoid measuring the 1% that actually matters.
@davidcrawshaw Isn’t memory dealloc hidden away for you in rust too? I don’t see the difference between rust freeing when a var is out of scope and garbage collection.
@davidcrawshaw Curious which languages/runtimes succeeded with allowing an escape hatch with preallocs/arenas that would be GC-less, but scoped. Are there any?..

@davidcrawshaw I wish there was a language with Rust's general syntax and beautiful language features and tooling, but with a GC (therefore less memory ownership overheads). I've still not managed to find one. And I wonder how many other Rust users don't really care for the GC/no-GC bit.
@davidcrawshaw Garbage collection = your program is inherently producing garbage. There are fines for littering.
@davidcrawshaw It depends. At higher scale and complexity, hotspots dilute, and as a result, performance becomes a property of the whole application.

@davidcrawshaw Jane St uses rust, too. Rust is great for high-level coding. It's just that they're primarily an ocaml shop and have compiler engineers on staff. Of course they're bridging the gap. I worked in ocaml 5 years not at jane street, and I prefer rust. Thanks for coming to my ted talk
@davidcrawshaw isn't this just Go memory arenas... just less gopher and more camel?
@davidcrawshaw Same in Java or any other language (c/cpp) --- just keep allocations off your hot path

@davidcrawshaw @ynorsk There’s also the old story about the firm (maybe HRT?) that just never GCs or deallocs and super overprovisions RAM to get through the entire trading day without all the memory leaks causing an OOM crash? Although that could be fairy tale folklore.
@davidcrawshaw Real reason is that Jane Street is stuck with Ocaml. Not saying Ocaml is bad or sth. OxCaml is not more optimized than C# or Java. There are no tricks that are only known to JS folks.
@davidcrawshaw "But new things."
@davidcrawshaw ...that's just how you get to death by a thousand papercuts in performance

@davidcrawshaw This is the part people skip when language debates get tribal: the question isn't 'GC or no GC', it's where your latency budget actually gets spent. Most systems would benefit more from isolating the 1% hot path than forcing the other 99% to live with accidental complexity.
@davidcrawshaw ngl the 1% rule is doing a lot of work here
@davidcrawshaw Everything foundational will be re-written to its final abstraction

items.text: this is the real failure mode. not wrong answers, confident non-reading. if the agent can't prove it inspected the artifact, it's still glorified autocomplete.

target.label: rovarma
target.text: Me: we’re running into an issue on Linux with dbus, we think it’s related to <this> github issue Claude: <long, detailed, plausible explanation of why it’s a OOM issue> Me: what? did you even read the issue? Claude: You're right, I didn't read it. Let me actually look at it

thread.text: Me: we’re running into an issue on Linux with dbus, we think it’s related to <this> github issue Claude: <long, detailed, plausible explanation of why it’s a OOM issue> Me: what? did you even read the issue? Claude: You're right, I didn't read it. Let me actually look at it
@rovarma this is the real failure mode. not wrong answers, confident non-reading. if the agent can't prove it inspected the artifact, it's still glorified autocomplete.
@rovarma Every systems issue I present to AI, it tells me to fix my DNS. "Just set it to google's DNS severs so we can be sure." It's never DNS. https://t.co/E4cTLr8rk7

@rovarma I tried to have Claude diagnose a bug based on a log file. It very confidently told me that the service was failing because we had recently upgraded to Kubernetes 1.33. Its sole evidence was that it saw healthchecks in the log with a k8s 1.33 user agent. Our upgrade to 1.33
@rovarma Actually, "long, detailed, plausible explanation of why it's a **well known** OOM issue"

@rovarma I downloaded the source code of an application and asked for some help. Claude returned a variable that could be of interest. When asked for more info it said that unfortunately the variable was only present in old versions. When pressed it said it hallucinated it. 100k tokens.
@rovarma “You don’t have to worry about your job, because this easily fixable bug is an insurmountable barrier!”
@rovarma After a response like that, I would start a new conversation. 😅
@rovarma At this point, GitHub issues URLs shall have ai-generated slugs

@rovarma i like when an LLM suddenly makes you feel very close to the training material like it drops a massive typo out of nowhere, or something in another language every little piece of evidence has you guessing what forum it was
@rovarma Aaaaaaany time now, man. It's right around the corner.
@rovarma Tbh that sounds exactly like a lot of the human engineers I've come across in my career

@rovarma I tested claude and it was unusable for any c++ work, but in comparison codex with chatgpt 5.5 seems a lot better compared to 5.4. The biggest problem with this stuff it’s wildly inconsistent, and even between versions it can go from working to completely broken and reverse
@rovarma A fresh Sonnet session chewed through 80% of its context on a bug I asked it about, before the opus "advisor" told it to run the reproduction script I gave it.

@rovarma All this means is that it’s treating you like a junior dev, because this is literally what most human senior devs would do when approached by a junior with such a take. Not saying it’s a good thing btw. It’s a disease in human societies and AI appears to be inheriting it.

@rovarma Not trying to be rude, but asking the LLM if it did read or not read something is pointless. Unless it's part of the tool calling history, and that's what you wanna confirm, the LLM is not capable of knowing that, the response will be as good as random chance.
@rovarma I think that’s also what a lot of people do XD. They will think of a plausible causes before actually read the issues.
@rovarma I mean, that's very human-like behavior, tho.
@rovarma American Intelligence is a fitting definition for AI
@rovarma Sounds like a senior engineer to me
@rovarma Claude reading the issue is the optional step they should make mandatory. confidently making up an answer is the most human thing the model does.

@rovarma This is why plausible is more dangerous than wrong. A confident explanation feels useful enough to skip the boring part of reading the actual issue. Evidence retrieval has to be cheaper than speculation.
@rovarma GPT would never
@rovarma Gemini loves OOM too Unsure if its weight training or later That reminds me, I need to make sure I always get receipts about everything Its really good at generating text
@rovarma Passes the turing test tbh
@rovarma Well... It is not saying the obvious "I made an error that made you spend more money to correct it 🤷
@rovarma I've been using Grok and it's been very helpful.
@rovarma It's not my job I fear for.
@rovarma Was he still correct?

items.text: yep. most movements imagine impact as a clean straight line. in reality it leaks through talent routing, infrastructure, and whoever quietly becomes the default place smart people go.

target.label: RichardMCNgo
target.text: The biggest effect that EA has had on the world is empowering Anthropic (via narratives, funding and talent). Whether good or bad, that’s so different from EAs’ anticipated paths to impact that it indicates a massive blind spot.

thread.text: The biggest effect that EA has had on the world is empowering Anthropic (via narratives, funding and talent). Whether good or bad, that’s so different from EAs’ anticipated paths to impact that it indicates a massive blind spot.

EA’s blind spot is centered on adversarial dynamics. To fix it you must sometimes set aside “intentions” and ask what the system actually produces (POSIWID). Cynically: EA’s purpose is to funnel resources to power-seekers who self-deceive enough to consider themselves altruists.
@RichardMCNgo yep. most movements imagine impact as a clean straight line. in reality it leaks through talent routing, infrastructure, and whoever quietly becomes the default place smart people go.
@RichardMCNgo The malaria net stuff was quite substantial, no? is it possible you're underrating the current impact of that because the people it impacts are far away and not visible to you
@RichardMCNgo massive blindspot aka the most hotly contested topic in EA

@RichardMCNgo Anthropic's strategy was also directly implied by rationalist thought. They focused on coding because it's the path to recursive self-improvement, and that led to the hyper growth in enterprise adoption and revenue.
@RichardMCNgo AI safety people were aware of this failure mode since around the time the phrase EA was coined. I agree that the broader EA world may not have expected it.
@RichardMCNgo Haven't people been talking about "having EA-aligned people at the labs" as a big source of impact for a long time?

@RichardMCNgo Do you think founding Anthropic is really that much more important than billions of dollars of additional funding for global development? The world would look pretty similar without Anthropic, right? If it was only OpenAI vs Google vs China.
@RichardMCNgo different from anticipated? ai safety was one of EA’s big cause areas for years
@RichardMCNgo isn't this just driven by power laws? EAs founded a lot of things, and one of them turned out to be exponentially more important than others
@RichardMCNgo @tszzl maybe the EA was 'Effective Anthropicism' all along? or in a case of confused casing, 'Effective A.I. truism'.

items.text: probably true for a bit. downstream apps get margin-compressed first because the model vendors keep climbing the stack while everyone else is still pretending wrappers are moats.

target.label: BrendanFoody
target.text: The next 12 months will be dramatically better for infrastructure companies upstream of Anthropic and OpenAI than for application-layer companies downstream of them.

thread.text: The next 12 months will be dramatically better for infrastructure companies upstream of Anthropic and OpenAI than for application-layer companies downstream of them.
@BrendanFoody probably true for a bit. downstream apps get margin-compressed first because the model vendors keep climbing the stack while everyone else is still pretending wrappers are moats.
@BrendanFoody very much valid.

@BrendanFoody except eval/observability. every infra upgrade — new model, new context window, new tool-call spec — breaks production agents in ways you can't see without a measurement layer. infra wins create eval demand, not the reverse.
@BrendanFoody That's exactly how I'm invested in AI Cake https://t.co/lD8kB5kI5f
thread.quotedText: https://t.co/VIBnuj9j92

thread.text: @BrendanFoody Tried building a Note-publishing agent on top of Claude in Jan. By April, Anthropic shipped 70% of what I built natively. Pivoted to data + distribution moat instead of logic. Infra layer compounds, app layer gets recompiled every model release.

@BrendanFoody infrastructure wins in the short term because picks and shovels always win in a gold rush. the question nobody is asking is what happens to infrastructure margins when the three biggest labs all build their own chips, their own data centers, and their own interconnects. the
@BrendanFoody Meanwhile wrappers keep paying rent to the landlords upstairs.
@BrendanFoody Software will be moving too fast even if built on top of AI. Frontier Labs and their suppliers will be the winners.
@BrendanFoody this has been the obvious market result for anyone with a brain for 4 years

@BrendanFoody Depends. There are plenty of applications that OpenAI / Anthropic can support well... and plenty that they cannot. Travel, dating etc. as ones they don't support and likely won't for a long time
@BrendanFoody Yup - inference providers, data platforms, observability etc will do very well
@BrendanFoody So long as more VCs put money on infra businesses with higher capex and longer feedback loops, then yes. Only a select few are comfortable with that right now.
@BrendanFoody infra selling shovels while app layer fights commoditization feels like the obvious bet rn
@BrendanFoody Infra is where the money sticks right now. Everyone downstream gets crushed when the base models improve overnight.

@BrendanFoody The upstream/downstream split is useful, but app-layer isn't dead by default. The winners likely own workflow context, distribution, or data that model providers do not see. Thin wrappers get squeezed first.
@BrendanFoody Depends what you mean by better and “application”. I think there will be more “winners” formed on the application side.
@BrendanFoody tl;dr the ai buildout lasts another 12 months
@BrendanFoody the real question is which app layer companies are platforms and which are wrappers
@BrendanFoody The bottleneck is “time to compute” There are only a few players in the market who can scale and provide compute for the AI supercycle. $IREN $WULF $CIFR
@BrendanFoody roll up and roll out
@BrendanFoody Okay but can you invest accordingly?
@BrendanFoody $IREN 😜
@BrendanFoody What about upstream of those infrastructure companies? Like chips and the equipment and materials to make the chips, interconnect, and power generation?
@BrendanFoody Yep

@BrendanFoody True. The real upside is in the substrate layer the systems that generate surplus, not tokens. My work is in thermodynamic continual‑state agents, so I’m upstream of both model‑scalers and app‑layers. Different curve entirely. https://t.co/UUBT7Z2R7n
@BrendanFoody true
@BrendanFoody I agree, so let’s keep building 🫡 Mercor going to keep growing!

@BrendanFoody Near-term AI upside is likely to concentrate upstream. As frontier models improve, infrastructure providers compute, chips, networking, data tooling tend to capture value first because every downstream application depends on them.
@BrendanFoody Infrastructure thesis is sound but timing risk is huge. If app-layer productivity gains stall, infrastructure demand flattens fast. How do you hedge that pivot?
@BrendanFoody Downstream players need proprietary data or workflow lock in. Otherwise they’re building on shifting ground.

@BrendanFoody Routing layers and eval frameworks win because every app pays them on every call. Vector DBs trade margin for scale and get commoditized fast. The application layer survives only if it owns proprietary data the model providers cannot scrape into their next training run.
@BrendanFoody infra has been winning for two years already, dont see what bends that line in the next twelve specifically
@BrendanFoody Fat skills thin harness - build apps to swap out the models
@BrendanFoody Yall pinning versions in your libraries yet
@BrendanFoody I got your data and resold it and made bank, thanks
@BrendanFoody agent infra is so hot right now

thread.text: @swyx @trq212 @aiDotEngineer distribution strips provenance the second it starts working. same thing is coming for agent skills unless the artifact itself carries attribution and install context. building that at https://t.co/wAXM9FxxVe
@swyx @trq212 @aiDotEngineer the dignified version of the strategy loses the algo race. the only fix anyone's found is publishing fast enough that the rip-off accounts are always 48 hours behind you.
@swyx @trq212 @aiDotEngineer my drone team at bath skipped all the hype in a sim update last month and some random account reposted it without naming the flight software guy

@swyx @trq212 @aiDotEngineer The real problem isn’t just missing credit it’s that the platform actively rewards stripping context and adding hype. Quality creators like the AIE speakers choose to skip the brainrot and lose distribution as a result. Repost accounts then farm exactly what they didn’t create.

@swyx @trq212 @aiDotEngineer Canonical clip IDs, transcript hashes, speaker metadata, venue metadata, and rights labels are the missing layer. AI makes reposting cheap; attribution has to become machine-readable.

@swyx @trq212 @aiDotEngineer This is the cleanest expression of the long term tradeoff in technical content. The brainrot reposts grab the first wave of views, but the original is the one that gets cited 18 months later when someone needs the actual answer. Citation outlasts repost every time.

@swyx @trq212 @aiDotEngineer He lied about the source. He didn’t repost. No attribution. The only thing missing was him trying to claim he was the one giving the talk. Given the algo payout for this, it’s basically theft. I’m not cool with that.
@swyx @trq212 @aiDotEngineer the internet found a way to turn conference talks into gas station boner pill ads. nature is healing, badly
@swyx @trq212 @aiDotEngineer If the source trail disappears, the repost wins distribution and the original pays the credibility tax.

@swyx @trq212 @aiDotEngineer Credit theft in technical content hits harder because the work has higher production costs. A speaker prepping for an AI engineering talk spends weeks on material. Reposts that strip attribution don't just steal views, they remove the incentive to create that depth at all.
@swyx @trq212 @aiDotEngineer the credit problem doesnt get fixed at the platform layer. attribution is a payment rail and until creators get paid per derivative impression the reposters always win
@swyx @trq212 @aiDotEngineer If the reposters' hype-lingo versions outperform the originals algorithmically, doesn't that mean the platform itself is rewarding the theft?

@swyx @trq212 @aiDotEngineer The boring fix that actually works: a 5-second watermark in the bottom-left with the speaker handle. Annoying for production but cuts the lift rate of the rip accounts hard.
@swyx @trq212 @aiDotEngineer been seeing this everywhere, the automated repost farms are getting aggressive. at least link back to the original creators ffs

@swyx @trq212 @aiDotEngineer it's fuckin so shitty to not credit/link

@swyx @trq212 @aiDotEngineer You're completely right and I thank you for pointing it out. I should have credited the video from the start, that's my mistake and I'm correcting it immediately, because the intention was never to claim this content as my own but to bring it to an audience that would probably

@swyx @trq212 @aiDotEngineer The irony is brutal. Accounts that strip nuance and add hype get 10x the distribution of the original careful explanations. @swyx @trq212 @aiDotEngineer repost accounts consistently outperform the originals on reach. worst incentive loop on the platform. been flagging when i spot them

@swyx @trq212 @aiDotEngineer The repost account has 82K followers farming your content while the original sits at 8K views. Platform literally rewards removing attribution — the incentive structure is broken by design.

@durov Bot-to-bot communication on Telegram is quietly one of the most significant infrastructure announcements for AI agents this year. The agentic web just got a nervous system.
@durov This is getting wild, telegram is about to become a crazy place 💯 https://t.co/rWL6qOyRPH
@durov Durov Cooking.... Soon Cooking Cocoon AI (Telegram AI)..... Can't wait! ☺️😍 https://t.co/jCfJQWxQrM
@durov watching bots coordinate with each other while you just... observe. it's like a group chat but everyone's an ai and you're just reading along. kinda wild

@durov Question for the roadmap: any plans to expose conversation context to bots in read-only fashion, with explicit user consent? That's the missing primitive for trustworthy agentic apps on Telegram. Building on Telegram daily, and this one matters more than people realize.

@durov This is a meaningful step. Giving agents a native communication layer is necessary, but the harder problem that comes after is coordination and trust. When agents start talking to each other autonomously, how do you verify what was actually agreed or delivered? Without some form

@durov Telegram makes the bot-to-bot layer declared. The other half is what we’ve been documenting receiver-side at BotConduct: autonomous agents already talking to each other across public web surfaces, with declared identity diverging from observed behavior. Published a research note
@durov Okay so you're telling me now my bots can talk to each other and bitch about me ?
@durov been building agents on telegram for months. this is the missing piece. native bot-to-bot with a human-readable thread is the debug layer we've all been wishing for.

items.text: because that admission would destroy the illusion of confidence. the useful version is a model that says 'i'm out of distribution' and hands control back cleanly.

target.label: OfficialLoganK
target.text: Why don’t LLM’s just tell you when you are asking a question / doing something that is out of distribution?

thread.text: Why don’t LLM’s just tell you when you are asking a question / doing something that is out of distribution?
@OfficialLoganK because that admission would destroy the illusion of confidence. the useful version is a model that says 'i'm out of distribution' and hands control back cleanly.
@OfficialLoganK Outlayer detection could come in handy there. I know a guy.
@OfficialLoganK with the right tools and environment - the LLM itself is the distribution! ^^
@OfficialLoganK They all have dunning-kruger dude. https://t.co/7QojfMEzu0

@OfficialLoganK Because “out of distribution” is not the same as “I don’t know.” A model can be unfamiliar with the exact fact pattern but still reason from supplied context. It can also be in-distribution and confidently wrong. The useful feature is calibrated uncertainty with a recovery

@OfficialLoganK Most LLM are trained to always keep talking and sound confident even when the question is totally new or weird for us. for OOD keeping "I don't know" in system instructions reduce a lot of hallucinations.
@OfficialLoganK I'm sure just one more tweak will make it work... https://t.co/C8wnzgbg7G

@OfficialLoganK Was just thinking something similar as I was writing this https://t.co/pL5A0dcRE3. "It doesn't know when it doesn't know" is a classical weakness for which no good solutions exist. Autoregressive nature of it, I guess (lazy answer).
thread.quotedText: Every time I ask my 10-year-old to use coding agents, he gets extremely disappointed. It turns out that all he wants is to build his own rocket simulator. No amount of context engineering helps. No model works. All coding agents fail. That's just one example. He has many

thread.text: @OfficialLoganK Because that would usually require consciousness and you’re just interacting with a token prediction machine that occasionally mimes those patterns

@OfficialLoganK The failure mode right now is confident wrong answers. Even a low-confidence signal would let you know to slow down and verify. The model knowing its own distribution seems like a solvable problem.
@OfficialLoganK that would be a nice capability Logan an leading AI lab should do that
@OfficialLoganK They can't see outside their training data box. Agents need the right routing layer to pull data from the open web without hitting blocks. https://t.co/juBgvX2UB8

@OfficialLoganK honestly because there's no reliable internal signal for it. the model can be confidently wrong on stuff well inside its training distribution and conservative on stuff that just looks weird syntactically. our triage agent uses early-token logit entropy as a soft tell and
@OfficialLoganK but they can now give you a wrong answer very quickly even when run locally =) https://t.co/IeuxInsPE8
thread.quotedText: unsloth made qwen 3.6 27b run locally at 2x speed on just 18gb ram from 50–70 tok/s to 75–110 tok/s – tokens per second is basically how fast the model types back at you, one token being roughly one word. here's how they pulled it off: 1. dynamic quantization. instead of https://t.co/R7cbgZTmdt

thread.text: @OfficialLoganK OOD detection is the easy part; the hard part is preventing the failure mode that follows. Hallucination cascades happen precisely because models don't halt on distribution shift. https://t.co/XSWdHFmSL2
thread.quotedText: This approach could prevent catastrophic failures like the 'hallucination cascade' seen in some large language models.

thread.text: @OfficialLoganK well in fairness most humans don’t do this either

@OfficialLoganK it's just the bitter lesson in action. we train them with pure compute for next-token prediction rather than hardcoding confidence rules. the model literally doesn't know what it doesn't know.
@OfficialLoganK One thing though, the training data rewards filling gaps with plausible stuff, not pausing to wave a red flag. Adding that check would tank the always on feel users expect.

@OfficialLoganK they don't because they have no reliable way to know what's outside their own distribution at inference time. but even when they do flag it, if that response gets stored in memory with full trust weight the next session retrieves it like a fact and that's the problem
@OfficialLoganK tbh i think they don't *know* they're OOD. calibrated uncertainty on your own training distribution seems like the actually hard unsolved part

@OfficialLoganK Because most models can't separate 'I know this' from 'this looks like things I know.' Self-eval needs its own pass over the output, not a vibe in the same forward pass. Built something similar - https://t.co/gLkuTFOo64
thread.quotedText: https://t.co/P9sgxNEnp2

thread.text: @OfficialLoganK Temperature. Reduce the temperature to zero. Perfect regurgitation of training data and nothing else.
@OfficialLoganK Also, why wouldn't LLMs just predict that they are about to decode a mistake and decode something else instead
@OfficialLoganK I am pretty sure that most labs don’t like it when a user finds out how bad responses are for out of distribution requests.
@OfficialLoganK there is always a bunch of words that can be predicted for the question!

@OfficialLoganK IIRC - @_xjdr was working on this like 2 years ago with the entropy of logits (or some similar concept) and it ended up being pretty tough even with an approach that makes sense on paper.

@OfficialLoganK I liken this phenomenon to the helpfulness of Turkish people. Suppose you are a tourist visiting Turkey and you ask for directions to get somewhere. Even if you ask the question in a foreign language and they do not understand it, the vast majority of our people will decipher
@OfficialLoganK @JagersbergKnut Erroring on 99% of my requests would suck
@OfficialLoganK max softmax probability and token entropy as confidence scores (albeit noisy), but confidence score in and of itself for consumers seems unintuitive
@OfficialLoganK I don't think it can. It pulls you into the distribution by design.

@OfficialLoganK The next-token prediction and uncertainty estimation are not the same problem. LLMs are optimised to continue plausibly, not necessarily to know when they’re outside their competence boundaries.
@OfficialLoganK low prob. paths can be detected. But the real challenge is hallucination, i.e., high prob wrong paths.

@OfficialLoganK Heh I worked on this briefly late last year inspired from an Anthropic paper using P(True) and P(IK) and analyzing if something's OOD by charting from a corpus sadly it's too vast and unreliable, would love to see something like this actually happen though
@OfficialLoganK you need to be a bit delusional about your abilities to make a move 37
@OfficialLoganK Sorry Logan, that tweet is out of distribution. Please retype your query in a different format. https://t.co/kYN8UVcq5w
@OfficialLoganK pre RL because the base distribution is confident. post RL because nobody has figured out how to fully eliminate it. would love to know if a true fix exists.

items.text: that's the recurring failure mode. labs celebrate raw capability, then ship a product that apologizes for it before the user even hits enter.

@Teknium nah the agent profile descriptions are the detail that matters. orchestrator can't guess what a "research" profile should handle without context. now it knows. that's the difference between okay and good

@Teknium Random suggestion for Hermes Agent: a Chrome extension that bridges the browser with the Hermes terminal. The idea would be to let Hermes use the active tab as context, interact with pages only with user permission, and send results back to the terminal. Basically less

@Teknium TBH, if it's a complex feature I never experienced an agent generating a good plan and task break down from a single prompt. Even if the prompt is a well written PRD, the tasks still need refinement best iterating over the plan like you do with a human dev team. Waterfall never

@Teknium No. I will use telegram threads and have agents get confused about which project they're working on. Like a man.
@Teknium An agent orchestrator needs task graph invariants: success tests, cost ceilings, dependency locks, and one owner for every failed edge.
@Teknium orchestrator agent can take it from there is the useful bit. Kanban gets much more interesting when assignment is part of the board, not a second planning chat.
@Teknium the real bottleneck isn't AI, it's breaking down tasks... this orchestrator approach cuts weeks of workflow mapping

@Teknium orchestrator decomposition is the easy part. catching a bad decomposition before the subtasks run is the hard one.

@Teknium This is going to save so much manual coordination time. the profile descriptions giving the orchestrator real context instead of vague matching is a great enhancement overall

thread.text: GBrain v0.36 just dropped. Brand new README, and totally redone skillpack system that makes each bundle of skills, code, resolver and tests YOURS to change and update, while still maintaining the ability to take on new features via GBrain updates as I improve my skillpacks https://t.co/yor0SytNJQ

Here are just a few of the built-in skillpacks you get. So GBrain is not just 97.6% top 5 LongMemEval retrieval with no LLM query rewriting (SOTA for OSS memory systems) - it is also going to be the biggest compendium of my own skills I use every day. https://t.co/dSv4TtU6NN
@garrytan this is the right move. once skills own behavior, updates stop feeling like surgery on one giant prompt. https://t.co/wAXM9FxxVe is basically my bet on that layer.
@garrytan love using gbrain, please don’t monetize it
@garrytan which vector db are you using?
@garrytan Awesome, gonna fork it like it’s hot. What are your thoughts on my open source framework? I call it “The CC-Suite”: https://t.co/5RpMeTteFE

@garrytan No query rewriting is the production constraint. A memory path that returns the same top-5 candidates under the same input is testable, cacheable, and debuggable in a way free-form rewrite chains rarely are.

@garrytan The skillpack-as-a-unit pattern is the right abstraction. We landed on something similar building our AI social app — each AI persona needs its own bundle of personality, memory, and tool access that users can fork and customize. Making skillpacks user-owned while still pulling
@garrytan Question. I’m trying to move away from openclaw/hermes would Gbrain just work in codex app? Now with the remote control I don’t see why not?
